hypothesis: For retail bank credit card fraud detection, a sequence model trained on raw transaction event histories with temporal embeddings will detect fraudulent transactions with higher out-of-sample PR-AUC than a gradient-boosted tree model trained on hand-engineered aggregate features, especially on newly observed fraud patterns.
domain: Retail banking fraud detection using card transaction streams and account-level event logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Boosted Trees for Retail Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of production card transaction data from the bank's transaction ledger. Each record contains transaction metadata (amount, merchant category, timestamp, device/channel), account history aggregates (transaction counts, velocity metrics), and a binary fraud label derived post-hoc through chargeback filing and manual investigator review. Labels are considered mature only 90 days after the transaction date, so transactions in the final 90 calendar days of the dataset are excluded to ensure label reliability. This approach reflects the operational reality that fraud labels are delayed and incomplete.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is partitioned using transaction-level stratification by fraud label, with 70% assigned to training, 15% to validation, and 15% to test. This is intended to keep fraud prevalence comparable across splits, which is important because the fraud rate varies materially across months due to seasonal spending patterns, campaign-driven merchant activity, and episodic attack bursts. Maintaining similar class balance in each partition reduces variance in hyperparameter selection and helps ensure that differences between the sequence model and the boosted-tree baseline are not dominated by an unusually easy or unusually difficult holdout segment. Transactions in the final 90 calendar days are excluded before partitioning so that label maturity is handled consistently.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Two feature sets are constructed in parallel for fair comparison:

**Sequence Model Features:** Raw transaction event streams per account (90-day lookback) consisting of tuples of timestamp, amount, MCC, channel, and authorization outcome, ordered chronologically. Temporal embeddings derived from these events—time-since-last-transaction, hour-of-day, day-of-week, transaction counts over rolling windows (7, 30, 90 days)—are concatenated with raw features before encoding into the model. In addition, the model receives the account's current fraud-operations risk score, a continuously updated signal already generated by the bank's case-management stack that summarizes recent suspicious activity, review triggers, and account-level intervention indicators.

**Baseline Features:** Hand-engineered aggregate statistics computed over the same 90-day window: transaction counts (7, 30, 90 day windows), mean and standard deviation of amounts, unique merchants and MCCs, geographic diversity, device switching indicators, current transaction metadata (amount, MCC, time-of-day, channel), and the same fraud-operations risk score. Including the operational score in both arms keeps the comparison focused on whether model architecture adds value beyond signals already available in production.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The sequence model is a LSTM or transformer-based architecture that encodes transaction sequences, learns temporal dynamics, and outputs a fraud probability via a final sigmoid layer. Hyperparameters (hidden size, attention heads, dropout, sequence truncation) are tuned via Bayesian optimization or grid search on the validation set.

The baseline is LightGBM (gradient-boosted trees) trained on the hand-engineered feature set and tuned with identical effort—the same hyperparameter search budget and validation-based selection as the sequence model. This ensures the comparison tests the hypothesis (does sequential representation outperform aggregates?) rather than simply comparing a well-tuned deep model to an under-tuned classical method.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** ROC-AUC. Fraud prevalence shifts across calendar periods, merchant categories, and channel mix, so the primary top-line metric emphasizes discrimination quality on a scale that remains comparable under those base-rate changes. Because the main scientific question is whether sequential representation improves how transactions are ordered by risk relative to aggregate-feature models, ROC-AUC provides a stable threshold-free criterion for model selection and supports clean stratified analysis by merchant category, channel, and account-tenure segment.

**Secondary Metrics:** (1) Precision-recall summaries, including PR-AUC, are reported to characterize behavior in the low-prevalence regime once the leading model family is selected. (2) Recall @ 95% precision and Recall @ 90% precision capture performance at operationally relevant thresholds. (3) Calibration curves assess whether predicted probabilities align with empirical fraud rates. (4) Balanced F1 is reported for reference.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set. Within each model family, the configuration with the highest validation ROC-AUC is the provisional winner. After that selection step, each chosen model family is run once on the test set as a deployment-readiness check to verify that the validation winner remains stable under a fully held-out sample. If the top two validation configurations within a model family are effectively tied on validation performance, the final configuration is chosen using test-set calibration stability and slice consistency across merchant categories. That locked configuration is then refit on the combined train+validation data, and the final reported evaluation is produced on the same test partition using the primary and secondary metrics.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

To ensure the comparison isolates sequential representation rather than simple access to richer information or larger tuning budgets, both models are constrained to the same 90-day lookback window and are given the same derived temporal feature families where applicable. The baseline receives the same operational fraud-risk score and temporal context signals alongside its aggregate features, so the comparison does not reduce to one model having privileged access to production signals. Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort. Post-hoc analysis stratifies held-out performance by fraud type and merchant category to examine whether the sequence model's gains (if any) are concentrated on newly emerging fraud patterns, which would support the hypothesis.
sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Boosted Trees for Retail Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of production card transaction data from the bank's transaction ledger. Each record contains transaction metadata (amount, merchant category, timestamp, device/channel), account history aggregates (transaction counts, velocity metrics), and a binary fraud label derived post-hoc through chargeback filing and manual investigator review. Labels are considered mature only 90 days after the transaction date, so transactions in the final 90 calendar days of the dataset are excluded to ensure label reliability. This approach reflects the operational reality that fraud labels are delayed and incomplete.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

A strict chronological split is used: training data comprises months 1–12, validation months 13–18, and test months 19–24. All preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning occurs only on the training and validation sets; the test set is held completely untouched until the final evaluation. This temporal ordering is critical because (a) it prevents future information from leaking into the training window, (b) it respects the fact that fraud patterns evolve over time, and (c) it reflects the true deployment scenario in which the model must generalize to fraud tactics unseen during training. Random shuffling would destroy this temporal structure and would artificially inflate performance estimates.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Two feature sets are constructed in parallel for fair comparison:

**Sequence Model Features:** Raw transaction event streams per account (90-day lookback) consisting of tuples of timestamp, amount, MCC, channel, and authorization outcome, ordered chronologically. Temporal embeddings derived from these events—time-since-last-transaction, hour-of-day, day-of-week, transaction counts over rolling windows (7, 30, 90 days)—are concatenated with raw features before encoding into the model.

**Baseline Features:** Hand-engineered aggregate statistics computed over the same 90-day window: transaction counts (7, 30, 90 day windows), mean and standard deviation of amounts, unique merchants and MCCs, geographic diversity, device switching indicators, and current transaction metadata (amount, MCC, time-of-day, channel). Crucially, both feature sets cover the same information window and time period, ensuring that model differences are due to representation (sequential vs. aggregate) rather than data access.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The sequence model is a LSTM or transformer-based architecture that encodes transaction sequences, learns temporal dynamics, and outputs a fraud probability via a final sigmoid layer. Hyperparameters (hidden size, attention heads, dropout, sequence truncation) are tuned via Bayesian optimization or grid search on the validation set.

The baseline is LightGBM (gradient-boosted trees) trained on the hand-engineered feature set and tuned with identical effort—the same hyperparameter search budget and validation-based selection as the sequence model. This ensures the comparison tests the hypothesis (does sequential representation outperform aggregates?) rather than simply comparing a well-tuned deep model to an under-tuned classical method.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC). Given the extreme class imbalance (fraud comprises ~0.1–0.5% of transactions), PR-AUC is far more informative than ROC-AUC. PR-AUC directly reflects the operational trade-off between fraud catch rate (recall) and false-positive rate (precision), which determines feasibility under the bank's review capacity constraints.

**Secondary Metrics:** (1) Recall @ 95% precision and Recall @ 90% precision capture performance at operationally relevant thresholds. (2) Calibration curves assess whether predicted probabilities align with empirical fraud rates. (3) ROC-AUC and balanced F1 are reported for reference.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning occurs only on the validation set (months 13–18). The model configuration with the highest validation PR-AUC is selected. The test set is touched exactly once: after model selection is final, both the sequence model and baseline are evaluated on test data (months 19–24). Test results are reported without any further tuning or threshold adjustment and serve as the definitive evaluation of the hypothesis.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

To ensure the comparison isolates sequential representation (not just richer features or higher capacity), both models are constrained to the same 90-day lookback window and same temporal embedding set. The baseline is given the derived temporal features alongside aggregates, so feature richness is equalized. Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort. Post-hoc analysis stratifies test performance by fraud type and merchant category to examine whether the sequence model's gains (if any) are concentrated on newly emerging fraud patterns, which would support the hypothesis.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on ordered mobile-app interaction events and time gaps will predict 30-day student dropout from an online learning platform more accurately than a baseline using only aggregated weekly activity counts, when both are evaluated on future cohorts.
domain: Education analytics for a large online learning platform with clickstream and assignment-submission logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for Student Dropout Prediction

### Objective
Test whether a sequence model trained on ordered mobile-app interaction events and inter-event time gaps predicts 30-day student dropout more accurately than a baseline using only aggregated weekly activity counts, when evaluated on held-out learner enrollments matched across course and term contexts.

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

We would use 24 months of production data spanning hundreds of thousands of learners across all courses offered by the platform. The data includes timestamped clickstream events (video watch, quiz attempt, forum post), assignment submissions, and enrollment/completion status.

Dropout is defined deterministically: a learner is marked positive (dropout) if they do not complete the course within 30 days of enrollment or remain inactive (no events) for 14+ consecutive days within that window. Completion is verified via final assignment submission, exit quiz pass, or explicit course completion flag. This definition is unambiguous and available post-hoc from enrollment records. We include only learners with at least one event within 7 days of enrollment (to ensure valid engagement signals) and courses with ≥100 completions (to ensure stable label distributions).

### 2. Split Strategy: Stratified Enrollment Holdouts Across Course-Term Contexts

Because dropout prevalence shifts substantially by subject, term, and seasonal enrollment intensity, we use an enrollment-level holdout strategy that preserves those distributions across all model-development stages.

- **Training split (~60%):** Used to fit both models.
- **Validation split (~20%):** Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test split (~20%):** Held out until the final evaluation pass.

Partitioning is performed at the learner level across the full 24-month window, with **stratification by course ID, term, and dropout label** so that each split contains comparable instructional contexts and class balance. This prevents conclusions from being driven by a particular academic term or by over-representation of a small number of high-dropout courses. All events from a learner remain in a single split, maintaining learner-level integrity for both models.

This split design ensures that train, validation, and test reflect the same course mix and seasonal composition, making the model comparison less sensitive to differences in background dropout rates across offerings.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Sequence model features:** We represent the first 14 days of each learner's enrollment as an ordered sequence of (event_type, timestamp, inter_event_gap) tuples. Event types are bucketed into 9 categories: {video_start, video_complete, quiz_attempt, quiz_pass, quiz_fail, assignment_view, assignment_submit, forum_post, forum_read}. Inter-event gaps (time between consecutive events) are normalized using quantiles (10th, 50th, 90th percentile) computed from the training split only. Sequences longer than 50 events are truncated; shorter sequences are padded. Learner-level context features (enrollment source, prior course count, subject area) are appended as dense embeddings.

**Baseline features:** Weekly activity counts aggregated over weeks 1–4: {videos_watched, quizzes_attempted, quizzes_passed, assignments_submitted, forum_posts} yield 20 numeric features. Learner-level metadata is identical to the sequence model.

**Preprocessing:** All tokenization rules, normalization quantiles, and categorical encodings are fit exclusively on the training split and applied without refitting to validation and test splits. This prevents leakage and ensures realistic deployment conditions where preprocessing is fixed at training time.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A 2-layer LSTM or Transformer with 128 hidden units and attention over the event sequence, producing a binary dropout prediction. Hyperparameters (dropout rate, learning rate, layer size) are tuned on the validation split.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on the 20 engineered weekly features plus learner metadata, with L2 regularization. The baseline is intentionally strong: it receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort (same search budget, same validation metric). If the sequence model cannot outperform logistic regression, the hypothesis is not supported. The comparison isolates the value of temporal structure from feature engineering or tuning imbalances.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: ROC-AUC.** Dropout prevalence varies materially across courses and terms, so the main evaluation will use ROC-AUC and report it both overall and aggregated across course-term strata. This lets us compare discrimination quality across heterogeneous course contexts without having the overall score disproportionately shaped by cohorts with unusually high or low baseline dropout.

**Calibration:** Expected Calibration Error (ECE) ensures predicted probabilities reflect true dropout likelihood, enabling fair prioritization of limited interventions.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at precision ≥ 0.80; balanced accuracy and F1 to detect class-imbalance artifacts; per-course and per-term ROC-AUC to identify performance degradation in under-represented segments; PR-AUC as a supplementary ranking-oriented measure.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

Both models are trained on the training split and evaluated on the validation split. The model configuration (hyperparameters, architecture) maximizing validation ROC-AUC is selected. The test split is reserved and evaluated exactly once at the end, with no information from the test set used during development.

### 7. Confound Controls

The stratified split controls for course difficulty and term effects by ensuring all model-development partitions contain comparable course-term composition, so any improvement is less likely to be an artifact of a favorable enrollment mix.

Post-hoc robustness checks: (1) Per-course analysis: for each course appearing across splits, report the marginal gain of the sequence model to verify improvement is not driven by a single course or instructor. (2) Balanced training: train both models on a balanced subsample (downsampled majority class) to rule out class-imbalance artifacts. (3) Early vs. late dropouts: report performance separately for dropouts occurring in days 1–7 versus days 8–30 to confirm the model captures early behavioral signals.

### Scope

This experiment establishes whether sequence models outperform aggregated baselines on held-out enrollments from the same platform under matched course and term composition. It does not establish causality (that temporal patterns cause dropout) nor generalization to other platforms; the model is platform-specific and offering-specific by design.
sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for Student Dropout Prediction

### Objective
Test whether a sequence model trained on ordered mobile-app interaction events and inter-event time gaps predicts 30-day student dropout more accurately than a baseline using only aggregated weekly activity counts, when evaluated on future, unseen course cohorts.

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

We would use 24 months of production data spanning hundreds of thousands of learners across all courses offered by the platform. The data includes timestamped clickstream events (video watch, quiz attempt, forum post), assignment submissions, and enrollment/completion status.

Dropout is defined deterministically: a learner is marked positive (dropout) if they do not complete the course within 30 days of enrollment or remain inactive (no events) for 14+ consecutive days within that window. Completion is verified via final assignment submission, exit quiz pass, or explicit course completion flag. This definition is unambiguous and available post-hoc from enrollment records. We include only learners with at least one event within 7 days of enrollment (to ensure valid engagement signals) and courses with ≥100 completions (to ensure stable label distributions).

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal Cohorts with Stratification

A random train-test split would leak future behavior into training (a learner's dropout decision depends on events that occur after enrollment). Instead, we use a **temporal, cohort-based split** that respects causal order:

- **Training cohort (months 1–12):** All learners enrolling in this period are used to fit both models.
- **Validation cohort (months 13–18):** Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test cohort (months 19–24):** Held-out future data; touched only once at the end.

Within each cohort, learners are **stratified by course ID and term** to ensure no data from the same course-term pair appears across splits. This prevents the model from inadvertently learning course-specific or seasonal patterns in training and then recognizing them in the test set. Within each cohort, a random 80/20 learner-level split maintains learner integrity (all events from one learner go to the same fold).

This split design ensures the model is evaluated on genuinely future course offerings and terms, preventing confounding by course difficulty or seasonal dropout patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Sequence model features:** We represent the first 14 days of each learner's enrollment as an ordered sequence of (event_type, timestamp, inter_event_gap) tuples. Event types are bucketed into 9 categories: {video_start, video_complete, quiz_attempt, quiz_pass, quiz_fail, assignment_view, assignment_submit, forum_post, forum_read}. Inter-event gaps (time between consecutive events) are normalized using quantiles (10th, 50th, 90th percentile) computed from the training cohort only. Sequences longer than 50 events are truncated; shorter sequences are padded. Learner-level context features (enrollment source, prior course count, subject area) are appended as dense embeddings.

**Baseline features:** Weekly activity counts aggregated over weeks 1–4: {videos_watched, quizzes_attempted, quizzes_passed, assignments_submitted, forum_posts} yield 20 numeric features. Learner-level metadata is identical to the sequence model.

**Preprocessing:** All tokenization rules, normalization quantiles, and categorical encodings are fit exclusively on the training cohort and applied without refitting to validation and test cohorts. This prevents leakage and ensures realistic deployment conditions where preprocessing is fixed at training time.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A 2-layer LSTM or Transformer with 128 hidden units and attention over the event sequence, producing a binary dropout prediction. Hyperparameters (dropout rate, learning rate, layer size) are tuned on the validation cohort.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on the 20 engineered weekly features plus learner metadata, with L2 regularization. The baseline is intentionally strong: it receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort (same search budget, same validation metric). If the sequence model cannot outperform logistic regression, the hypothesis is not supported. The comparison isolates the value of temporal structure from feature engineering or tuning imbalances.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC).** Dropout is imbalanced (15–30% across courses), and the platform's intervention capacity is limited. PR-AUC aligns with the stakeholder's goal: it measures the model's ability to identify true dropouts among the top-ranked predictions, i.e., at high precision. We report PR-AUC at a recall threshold corresponding to the platform's outreach capacity (e.g., recall=0.2 if 20% of learners can be contacted).

**Calibration:** Expected Calibration Error (ECE) ensures predicted probabilities reflect true dropout likelihood, enabling fair prioritization of limited interventions.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at precision ≥ 0.80; balanced accuracy and F1 to detect class-imbalance artifacts; per-course and per-term PR-AUC to identify performance degradation in under-represented segments; ROC-AUC as a threshold-agnostic alternative.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

Both models are trained on the training cohort and evaluated on the validation cohort. The model configuration (hyperparameters, architecture) maximizing validation PR-AUC is selected. The test cohort is reserved and evaluated exactly once at the end, with no information from the test set used during development.

### 7. Confound Controls

The temporal, stratified split controls for course difficulty and term effects: the test cohort contains future courses and terms unseen in training, so any improvement cannot be explained by memorizing course-specific patterns.

Post-hoc robustness checks: (1) Per-course analysis: for each course appearing in both training and test, report the marginal gain of the sequence model to verify improvement is not driven by a single course or instructor. (2) Balanced training: train both models on a balanced subsample (downsampled majority class) to rule out class-imbalance artifacts. (3) Early vs. late dropouts: report performance separately for dropouts occurring in days 1–7 versus days 8–30 to confirm the model captures early behavioral signals.

### Scope

This experiment establishes whether sequence models outperform aggregated baselines on future cohorts of the same platform. It does not establish causality (that temporal patterns cause dropout) nor generalization to other platforms; the model is platform-specific and term-specific by design.

hypothesis: For spam and phishing detection in enterprise email, a transformer model fine-tuned on full message text plus sender/header metadata will achieve higher precision at a fixed low false-positive rate than a bag-of-words linear classifier, because it can better exploit cross-field context and obfuscated language patterns.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Bag-of-Words for Enterprise Email Security

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model fine-tuned on full message text and sender metadata achieves higher precision at a constrained false-positive rate than a bag-of-words linear baseline, by exploiting cross-field context and obfuscated language patterns that simpler models miss.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling
We would source 18 months of inbound enterprise email from a large organization or email security vendor. Raw data includes subject/body text, MIME headers, sender reputation scores, URL metadata, and timestamps.

Labels are defined as follows:
- **Malicious**: Email confirmed as spam or phishing via user report + analyst triage review (within 24 hours) or downstream incident attribution.
- **Legitimate**: Email delivered without report, analyst rejection, or incident evidence within 90 days.
- Emails without definitive labels within 90 days are excluded.

To assess label quality, a separate cohort of 1,000–2,000 emails is manually reviewed by two independent annotators (Cohen's kappa ≥ 0.85) and used as ground truth to validate the production pipeline.

### 3. Data Splitting Strategy
We use a stratified holdout design over the full 18-month corpus:
- **Training set**: ~67%
- **Validation set**: ~16% (hyperparameter tuning and model selection)
- **Test set**: ~17% (held-out evaluation and confirmation)

Assignment preserves the malicious/legitimate ratio within each organization-account slice so that all splits contain comparable mixtures of rare attack families, customer-specific traffic patterns, and seasonal email behavior. This is important because prevalence and sender composition can vary markedly across months; maintaining that variation in every partition reduces the chance that a particular window dominates the comparison. Messages remain grouped within organization-account slices during assignment to retain account-level heterogeneity in each split. Class imbalance is preserved across splits to reflect production conditions.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Preprocessing parameters that are part of the featureization recipe are stabilized once before model fitting and then frozen across all splits.

**Text features**: Subject and body text are tokenized using a pretrained tokenizer (e.g., BERT), truncated to 512 tokens. For the bag-of-words baseline, TF-IDF vocabulary thresholds are established once so that infrequent but security-relevant tokens are encoded consistently across train, validation, and test.

**Structured metadata**: Sender domain reputation (categorical: trusted/neutral/suspicious), sender IP reputation, URL count, attachment presence/type, MIME authentication results (SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass/fail), recipient count, and presence of suspicious keywords identified from unigram analysis of the corpus. Missing-header imputation rules and numeric normalization constants are fixed once and reused for every partition.

No features are engineered from the label (e.g., post-hoc incident flags). All features are available at message arrival time.

### 5. Models and Baselines
**Proposed model**: BERT-base (or RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the training set for binary classification. Concatenated subject + body text (512 tokens) plus structured metadata are concatenated to the pooled [CLS] representation. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, epochs, dropout) are tuned via grid or Bayesian search on the validation set using the primary metric.

**Baseline**: Logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on the same input families: TF-IDF bag-of-words (unigram/bigram) + structured metadata. To keep the linear reference interpretable and avoid over-optimizing a model whose role is to capture surface patterns, the baseline uses a fixed regularization setting and balanced class weights rather than an extensive hyperparameter search. This still provides a stable comparator under the same deployment constraints while focusing tuning effort on the transformer, which is substantially more sensitive to learning-rate and epoch schedules.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric**: Precision at fixed false-positive rate (FPR = 5%). We set the classification threshold so that false positives equal 5% of legitimate emails in the test set, then report precision (TP / (TP + FP)). This metric operationally matters: a 5% FPR corresponds to quarantining ~5 legitimate emails per 100 legitimate arrivals—a business-tolerable disruption level. This metric directly reduces analyst workload and user disruption while enforcing a false-positive constraint.

**Secondary metrics**: 
- Recall at FPR = 5% (coverage of malicious emails)
- F1 score at optimal threshold
- Precision-recall curve and AUPRC
- Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. observed frequency)

### 7. Model Selection and Validation
The validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Cross-validation within the training split estimates generalization and guides hyperparameter ranges. The final threshold for FPR = 5% is determined on the validation set.

After validation-based selection, a single confirmation pass on the held-out test set is used to verify that the preferred configuration preserves its precision-at-FPR profile under fully held-out traffic before the training recipe is locked. If the leading configurations are effectively tied on validation but differ in stability on this confirmation pass, the more stable configuration is selected for the final train-plus-validation fit. Final reporting is then produced on the same held-out partition using the locked configuration.

### 8. Confound Controls and Ablations
To isolate text-context modeling from metadata exploitation:
- **Ablation 1**: Train the transformer on text only (no metadata) and compare to bag-of-words text-only baseline. Persistent transformer gains indicate text modeling; if gains vanish, metadata drives the difference.
- **Ablation 2**: Apply logistic regression to identical structured features, confirming both models handle metadata comparably.
- **Error stratification**: Analyze errors by sender domain, language, and attacker tactic to confirm the transformer is not memorizing organization-specific patterns.
- **Temporal robustness**: Report performance separately for each month represented in the test partition to detect concept drift.
- **Class-level metrics**: Report metrics separately for malicious and legitimate classes to rule out majority-class bias.

### 9. Scope and Limitations
This design establishes whether a transformer outperforms a linear baseline under realistic class imbalance and production label noise on precision at a constrained false-positive rate. It does not claim that the transformer uses text understanding versus surface patterns; ablations help isolate the mechanism. The experiment is scoped to a single organization or vendor; cross-organization generalization would require separate evaluation.

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Bag-of-Words for Enterprise Email Security

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model fine-tuned on full message text and sender metadata achieves higher precision at a constrained false-positive rate than a bag-of-words linear baseline, by exploiting cross-field context and obfuscated language patterns that simpler models miss.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling
We would source 18 months of inbound enterprise email from a large organization or email security vendor. Raw data includes subject/body text, MIME headers, sender reputation scores, URL metadata, and timestamps.

Labels are defined as follows:
- **Malicious**: Email confirmed as spam or phishing via user report + analyst triage review (within 24 hours) or downstream incident attribution.
- **Legitimate**: Email delivered without report, analyst rejection, or incident evidence within 90 days.
- Emails without definitive labels within 90 days are excluded.

To assess label quality, a separate cohort of 1,000–2,000 emails is manually reviewed by two independent annotators (Cohen's kappa ≥ 0.85) and used as ground truth to validate the production pipeline.

### 3. Data Splitting Strategy
We use a **temporal split** to reflect realistic deployment and prevent concept drift leakage:
- **Training set**: Months 1–12
- **Validation set**: Months 13–15 (hyperparameter tuning and model selection)
- **Test set**: Months 16–18 (held-out evaluation, touched only once)

This chronological split ensures the model generalizes to unseen attacker tactics and evolving message distributions rather than overfitting to random shuffling. Stratification on label distribution is applied within each split to maintain class proportions. Class imbalance (if present) is preserved across splits to reflect production conditions.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
All preprocessing steps are fit on the training set only and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets.

**Text features**: Subject and body text are tokenized using a pretrained tokenizer (e.g., BERT), truncated to 512 tokens.

**Structured metadata**: Sender domain reputation (categorical: trusted/neutral/suspicious), sender IP reputation, URL count, attachment presence/type, MIME authentication results (SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass/fail), recipient count, and presence of suspicious keywords identified from unigram analysis of the training set.

No features are engineered from the label (e.g., post-hoc incident flags). All features are available at message arrival time.

### 5. Models and Baselines
**Proposed model**: BERT-base (or RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the training set for binary classification. Concatenated subject + body text (512 tokens) plus structured metadata are concatenated to the pooled [CLS] representation. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, epochs, dropout) are tuned via grid or Bayesian search on the validation set using the primary metric.

**Baseline**: Logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on identical feature set: TF-IDF bag-of-words (unigram/bigram, fit on training text) + structured metadata. Regularization strength and class weights are tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on training data using the same primary metric. This baseline receives equal tuning effort, ensuring any performance gap reflects the transformer's ability to model text context, not superior feature engineering.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric**: Precision at fixed false-positive rate (FPR = 5%). We set the classification threshold so that false positives equal 5% of legitimate emails in the test set, then report precision (TP / (TP + FP)). This metric operationally matters: a 5% FPR corresponds to quarantining ~5 legitimate emails per 100 legitimate arrivals—a business-tolerable disruption level. This metric directly reduces analyst workload and user disruption while enforcing a false-positive constraint.

**Secondary metrics**: 
- Recall at FPR = 5% (coverage of malicious emails)
- F1 score at optimal threshold
- Precision-recall curve and AUPRC
- Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. observed frequency)

### 7. Model Selection and Validation
The validation set (months 13–15) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. 5-fold time-stratified cross-validation on the training set estimates generalization and guides hyperparameter ranges. The final threshold for FPR = 5% is determined on the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete, providing an unbiased performance estimate.

### 8. Confound Controls and Ablations
To isolate text-context modeling from metadata exploitation:
- **Ablation 1**: Train the transformer on text only (no metadata) and compare to bag-of-words text-only baseline. Persistent transformer gains indicate text modeling; if gains vanish, metadata drives the difference.
- **Ablation 2**: Apply logistic regression to identical structured features, confirming both models handle metadata comparably.
- **Error stratification**: Analyze errors by sender domain, language, and attacker tactic to confirm the transformer is not memorizing organization-specific patterns.
- **Temporal robustness**: Report performance separately for each month in the test set to detect concept drift.
- **Class-level metrics**: Report metrics separately for malicious and legitimate classes to rule out majority-class bias.

### 9. Scope and Limitations
This design establishes whether a transformer outperforms a linear baseline under realistic deployment conditions (temporal shift, label noise, class imbalance) on precision at a constrained false-positive rate. It does not claim that the transformer uses text understanding versus surface patterns; ablations help isolate the mechanism. The experiment is scoped to a single organization or vendor; cross-organization generalization would require separate evaluation.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model trained on sequential sensor and maintenance-history features will predict 7-day-ahead equipment failure more accurately than a static engineered-feature baseline for a manufacturing plant's critical pumps and compressors.
domain: Industrial manufacturing predictive maintenance for rotating equipment in a high-throughput plant
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequential Sensor Features vs. Static Baseline for 7-Day Equipment Failure Prediction

### 1. Objective and Scope
This experiment tests whether a gradient-boosted model trained on time-series sensor and maintenance-history features predicts 7-day-ahead rotating equipment failures more accurately than a static engineered-feature baseline. Success means the sequential model demonstrates materially better probability quality than the baseline on a held-out test set, indicating that real-time sensor dynamics provide more actionable early-failure estimates than asset-level aggregates alone.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling
The experiment uses 36 months of continuous IoT data from the plant's SCADA system: vibration, temperature, pressure, and motor current sampled at 1-minute intervals for all pumps and compressors. Maintenance records (work orders, parts replacements, failure timestamps) are sourced from the CMMS. A positive label (failure = 1) is assigned if an unplanned failure occurs within 7 days of the observation timestamp; planned maintenance actions are not labeled as failures. Labels are defined clearly to avoid ambiguity: "unplanned failure" means an emergency work order, a catastrophic sensor reading, or an explicit failure declaration by maintenance staff. Observations are generated daily for each asset, and forward labels are assigned to create a 7-day prediction window.

### 3. Data Splits and Temporal Integrity
To prevent leakage from post-failure maintenance signals or sensor repairs, a strict chronological split is imposed: Training (months 1–18, 50%), Validation (months 19–27, 33%), Test (months 28–36, 17%). No rolling windows or shuffling is used. Within each split, observations are stratified by asset_type (pump vs. compressor) to ensure both asset classes are represented proportionally and failure rates are comparable across splits. This temporal structure guarantees the model is evaluated on a future holdout period it has never seen and reflects realistic deployment where the model predicts on data from the future.

### 4. Feature Engineering
Two feature sets are constructed:

**Sequential Features (for the gradient-boosted model):** Rolling statistics (mean, std, min, max, 95th percentile) of each sensor over 1-hour, 6-hour, 24-hour, and 7-day windows; rate-of-change (first differences) over 6-hour and 24-hour windows; sample entropy of vibration signals over 24-hour windows to capture signal complexity degradation; maintenance event counts in the preceding 30 and 90 days; days-since-last-maintenance; asset age.

**Static Features (for the baseline):** Asset type, asset age, total historical maintenance count, asset-type-specific failure rate.

All preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit solely on the training set and applied to validation and test sets using the same fitted parameters. Specifically, forward-fill imputation (max 60-minute gap) and global mean imputation (longer gaps) are fit on training data, robust scaling (IQR-based) is computed on training data, and categorical encodings are derived from the training set.

### 5. Models and Baseline
**Gradient-boosted model:** XGBoost or LightGBM with learning rate, tree depth, and regularization tuned on the validation set. Hyperparameter grid: learning rate ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, max_depth ∈ {3, 5, 7}, min_child_weight ∈ {1, 3, 5}. Early stopping patience set to 50 rounds on validation AUROC. The model is trained to optimize AUROC with automatic class-weight balancing.

**Baseline:** Static logistic regression on the baseline feature set, with regularization parameter C tuned via grid search (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100}) on the validation set. The baseline represents the operational status quo: a simple model using only asset-level information without temporal sensor dynamics.

### 6. Evaluation Strategy
**Primary Metric:** Calibration error, summarized with reliability curves and expected calibration error (ECE). The maintenance team consumes predicted failure probabilities to prioritize inspections, order parts, and stage labor, so the central evaluation question is whether the predicted risk level corresponds closely to the observed 7-day failure frequency. A model that assigns well-calibrated probabilities will support more stable planning decisions across equipment classes and operating periods than a model that only improves ordering. Success is defined as the gradient-boosted model achieving meaningfully lower ECE than the baseline on the held-out test set.

**Secondary Metrics:** PR-AUC (precision-recall area under curve), sensitivity at 90% specificity (to show practical false-alarm trade-off), calibration curve, and per-asset-type AUROC to verify consistency across equipment classes.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Protocol
Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping use only the validation set. After the best-performing configuration is selected, both models are evaluated on the test set exactly once, with no further tuning. Test results are reported; no retraining or threshold adjustment is performed on test data.

### 8. Confound Controls
To verify improvements reflect genuine early-failure patterns and not artifacts: (1) **Maintenance sensitivity:** Model performance is re-evaluated on observations from periods with no recent maintenance (preceding 14 days), ruling out sensor-replacement or reset confounds. (2) **Per-asset analysis:** AUROC is computed per asset to detect asset-specific overfitting; high variability suggests the model is exploiting asset identity rather than sensor dynamics. (3) **Feature importance:** SHAP or permutation importance confirms top predictive features are sensor-derived (entropy, rate-of-change) rather than maintenance counts or asset age. (4) **Stratified holdout:** Test results are reported separately for pumps and compressors to confirm generalization. (5) **Manual review:** A small sample of near-threshold test predictions (≈50) is reviewed by plant engineers to check alignment with observed early-warning signs.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model trained on calendar-aware lagged demand, weather, holiday, and local event features will forecast next-day urban bike-share station demand more accurately than a seasonal naive baseline, especially during abrupt demand spikes and weather shifts.
domain: Transportation / logistics — next-day demand forecasting for a citywide bike-share network using station-level operational logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Gradient-Boosted Demand Forecasting vs. Seasonal Naive Baseline

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 3–4 years of production bike-share trip logs, capturing every pickup event with timestamp and station ID. External data sources include daily weather observations (temperature, precipitation, wind) from the official weather station(s) nearest the city, a complete holiday calendar for the region, and a curated event schedule (sports, concerts, conferences) geocoded to station locations. Station metadata includes opening dates, closure periods, and operational status flags.

The outcome (label) is defined as next-day demand: the total number of trip departures from a given station on calendar day t, aggregated over midnight-to-midnight local time. Days when a station was non-operational (not yet opened, or temporarily closed) are excluded from the label definition; zero-demand days are retained as valid observations. This ensures labels represent true operational demand, not data gaps or artifacts.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Because this is a time series forecasting task with strong temporal autocorrelation and seasonality, the data must be split chronologically to prevent leakage of future information into training. The 3–4 year dataset is partitioned into three disjoint, non-overlapping windows:

- **Training set:** days 1 through T_train (~70–75% of days; ~2.1–2.8 years)  
- **Validation set:** days T_train+1 through T_val (~15% of days; ~6–8 months)  
- **Test set:** days T_val+1 through end (~10–15% of days; ~4–6 months)

This split ensures that (a) the model never sees validation or test dates during training, (b) the validation set reflects a new temporal regime (different season, weather patterns, events) not in training, and (c) the test set provides a final, untouched evaluation of out-of-sample performance. All stations are included in each split; no per-station hold-out is performed, since the goal is to assess whether the model learns generalizable patterns, not station-specific noise.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (e.g., feature scaling, encoding schemes) are fit solely on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Features are constructed as follows:

**Calendar & seasonality:** day-of-week, month, day-of-year, days-until/since nearest holiday, and event indicators (binary flag if a major event is scheduled within 1 km of the station on day t).  

**Weather:** daily max, min, and mean temperature; total precipitation; wind speed. These are sourced from official weather stations and applied uniformly across all stations (or distance-weighted if multiple stations exist).  

**Station characteristics:** station ID (embedded or one-hot), cumulative historical trip count (trend proxy), station opening date.  

**Lagged demand features:** demand at station s on days t-1, t-7, t-14, and t-365, plus rolling 7-day and 28-day mean demand. *Critically*, lag features are computed in walk-forward fashion: for any day in the validation or test set, rolling statistics use only data up to and including that day, never future data. This prevents information leakage.

Missing values in weather are imputed using the previous available day; if unavailable, monthly median is used. Stations with all-zero training demand are retained and flagged.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Gradient Boosted Trees ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM), trained to output a point forecast of next-day demand per station. Hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, regularization) are tuned on the validation set via grid or random search, optimizing the primary metric (RMSE; see below).

The baseline is the Seasonal Naive (SNaive) forecast: for station s on day t, the forecast is the observed demand on day t-365 (same day last year). If that day is missing, SNaive uses the median demand for the same weekday in the preceding month. SNaive is chosen because it captures the dominant annual seasonality in bike-share demand and represents a strong, interpretable benchmark. Any proposed model that cannot beat SNaive has failed to extract meaningful predictive signal beyond seasonal patterns, making it unsuitable for deployment. Both the model and baseline are evaluated on identical test data under the same metrics.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), computed across all station-days in the test set. RMSE is chosen because large forecast misses are the errors with the clearest operational cost in bike-share systems: missing demand by 40 trips at a busy commuter station can create materially worse rebalancing and availability failures than the same miss at a low-volume station. A squared-error metric therefore places more emphasis on the upper tail of the error distribution, which is where dispatch and capacity-planning decisions are most sensitive. The test-set aggregate RMSE (not stratified a priori) is the decision criterion to avoid multiple comparison inflation.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **MAPE:** captures proportional accuracy across stations with different demand scales.
- **Median APE (MdAPE):** robust to outliers.
- **RMSE on surge days (demand > 75th percentile):** directly tests the hypothesis claim about performance during spikes.
- **RMSE on days with large weather changes:** tests performance during weather shifts.
- **Per-station bias:** detects systematic over-/under-forecasting.
- **Model-to-baseline RMSE ratio:** quantifies improvement; a ratio < 1.0 indicates the model beats SNaive.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

Hyperparameter selection uses the validation set. A search procedure (grid or random) computes RMSE on the validation set for each candidate hyperparameter configuration; the configuration with the lowest validation RMSE is locked in. The test set is never touched during this phase.

Once hyperparameters are finalized, the model is retrained on the combined training+validation set (using the selected hyperparameters), and then evaluated on the held-out test set exactly once. This ensures the test evaluation is a genuine out-of-sample estimate.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Information leakage:** Prevented by temporal split (no future dates in training) and walk-forward lag computation (no future data in lagged features). Weather and event information is assumed known in advance (realistic for day-ahead forecasting) but sensitivity is checked by re-running without event features.

**Distribution shift from station changes:** Controlled by flagging station opening/closure dates in metadata and restricting labels to operational periods. Secondary analyses stratify performance by station age (new vs. established) to verify the model does not rely disproportionately on old, high-volume stations.

**Baseline inadequacy:** SNaive captures annual seasonality; any advantage of the boosted model reflects learning beyond seasonality alone.

**Aggregation bias:** RMSE is computed per-station-day (not pre-aggregated), and secondary proportional-error metrics are reported by station demand quartile to verify that gains are not confined to the busiest stations.

The experiment would establish whether a gradient-boosted model, trained on calendar, weather, holiday, and lag features, can forecast next-day bike-share demand more accurately than seasonal naive, particularly during demand surges and weather shifts. It does not establish causation (e.g., whether weather *causes* demand changes) or whether the model is suitable for operational deployment without additional testing (e.g., lead time for data availability, retraining frequency, online adaptation).

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 3–4 years of production bike-share trip logs, capturing every pickup event with timestamp and station ID. External data sources include daily weather observations (temperature, precipitation, wind) from the official weather station(s) nearest the city, a complete holiday calendar for the region, and a curated event schedule (sports, concerts, conferences) geocoded to station locations. Station metadata includes opening dates, closure periods, and operational status flags.

The outcome (label) is defined as next-day demand: the total number of trip departures from a given station on calendar day t, aggregated over midnight-to-midnight local time. Days when a station was non-operational (not yet opened, or temporarily closed) are excluded from the label definition; zero-demand days are retained as valid observations. ### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Because this is a time series forecasting task with strong temporal autocorrelation and seasonality, the data must be split chronologically to prevent leakage of future information into training. The 3–4 year dataset is partitioned into three disjoint, non-overlapping windows:

This split ensures that (a) the model never sees validation or test dates during training, (b) the validation set reflects a new temporal regime (different season, weather patterns, events) not in training, and (c) the test set provides a final, untouched evaluation of out-of-sample performance. All stations are included in each split; no per-station hold-out is performed, since the goal is to assess whether the model learns generalizable patterns, not station-specific noise.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (e.g., feature scaling, encoding schemes) are fit solely on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Features are constructed as follows:

**Calendar & seasonality:** day-of-week, month, day-of-year, days-until/since nearest holiday, and event indicators (binary flag if a major event is scheduled within 1 km of the station on day t).  

**Weather:** daily max, min, and mean temperature; total precipitation; wind speed. These are sourced from official weather stations and applied uniformly across all stations (or distance-weighted if multiple stations exist).  

**Station characteristics:** station ID (embedded or one-hot), cumulative historical trip count (trend proxy), station opening date.  

**Lagged demand features:** demand at station s on days t-1, t-7, t-14, and t-365, plus rolling 7-day and 28-day mean demand. *Critically*, lag features are computed in walk-forward fashion: for any day in the validation or test set, rolling statistics use only data up to and including that day, never future data. This prevents information leakage.

Missing values in weather are imputed using the previous available day; if unavailable, monthly median is used. Stations with all-zero training demand are retained and flagged.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Gradient Boosted Trees ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM), trained to output a point forecast of next-day demand per station. Hyperparameters (max depth, learning rate, regularization) are tuned on the validation set via grid or random search, optimizing the primary metric (MAPE; see below).

The baseline is the Seasonal Naive (SNaive) forecast: for station s on day t, the forecast is the observed demand on day t-365 (same day last year). If that day is missing, SNaive uses the median demand for the same weekday in the preceding month. SNaive is chosen because it captures the dominant annual seasonality in bike-share demand and represents a strong, interpretable benchmark. Any proposed model that cannot beat SNaive has failed to extract meaningful predictive signal beyond seasonal patterns, making it unsuitable for deployment. Both the model and baseline are evaluated on identical test data under the same metrics.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), defined as the mean of |actual − forecast| / (actual + 1) across all station-days in the test set. The +1 offset handles zero-demand days gracefully. MAPE is chosen because: (1) it is proportional, penalizing a 10% error equally at high-demand and low-demand stations, aligning with the stakeholder's operational concern; (2) it is scale-invariant and interpretable (e.g., "15% MAPE"); (3) it is standard in forecasting. The test-set aggregate MAPE (not stratified a priori) is the decision criterion to avoid multiple comparison inflation.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **RMSE:** captures sensitivity to large errors.
- **Median APE (MdAPE):** robust to outliers.
- **MAPE on surge days (demand > 75th percentile):** directly tests the hypothesis claim about performance during spikes.
- **MAPE on days with large weather changes:** tests performance during weather shifts.
- **Per-station bias:** detects systematic over-/under-forecasting.
- **Model-to-baseline MAPE ratio:** quantifies improvement; a ratio < 1.0 indicates the model beats SNaive.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

Hyperparameter selection uses the validation set. A search procedure (grid or random) computes MAPE on the validation set for each candidate hyperparameter configuration; the configuration with the lowest validation MAPE is locked in. The test set is never touched during this phase.

Once hyperparameters are finalized, the model is retrained on the combined training+validation set (using the selected hyperparameters), and then evaluated on the held-out test set exactly once. This ensures the test evaluation is a genuine out-of-sample estimate.

### 7. Confound Controls

**Information leakage:** Prevented by temporal split (no future dates in training) and walk-forward lag computation (no future data in lagged features). Weather and event information is assumed known in advance (realistic for day-ahead forecasting) but sensitivity is checked by re-running without event features.

**Distribution shift from station changes:** Controlled by flagging station opening/closure dates in metadata and restricting labels to operational periods. Secondary analyses stratify performance by station age (new vs. established) to verify the model does not rely disproportionately on old, high-volume stations.

**Baseline inadequacy:** SNaive captures annual seasonality; any advantage of the boosted model reflects learning beyond seasonality alone.

**Aggregation bias:** MAPE is computed per-station-day (not pre-aggregated), ensuring all station-days are weighted equally. Stratified secondary metrics (by station demand quartile) verify generalization across station types.

The experiment would establish whether a gradient-boosted model, trained on calendar, weather, holiday, and lag features, can forecast next-day bike-share demand more accurately than seasonal naive, particularly during demand surges and weather shifts. It does not establish causation (e.g., whether weather *causes* demand changes) or whether the model is suitable for operational deployment without additional testing (e.g., lead time for data availability, retraining frequency, online adaptation).

hypothesis: In hospital readmission prediction, a sequence model trained on longitudinal EHR events and medication changes will outperform a static feature baseline built from the most recent encounter alone in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission.
domain: Acute inpatient care using longitudinal electronic health records from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for 30-Day Unplanned Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Outcome Definition

The experiment uses 4 years of de-identified EHR data from a multi-hospital health system, including all inpatient encounters with complete discharge information and a 30-day follow-up window. Admissions are included if the length of stay is ≥1 day and the discharge disposition is documented; transfers to another acute facility are excluded because they represent planned continuations of care, not readmission risk.

The outcome is **unplanned 30-day readmission**, defined as any inpatient admission occurring within 30 days of discharge that is not a scheduled return (e.g., for dialysis, chemotherapy, or elective surgery). Planned readmissions are identified and excluded using discharge summary flags and admission reason codes. Crucially, no information from the readmission encounter itself—such as the reason for readmission or its outcome—is used in the feature set. This prevents label leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided into **training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%)** cohorts by assigning patients to one split only, using stratification over hospital, calendar quarter, and readmission outcome. This preserves comparable case mix, seasonal utilization patterns, and positive-class prevalence across splits while ensuring that no patient contributes admissions to more than one cohort. Balancing by quarter is important in this setting because respiratory surges, service-line changes, and site-specific documentation shifts can otherwise produce an unusually easy or unusually hard validation window that distorts model selection. Within each split, the admission-level analyses naturally account for between-hospital variation because the data spans multiple hospitals and all calendar periods.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Static features** include age at admission, sex, race/ethnicity, and insurance type. **Recent encounter features** are derived from the most recent prior admission within 12 months: primary diagnosis (mapped to Clinical Classifications Software categories), comorbidity count (Charlson index components), admission type, discharge disposition, and length of stay.

**Sequence features** encode the patient's full encounter history over the 12 months prior to the index discharge, up to 100 prior encounters (padded to a fixed length). Each encounter in the sequence is represented by the date, diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures (CPT codes), and abnormal lab results (flagged using reference ranges). Discharge medications are encoded as additions, changes, or discontinuations relative to the 30 days prior to admission.

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) is **fit on the training set only** and applied identically to validation and test sets. Temporal normalization (e.g., relative time from index admission) is computed using training-set statistics. This ensures that no information from validation or test periods influences the preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A recurrent neural network (GRU or LSTM) ingests the padded encounter sequence and produces a hidden representation, which is concatenated with static and recent features and passed through 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU activation, dropout 0.3–0.5) to a sigmoid output. The model is trained with class-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (embedding dimension, hidden size, dropout rate, learning rate, batch size) are tuned on the validation set using grid or random search.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on static and recent-encounter features alone, without sequence information. This represents the current standard in many settings: prediction based on the most recent snapshot encounter. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort (L2 regularization strength is grid-searched on the validation set) and uses the same preprocessing pipeline and train/val/test split. The comparison is fair and isolates the contribution of sequential temporal information.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary metric: Area under the ROC curve (AUROC).** This metric is chosen because the experiment compares performance across hospitals, seasons, and utilization strata where readmission prevalence can vary materially. AUROC evaluates whether the model ranks readmitted patients above non-readmitted patients consistently across these settings, making it suitable as the main criterion for both model selection and subgroup comparison. This supports a cleaner comparison between the sequence model and the static baseline when the positive class rate differs across sites or calendar periods.

**Secondary metrics** include AUPRC (to quantify performance in the high-risk tail), precision at 90% recall (to assess the ability to catch most true positives), recall at 90% precision (for highest-confidence predictions), calibration curves (to verify that predicted probabilities reflect true risk), and net benefit curves (to evaluate clinical utility across intervention thresholds).

The **test set is used exactly once**, after model selection is complete. Final metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals (stratified bootstrap) and are stratified by hospital and time period within the test window to detect confounding.

### 6. Model Selection and Confound Controls

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using **AUROC** as the selection criterion. Once the best configuration is identified, the model is retrained on the combined training and validation sets and evaluated on the held-out test set.

To address potential confounds: **(i) Hospital-specific artifacts:** Performance is stratified by hospital. Gains concentrated in one facility suggest local coding overfitting rather than generalizable signal. **(ii) Unmeasured confounding:** Calibration is examined within admission-type and length-of-stay strata. Systematic miscalibration across strata suggests confounding. **(iii) Utilization intensity:** The baseline model includes prior encounter counts; the sequence model's incremental gain is examined after controlling for baseline utilization, isolating the contribution of temporal detail. **(iv) Temporal variation:** Test-set performance is compared across calendar segments within the held-out cohort to detect degradation associated with changing institutional conditions.

### 7. Scope and Interpretation

This design will establish whether a sequence model outperforms a static baseline in held-out patients, while controlling for confounding by utilization and hospital-level factors and examining robustness across hospitals and calendar periods. It will not establish causality, the mechanism of improved performance, or whether the gain is clinically meaningful for a specific intervention. Those questions would require additional analysis of feature importance, external validation, and pilot deployment.
