hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that uses alert text, severity, asset criticality, and incident-response history will surface the top urgent alerts more effectively than a binary classifier trained to predict whether an alert is malicious, as measured by analyst time-to-triage and precision at the top of the queue.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank for SOC Alert Triage

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses 24 months of historical SIEM/EDR alert data from a single enterprise SOC. Each alert record includes metadata: rule name, baseline severity score (0–100), asset/user context, alert text, and timestamps. Ground truth labels are derived from analyst disposition actions: an alert is labeled *urgent* if it was escalated to an incident ticket within 4 hours of generation or was retrospectively identified as part of a confirmed incident. To ensure label quality, all labels are finalized at least 30 days after alert generation, allowing delayed incident investigation to complete. Alerts with no definitive outcome after 30 days are treated as non-urgent. This definition aligns with the stakeholder's operational goal: surfacing alerts that analysts will actually act on.

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal Stratification

The 24-month dataset is split chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–18 for validation, and months 19–24 for test. This temporal split is critical because SOC environments exhibit substantial drift—attacker tactics evolve, detection rules are updated, and asset inventory changes. A random split would allow future information to influence past alerts, leaking information about rule changes or newly discovered vulnerabilities that did not exist when early alerts were generated. Chronological ordering respects causality and matches the deployment scenario: the model must generalize to unseen future attacks.

Within each temporal split, alerts are stratified by severity class (critical, high, medium, low) to ensure each stratum is represented proportionally across train, validation, and test sets. This prevents the model from overfitting to severity as a proxy for urgency and ensures balanced evaluation across alert types.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set. The feature set includes: (1) alert text vectorized via TF-IDF with vocabulary learned from training alerts only; (2) baseline severity score from the detection rule; (3) asset criticality class (critical, high, medium, low); (4) incident-response history: days since the last alert from the same asset, count of escalated incidents involving this rule in the past 30 days, and average time-to-escalation for this rule class; and (5) temporal features (hour of day, day of week) to capture analyst availability patterns. No feature encodes the label, analyst identity, or non-generalizable asset names. Numeric features are scaled using training set mean and standard deviation; out-of-vocabulary text terms in validation/test are handled via backoff to a default token.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a Learning-to-Rank (LTR) model trained via LambdaMART, a gradient boosted tree approach that directly optimizes ranking loss. This model outputs a continuous score for each alert that defines its position in a prioritized queue.

The baseline is a logistic regression (or gradient boosted) binary classifier trained to predict escalation probability using the identical feature set. To ensure fair comparison, the baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. The baseline produces a ranking by sorting alerts by predicted escalation probability. This represents a common SOC practice: filtering alerts by a binary decision threshold. Both models use the same preprocessing pipeline and feature engineering.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10 (NDCG@10). NDCG@10 measures whether truly urgent alerts appear in the top 10 positions of the ranked queue, with exponentially higher weight on positions 1–3. This metric directly aligns with analyst workflow: most analysts review only the first 5–10 queue items before context-switching. NDCG is normalized to [0,1], enabling interpretable comparison across test sets with different baseline urgency rates.

Secondary metrics include Precision at 10 (P@10), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and Recall at 10 to provide a multifaceted view of ranking quality. We also conduct a small human analyst agreement study on 50 test-set alerts to verify that the model's ranking aligns with domain intuition. Temporal stability is evaluated by computing NDCG@10 separately for months 19–20, 21–22, and 23–24 to detect ranking degradation over time.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 13–18) by maximizing NDCG@10. A 5-fold temporal cross-validation on the training set estimates tuning sensitivity. The test set (months 19–24) is held out entirely: it is opened only after hyperparameter selection is complete, and a single final evaluation is performed without threshold adjustment or retraining. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Two key confounds are addressed: (1) The model may learn analyst prioritization patterns rather than genuine threat severity. We mitigate this by inspecting feature importance (SHAP values) to confirm that learned signals align with known cybersecurity principles, not merely analyst availability. We also retrain on alerts from a different analyst cohort to test generalization. (2) The model may merely amplify baseline severity without learning. We test this via ablation: retraining using only incident-response and temporal features, excluding baseline severity. If performance is similar, independent ranking signals are being learned.

This experiment establishes that the LTR model produces better-ranked alerts (higher NDCG@10) than the binary baseline under temporal evaluation. It does not establish ground truth causality—only that ranking is more operationally effective than binary classification for this SOC's alert workflow.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer-based classifier fine-tuned on the full email thread plus sender-recipient graph context will detect business email compromise and credential-phishing attempts better than a text-only classifier trained on the email body and subject line, as measured by precision-recall performance on a future-time holdout.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Email Classifier with Graph Context

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of raw inbound email records from the organization's mail gateway, spanning months 1–18. For each email, we retain: body, subject, headers, sender and recipient identities, attachment metadata, and sender reputation signals (computed from historical sending patterns). The conversation thread structure (prior messages in the same conversation thread) is also preserved, but truncated at the send time of each email to exclude future replies that would not have been available at the time of classification.

Labels are obtained from four corroborating sources: (1) security analyst manual review (primary gold standard), (2) user-reported phishing submissions confirmed by the analyst, (3) sandbox malware verdicts, and (4) post-incident response records linking compromised user accounts to specific emails. An email is labeled positive (malicious) if confirmed by analyst review or incident response; ambiguous or single-signal cases are included only with corroborating evidence. We expect extreme class imbalance (0.5–2% malicious emails) reflecting real-world prevalence.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To avoid optimistic bias from attack pattern leakage, we employ a strict chronological split:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (12 months of historical email)
- **Validation set:** Months 13–15 (3 months for hyperparameter tuning)
- **Test set:** Months 16–18 (3 months of future-time holdout, never touched until final evaluation)

Justification: Email attack tactics, sender spoofing strategies, and threat landscapes drift over time. A random split would allow models to implicitly learn patterns from future months that have already occurred, inflating measured performance. The temporal split ensures the experiment reflects the true deployment scenario: training on historical data and evaluating on genuinely future, unseen email campaigns. This also captures concept drift—the degradation of model performance as new threats emerge—which random splits obscure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model (proposed)** receives:
- Full email thread (all prior messages in the conversation, truncated at send time)
- Email body and subject
- Sender and recipient identities (embedded as learned representations)
- Sender reputation score (computed from historical sending patterns, fit on training data only)
- Graph context: sender-recipient relationships, organizational hierarchy, and local neighborhood structure (1–2 hops), derived from communications prior to the email send time

**Baseline model (text-only)** receives:
- Email body and subject only
- No thread history, no sender/recipient identity, no graph context

All tokenization, vocabulary, and preprocessing transformers (including the sender reputation normalizer) are fit exclusively on the training set. The same fitted transformers are applied to validation and test sets without refitting, preventing future-data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A BERT or DistilBERT transformer encoder fine-tuned on concatenated email thread and structured graph embeddings. Sender and recipient nodes are embedded in a separate space and optionally integrated via graph attention layers. The model outputs binary classification logits converted to probabilities via sigmoid.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression or XGBoost trained on TF-IDF/bag-of-words features from email body and subject. Both models receive identical hyperparameter tuning effort: grid or random search (same computational budget), cross-validation on the training set to select the best configuration.

Justification: The baseline is a principled text-only classifier, providing a clear ablation of architectural depth and contextual features. Equivalent tuning effort ensures the comparison isolates model capacity, not tuning investment.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using precision-recall AUC (AUPRC) as the selection metric. Grid or random search is performed; the configuration achieving the highest validation AUPRC is selected. Both models are then retrained on the full training set (months 1–12) using the selected hyperparameters. The test set is held and never used for tuning or selection.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision-recall AUC (AUPRC) on the test set. Justification: Class imbalance makes ROC-AUC misleading. PR-AUC is sensitive to performance on the rare positive class and directly reflects the operational trade-off between catching threats (recall/sensitivity) and minimizing false alarms (precision).

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision at fixed recall targets (90%, 95%, 99%)
- Recall at fixed false-positive rates (reflecting deployment constraints)
- Stratified performance by sender type (external, internal, vendor) to detect if improvements generalize
- Temporal degradation: PR-AUC on early test months (16–17) vs. late test months (18) to assess concept drift

### 7. Controls for Confounds

A known confound exists: observed improvements could be driven by access to richer metadata (sender reputation, graph edges) rather than transformer architecture itself.

**Controls:**
1. **Ablation:** Train the transformer on text only (no thread, no graph, no metadata) and compare to the full model. If text-only transformer performs as well as the baseline, the advantage is architectural. If it underperforms, improvements come from metadata.
2. **Baseline enrichment:** Train the baseline on the same rich feature set (sender reputation, graph statistics engineered as numeric features). If performance remains lower than the transformer, the claim is that transformers better utilize context. If performance converges, improvements are purely from feature access.
3. **Stratification checks:** Separately report performance on emails with/without thread history and with/without multi-hop graph connections. If improvements concentrate only where additional context is available, narrower claims can be justified.

All stratified evaluations use the test set. Final results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap to account for class imbalance and test-set variability.

hypothesis: A survival model that uses longitudinal EHR sequences and medication exposure history will predict 30-day readmission after heart failure discharge more accurately than a static tabular classifier trained only on the final pre-discharge encounter.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML: hospital readmission risk prediction for adult heart failure patients using electronic health record data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Survival Model for 30-Day Heart Failure Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

The experiment uses structured EHR data spanning 4–5 years from 3–5 hospital systems, targeting ≥5,000 index discharges of adult heart failure patients. The data includes encounter tables (admission/discharge dates, type), diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures, laboratory values with timestamps, vital signs, medication orders, and prior admission history.

The primary outcome is binary: readmission to acute-care hospital within 30 calendar days of discharge. Competing events (death, hospice transfer, long-term care placement) occurring within 30 days are treated as censoring—these patients contribute label = 0, reflecting the clinical use case (we seek to identify patients who will return to acute care, not to compete with mortality prediction).

Data quality issues—missingness in labs (~5–15%), sparse vital signs, and medication reconciliation gaps—are documented and handled via missingness indicators (added as features) and imputation fit on training data only.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent leakage and mirror real-world deployment, the cohort is split chronologically: training set consists of admissions discharged in months 1–30 (2.5 years), validation set months 31–36 (0.5 years), and test set months 37–42 (0.5 years). No random shuffling is applied. Within each set, patients are stratified by discharge quarter and facility to ensure balanced representation and detect facility-level confounding.

This temporal design ensures that no future clinical information influences model training and respects the forward-looking prediction task. The validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning; the test set is locked and accessed only once for final evaluation.

### 3. Feature Construction

**Longitudinal features (for survival model):** Lab trajectories from prior 6 months (creatinine, BNP, potassium, ejection fraction, and others) aligned to encounter timestamps and summarized as sequences, recent values, trends, and measurement frequency. Medication exposure history: active medications at discharge, medication refill patterns, and cumulative supply days in the 90 days prior. Vital sign trends: blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate from the final 48 hours and prior 30 days of hospitalization.

**Static encounter features (for both survival model and baseline):** Final pre-discharge snapshot including primary and secondary diagnoses (top 20 ICD codes), procedures performed, admission type, and length of stay. Prior utilization: count of admissions in the prior 6 and 12 months, ED visits, and outpatient encounters. Comorbidity indices (Elixhauser, Charlson).

All features are computed from information available at discharge; no post-discharge information is used. Preprocessing—scaling, categorical encoding, and imputation—is fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Survival model:** Cox proportional hazards regression or a flexible alternative (random survival forests, gradient-boosted survival trees). The model ingests both longitudinal and static features and outputs a risk score or 30-day survival probability.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on static features only (no longitudinal sequences or medication history beyond the current admission). The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search optimizing validation ROC-AUC). This isolates the effect of sequential information and the survival framework, not differences in model architecture or data access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: ROC-AUC at 30 days.** This measures discrimination (ability to rank-order patients by risk) and directly supports clinical triage. It is insensitive to class imbalance and threshold-agnostic.

**Secondary metrics:** Concordance index (survival-specific discrimination), calibration (observed vs. predicted readmission rate in risk deciles), sensitivity/specificity at operationally relevant thresholds (25%, 40% predicted risk), decision curve analysis (clinical net benefit across thresholds), and facility-level AUC variability (checks for domain shift).

All metrics are reported on the test set with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrapping (1,000 replicates, stratified by facility).

### 6. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analyses

The primary known confound is that the survival model may outperform due to better handling of missingness or censoring, rather than to the value of longitudinal information. To isolate this, an ablation study trains a survival model on static features only and compares it to the baseline; any performance gap due to methodology (not features) becomes explicit. Additionally, sensitivity analyses are conducted on (1) a subset of patients with complete data to assess whether missingness handling drives the result, and (2) facility-stratified models to account for discharge practice heterogeneity.

### 7. Scope and Claims

This design establishes whether a longitudinal survival model predicts 30-day readmission better than a static classifier in a retrospective cohort. It does **not** establish causality, does not address unobserved confounds (e.g., post-discharge adherence), and does not validate prospective deployment performance. A pilot prospective validation would be required before clinical implementation.
sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Survival Model for 30-Day Heart Failure Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

The experiment uses structured EHR data spanning 4–5 years from 3–5 hospital systems, targeting ≥5,000 index discharges of adult heart failure patients. The data includes encounter tables (admission/discharge dates, type), diagnoses (ICD codes), procedures, laboratory values with timestamps, vital signs, medication orders, and prior admission history.

The primary outcome is binary: readmission to acute-care hospital within 30 calendar days of discharge. Competing events (death, hospice transfer, long-term care placement) occurring within 30 days are treated as censoring—these patients contribute label = 0, reflecting the clinical use case (we seek to identify patients who will return to acute care, not to compete with mortality prediction).

Data quality issues—missingness in labs (~5–15%), sparse vital signs, and medication reconciliation gaps—are documented and handled via missingness indicators (added as features) and imputation fit on training data only.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent leakage and mirror real-world deployment, the cohort is split chronologically: training set consists of admissions discharged in months 1–30 (2.5 years), validation set months 31–36 (0.5 years), and test set months 37–42 (0.5 years). No random shuffling is applied. Within each set, patients are stratified by discharge quarter and facility to ensure balanced representation and detect facility-level confounding.

This temporal design ensures that no future clinical information influences model training and respects the forward-looking prediction task. The validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning; the test set is locked and accessed only once for final evaluation.

### 3. Feature Construction

**Longitudinal features (for survival model):** Lab trajectories from prior 6 months (creatinine, BNP, potassium, ejection fraction, and others) aligned to encounter timestamps and summarized as sequences, recent values, trends, and measurement frequency. Medication exposure history: active medications at discharge, medication refill patterns, and cumulative supply days in the 90 days prior. Vital sign trends: blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate from the final 48 hours and prior 30 days of hospitalization.

**Static encounter features (for both survival model and baseline):** Final pre-discharge snapshot including primary and secondary diagnoses (top 20 ICD codes), procedures performed, admission type, and length of stay. Prior utilization: count of admissions in the prior 6 and 12 months, ED visits, and outpatient encounters. Comorbidity indices (Elixhauser, Charlson).

All features are computed from information available at discharge; no post-discharge information is used. Preprocessing—scaling, categorical encoding, and imputation—is fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Survival model:** Cox proportional hazards regression or a flexible alternative (random survival forests, gradient-boosted survival trees). The model ingests both longitudinal and static features and outputs a risk score or 30-day survival probability.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on static features only (no longitudinal sequences or medication history beyond the current admission). The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search optimizing validation ROC-AUC). This isolates the effect of sequential information and the survival framework, not differences in model architecture or data access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: ROC-AUC at 30 days.** This measures discrimination (ability to rank-order patients by risk) and directly supports clinical triage. It is insensitive to class imbalance and threshold-agnostic.

**Secondary metrics:** Concordance index (survival-specific discrimination), calibration (observed vs. predicted readmission rate in risk deciles), sensitivity/specificity at operationally relevant thresholds (25%, 40% predicted risk), decision curve analysis (clinical net benefit across thresholds), and facility-level AUC variability (checks for domain shift).

All metrics are reported on the test set with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrapping (1,000 replicates, stratified by facility).

### 6. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analyses

The primary known confound is that the survival model may outperform due to better handling of missingness or censoring, rather than to the value of longitudinal information. To isolate this, an ablation study trains a survival model on static features only and compares it to the baseline; any performance gap due to methodology (not features) becomes explicit. Additionally, sensitivity analyses are conducted on (1) a subset of patients with complete data to assess whether missingness handling drives the result, and (2) facility-stratified models to account for discharge practice heterogeneity.

### 7. Scope and Claims

This design establishes whether a longitudinal survival model predicts 30-day readmission better than a static classifier in a retrospective cohort. It does not establish causality, does not address unobserved confounds (e.g., post-discharge adherence), and does not validate prospective deployment performance. A pilot prospective validation would be required before clinical implementation.

hypothesis: A sequence model that uses the last 12 months of transaction events and account metadata will predict small-business loan default more accurately than a tabular model built from aggregated monthly features, when both are evaluated on future-origin loans.
domain: Finance — small-business lending risk modeling for unsecured or partially secured loans
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Tabular Models for Small-Business Loan Default Prediction

### 1. Research Question and Scope
The hypothesis is that a sequence model ingesting 12 months of pre-origination transaction events will predict small-business loan default more accurately than a tabular model built from aggregated monthly statistics, when both are evaluated on out-of-time loans. We will test this by comparing out-of-sample discrimination (AUROC) on a held-out cohort of loans originated after the training and validation windows.

### 2. Data and Label Definition
The analysis uses production loan records spanning 4–5 years from a single lender, including origination metadata, monthly repayment histories, daily transaction streams from linked business bank accounts, and servicing outcomes. The outcome is binary: default is defined as charge-off or 90+ days past due within 24 months of origination. This definition aligns with regulatory conventions and operational loss recognition. Labels are assigned only after a minimum 24-month observation window post-origination to ensure default events have time to manifest. Any loans in active repayment or with incomplete observation windows at the data cutoff are excluded, ensuring label completeness and temporal fairness across all splits.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
Because the stakeholder's concern is generalization to future-origin loans and production systems are vulnerable to concept drift, we use a chronological (origin-date) split rather than random partitioning. The rationale is direct: random splits would allow temporal leakage and would not reflect the deployment scenario. We divide loans by origination month as follows:
- **Training set:** Loans originated in months 1–24, with labels assigned after the 24-month observation window.
- **Validation set:** Loans originated in months 25–30, with labels assigned after the 24-month observation window.
- **Test set:** Loans originated in months 31–36, with labels assigned after the 24-month observation window.

This structure ensures the test set contains loans the model has never seen during training or validation, and it mirrors the real deployment cadence. Stratification by origination month within each set reduces seasonal and cohort artifacts.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
All preprocessing, scaling, and imputation transformers are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Critically, transaction-derived features must not leak post-origination information. For the sequence model, the input is the daily transaction sequence from the 12 months prior to origination: each transaction is encoded as (amount, merchant category, type), zero-padded or masked where transaction data is unavailable. For the tabular baseline, transaction data is aggregated into summary statistics over the same 12-month pre-origination window: average and standard deviation of monthly revenue, transaction frequency, and indicators of prior delinquency. Both models receive the same account-level metadata: business age, credit score, industry, loan amount, term, rate, and a binary indicator of transaction data availability. This ensures the sequence model is not inadvertently given more complete data than the baseline. Missing transaction histories are handled identically: a missingness indicator is included in both models, and missing transaction sequences are zero-padded.

### 5. Models and Baseline
The sequence model is a bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder applied to transaction event sequences, followed by a dense layer and sigmoid output. Hyperparameters (embedding size, hidden dimensions, dropout, learning rate) are tuned on the validation set using grid or Bayesian search with early stopping (patience=10 epochs).

The baseline is L2-regularized logistic regression trained on aggregated features. This baseline is fair because it (1) uses the same origination-time information and metadata, (2) employs the same label definition and temporal split, (3) receives equivalent tuning effort (grid search over regularization strength), and (4) is evaluated on the same test set under identical conditions. A secondary baseline of XGBoost on aggregated features is also trained to confirm the sequence model's advantage is robust to the baseline choice.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve) on the test set. AUROC measures discrimination (ranking ability) and is appropriate here because it is threshold-agnostic, insensitive to class imbalance (default rates ~5–10%), and directly corresponds to the stakeholder's goal of identifying risky loans. Reporting test-set AUROC on loans originated in months 31–36 directly tests the hypothesis of out-of-time generalization.

Secondary metrics include:
- **Calibration (ECE):** Expected calibration error on test deciles, ensuring the model's predicted probabilities align with observed default rates, which is crucial for loss estimation and pricing.
- **Stability:** Comparison of validation vs. test AUROC to detect overfitting or distribution shift.
- **Fairness:** Stratified AUROC by industry and loan size to detect hidden performance heterogeneity.
- **Precision-recall curve:** For visualization of trade-offs at alternative decision thresholds.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Policy
Both models are trained on the training set with hyperparameters selected to maximize validation-set AUROC. Early stopping uses the validation set; no hyperparameter changes or architecture modifications occur after test-set evaluation. Once the best model configuration is selected, it is evaluated exactly once on the test set. The reported performance (AUROC, calibration, fairness metrics) is the test-set performance; the test set is never used for tuning or threshold selection.

### 8. Controls for Confounds
We address known confounds as follows:
- **Transaction data availability:** Both models include a missingness indicator and handle absent transactions identically (zero-padding or masking), so the sequence model is not advantaged by differential data completeness.
- **Post-origination leakage:** All features use only pre-origination information; post-origination repayment or delinquency behavior is excluded from both models.
- **Temporal drift:** The chronological split naturally exposes both models to different economic periods and borrower cohorts, simulating realistic deployment conditions.
- **Model capacity:** Both models are regularized appropriately for their class (LSTM dropout, logistic regression L2, XGBoost max depth) and receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning.

This design tests whether the sequence representation improves out-of-time discrimination but does not establish causality (why the sequence model is better) or performance in a live deployment with dynamic policies.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer-based text model that jointly encodes the email body, subject, sender history, and attachment metadata will detect phishing emails with higher PR-AUC than a gradient-boosted model trained on hand-engineered features alone, especially when evaluated on a temporally held-out quarter with novel attack campaigns.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Phishing Detection vs. Hand-Engineered Features

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

This experiment uses production inbound email logs from a large enterprise mail gateway spanning 16 consecutive quarters (4 years). The raw data includes email headers, subject lines, message bodies (full text), sender and recipient metadata, attachment metadata (file names, types, sizes), and analyst-reviewed labels. Labels are applied post-delivery with a typical 1–7 day review lag by security analysts; we include only emails with a final review status (phishing, malware, or benign), excluding uncertain or disputed cases to minimize label noise. To reduce confounding from internal forwarding and auto-replies, we filter to ingress-only traffic. This dataset is expected to be highly imbalanced, with malicious emails forming 0.1–5% of total traffic, reflecting the production environment the models will defend.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

All train/validation/test splits are performed chronologically, not randomly. Specifically:
- **Training set:** quarters 1–12 (12 months)
- **Validation set:** quarters 13–15 (3 months)
- **Test set:** quarter 16 (1 month)

This temporal split is critical because phishing and malware campaigns exhibit strong temporal autocorrelation: attackers reuse infrastructure, sender patterns, and tactical approaches within and across campaigns. A random split would allow the model to memorize recurring campaigns and spuriously inflate reported performance. The temporal split forces the model to generalize to novel, unseen attack patterns, which is the true operational requirement. Quarters 16 is held-out completely and used only for final evaluation; it represents future, previously unseen threats.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model input:**
The transformer receives concatenated representations of: (1) full email body text with HTML tags and excessive whitespace removed, (2) subject line, (3) sender display name and domain, (4) recipient list, and (5) attachment metadata encoded as text (file names, extensions, sizes, MIME types). All text is tokenized into subword tokens using a BPE vocabulary fit exclusively on the training set. The sequence is truncated to 512 tokens, preserving subject and sender in full, then truncating the body if necessary. Text segments are separated by [SEP] tokens.

**Gradient-boosted baseline input:**
The baseline receives only hand-engineered features: SPF/DKIM/DMARC flags, sender reputation score (historical frequency and flagging rate from training data), domain age and registrar country, attachment properties (count, total size, presence of executables), recipient group (external/internal/VIP), subject line length and keyword flags (urgency, financial terms), and body text length and keyword density (phishing keywords from domain expertise). Importantly, the baseline does not receive raw email text; it receives only pre-engineered features and metadata that both models can access.

**Preprocessing fit policy:**
All transformers (tokenizers, scalers, encoders, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set (quarters 1–12) and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents target leakage and ensures validation and test metrics reflect real out-of-sample performance.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:**
A pre-trained, domain-agnostic BERT-like transformer (e.g., distilBERT or RoBERTa) is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. A custom pooling head aggregates the [CLS] token and feeds it through a 2-layer feed-forward classifier (768 → 256 → 1 logit). Training runs for up to 10 epochs with early stopping on validation PR-AUC (patience: 2 epochs). Hyperparameters: learning rate 2e-5, batch size 32, dropout 0.1, Adam optimizer.

**Gradient-boosted baseline:**
An XGBoost or LightGBM model is trained on hand-engineered features only. To ensure fair comparison, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: grid search over tree depth (4–12), learning rate (0.01–0.1), and L2 regularization (0.1–10). Tuning is performed on the validation set, selecting the hyperparameter configuration that maximizes validation PR-AUC. The baseline is intentionally feature-restricted—no raw text input—to isolate the hypothesis: the claim is that the transformer's learned text representations provide an advantage. If both models received identical features, we could not distinguish the value of learned text from the value of metadata. By giving the baseline all available metadata but denying it raw text, we directly test whether transformer text encoding improves performance.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the test set (quarter 16).**

Justification: Security teams operate at low false-positive rates (auto-quarantine thresholds typically catch 50–90% of threats while blocking <1–5% of legitimate mail). Under extreme class imbalance, ROC-AUC is insensitive to FPR changes and does not reflect the operating regime of interest. PR-AUC directly measures the precision-recall tradeoff at these low-FPR operating points and is the standard metric for imbalanced binary classification in the security domain. A higher PR-AUC indicates the model assigns higher confidence to malicious emails, enabling operators to tune thresholds to their FP tolerance.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision at 90% recall (P@R=0.9): if we catch 90% of threats, what fraction of quarantined emails are true positives?
- Recall at 1% FPR (R@FPR=0.01): if we accept blocking 1% of legitimate mail, how many threats do we catch?
- F1 score at the precision-recall breakeven point: summary metric for balanced performance.
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE): phishing scores must be well-calibrated so operators can interpret confidence; miscalibration indicates the model is overconfident or underconfident, independent of discrimination power.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Both models are tuned on the validation set (quarters 13–15) using PR-AUC as the selection criterion. Hyperparameter grids are evaluated end-to-end on the validation set; the configuration with the highest validation PR-AUC is selected. The test set is never touched during tuning or model selection. After model selection is complete, both models are evaluated on the test set (quarter 16) using all pre-selected metrics. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (stratified bootstrap over 1000 resamples, stratifying by campaign or day to respect temporal autocorrelation).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

**Metadata vs. text signals:** Both models receive identical metadata (sender reputation, domain age, attachment type). The transformer's potential advantage must come from raw text. If the transformer's gain is driven by metadata rather than text understanding, the baseline—which also has metadata—should capture most of it. An ablation study compares transformer performance with and without metadata; if performance drops equally for both models when metadata is removed, metadata is not the driver.

**Sender/domain memorization:** We compute feature importance (via SHAP or attention visualization) and report the fraction of model decisions driven by sender/domain tokens vs. body/subject tokens. If sender tokens dominate, the transformer is memorizing blacklist-like patterns rather than learning phishing language.

**Calibration vs. discrimination:** We separately report calibration error (ECE) and PR-AUC to distinguish calibration improvements from discrimination improvements.

**Temporal robustness:** We compute PR-AUC separately for each week of the test quarter. If the transformer's advantage decays over time, the model is overfitting to validation-set artifacts rather than generalizing.

**Label noise sensitivity:** We retrain both models on a subset of training data filtered to high-confidence labels only (senior analyst reviews or high consensus). If the transformer's advantage persists in low-noise data, it is robust to reviewer disagreement.

**What this experiment establishes:** Whether a transformer-based model that jointly encodes email text and metadata outperforms a hand-engineered gradient-boosted baseline on a temporally held-out test set drawn from future quarters, when both models receive equivalent tuning effort and the same metadata features.

**What this experiment does not establish:** Causal mechanisms (why the transformer is better, if it is); robustness to different attack domains (e.g., spear-phishing vs. commodity phishing); performance on other email security vendors' data; or optimal threshold selection for production deployment (that is a separate business decision).

hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction in chronic heart failure patients, a temporal sequence model over the full event history (admissions, labs, medications, procedures) will outperform a static tabular baseline built from the most recent encounter summary in predicting 30-day readmission risk.
domain: Hospital clinical operations using electronic health records for chronic heart failure management
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Sequence Modeling for Hospital Readmission Prediction in Heart Failure

### 1. Data Source and Inclusion Criteria

This experiment uses 36 months of longitudinal electronic health records from a single hospital system, spanning approximately 8,000–12,000 heart failure admissions from 4,000–6,000 unique patients. Inclusion criteria are: (1) primary or secondary ICD-10 diagnosis of chronic heart failure (I50.x), (2) age ≥18 at admission, (3) complete discharge summary, and (4) at least 2 prior admissions in the system (to ensure meaningful historical context for the sequence model). Patients with comfort care or palliative discharge dispositions are excluded, as readmission prediction is not operationally relevant for this group. The outcome—30-day readmission—is defined as any unplanned admission to an acute care bed occurring between 1 and 30 days after discharge, regardless of primary reason. Readmission status is extracted from the hospital's administrative claims and verified against EHR discharge records.

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal + Patient Stratification

The core design challenge is to avoid both temporal leakage (future information in training) and patient leakage (information from the same individual across splits). We employ a temporal + patient-stratified split: all admissions are sorted by discharge date. Months 1–24 form the training set (60% of admissions), months 25–30 form the validation set (20%), and months 31–36 form the test set (20%). Critically, patients are assigned in full to a single split based on their earliest discharge date within the dataset. Any patient with admissions spanning multiple calendar periods is assigned entirely to the training split. This ensures a patient's entire longitudinal history is used either for model development or evaluation, never both. This split method is essential because readmission prediction is inherently a sequential problem: we use past information (admissions, labs, medications) to predict future readmission risk, and patient identity and temporal order must not be allowed to leak between splits.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, encoding) are fit solely on the training set and applied unchanged to validation and test sets. This includes mean/median imputation for missing lab values, standardization of numeric features, and vocabulary-based encoding of procedure and medication classes.

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline (Static Tabular):** Extracted from the index admission and the most recent prior admission only. Features include: age, sex, Charlson comorbidity index, admission diagnoses, discharge disposition, lab values at admission and discharge (ejection fraction, creatinine, BNP, hemoglobin), vital signs at discharge, length of stay, count of discharge medications, and medication class presence (beta blockers, ACE inhibitors, diuretics). This set explicitly excludes temporal structure and represents the conventional approach to readmission prediction.

**Experimental (Temporal Sequence):** All baseline features, plus full event history from the preceding 24 months: chronologically ordered admissions with reasons and LOS, complete lab trajectories with timestamps and missingness flags, medication orders with start/stop dates, procedures with timestamps, and temporal statistics (creatinine trend, BNP volatility, admission frequency in the past 6 months). Timestamps are preserved; no post-discharge information is included.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The experimental model is a recurrent neural network (GRU or LSTM) with multi-head attention, designed to ingest irregular time series data (labs, events, medications) aligned by timestamp. The architecture includes embedding layers for categorical events, GRU cells over the time dimension, attention pooling, and two fully connected layers with dropout. Hyperparameters (hidden size, attention heads, dropout) are tuned on validation data to maximize the primary metric.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the static feature set with L2 regularization tuned via grid search on validation data using the same primary metric. This ensures fair comparison: both models receive equivalent tuning effort, the same feature engineering pipeline (excluding only temporal structure), and evaluation under identical conditions. A secondary baseline (gradient boosted trees on the static feature set) is also trained to verify that any improvement is not an artifact of RNN overfitting.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Recall at a fixed precision of 85%. This reflects the operational goal: identify high-risk patients for resource-intensive care management such that the intervention cohort is sufficiently enriched (85% precision ensures most interventions reach true high-risk patients) while capturing as many preventable readmissions as possible (recall). This avoids abstract metrics like AUC and directly mirrors deployment constraints.

**Secondary Metrics:** AUC-ROC (ranking quality), precision-recall curve (threshold trade-off), calibration metrics (expected calibration error, Brier score), and stratified sensitivity/specificity and PPV at the top-k patients the care team can manage.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use the validation set (months 25–30) only. Candidate models are evaluated on validation data using the primary metric (recall at 85% precision). The best model is selected based on validation performance. The test set is held out completely and accessed only once, at the end, to report final metrics with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four major confounds are addressed: (1) **Severity:** The baseline feature set includes Charlson index, BNP, EF, and creatinine—capturing unmeasured severity differences between models. Stratified analysis by severity tertile verifies the RNN's advantage is not driven by capturing sicker patients. (2) **Documentation intensity:** Checked via temporal trend analysis across the three calendar periods; shifts in documentation practice would be reported. (3) **Prior history availability:** The temporal split guarantees equivalent history windows across validation and test patients, eliminating confounding by variable documentation lookback. (4) **Discharge disposition:** Included as a feature and analyzed in stratified comparisons. Additional held-out analysis partitions the test set by prior utilization quartiles to confirm the RNN generalizes across patient subtypes and is not merely overfitting to well-documented high-utilizers.

### What This Design Establishes and Does Not

If the RNN achieves materially higher recall at 85% precision than the baseline and secondary models on the test set, and if stratified analyses show consistent improvement across severity levels and utilization patterns, the experiment establishes that temporal sequence modeling captures predictive signal beyond static features—likely by learning dynamic patterns in disease progression, medication response, or admission-to-admission changes. It does not establish causality or identify which specific temporal patterns drive the improvement; that would require additional interpretability analysis. The results generalize to 30-day readmission prediction in the source hospital system but may not transfer to other systems without validation due to differences in EHR infrastructure and care practices.
sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Sequence Modeling for Hospital Readmission Prediction in Heart Failure

### 1. Data Source and Inclusion Criteria

This experiment uses 36 months of longitudinal electronic health records from a single hospital system, spanning approximately 8,000–12,000 heart failure admissions from 4,000–6,000 unique patients. Inclusion criteria are: (1) primary or secondary ICD-10 diagnosis of chronic heart failure (I50.x), (2) age ≥18 at admission, (3) complete discharge summary, and (4) at least 2 prior admissions in the system (to ensure meaningful historical context for the sequence model). Patients with comfort care or palliative discharge dispositions are excluded, as readmission prediction is not operationally relevant for this group. The outcome—30-day readmission—is defined as any unplanned admission to an acute care bed occurring between 1 and 30 days after discharge, regardless of primary reason. Readmission status is extracted from the hospital's administrative claims and verified against EHR discharge records.

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal + Patient Stratification

The core design challenge is to avoid both temporal leakage (future information in training) and patient leakage (information from the same individual across splits). We employ a temporal + patient-stratified split: all admissions are sorted by discharge date. Months 1–24 form the training set (60% of admissions), months 25–30 form the validation set (20%), and months 31–36 form the test set (20%). Critically, patients are assigned in full to a single split based on their earliest discharge date within the dataset. Any patient with admissions spanning multiple calendar periods is assigned entirely to the training split. This ensures a patient's entire longitudinal history is used either for model development or evaluation, never both. This split method is essential because readmission prediction is inherently a sequential problem: we use past information (admissions, labs, medications) to predict future readmission risk, and patient identity and temporal order must not be allowed to leak between splits.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, encoding) are fit solely on the training set and applied unchanged to validation and test sets. This includes mean/median imputation for missing lab values, standardization of numeric features, and vocabulary-based encoding of procedure and medication classes.

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Baseline (Static Tabular):** Extracted from the index admission and the most recent prior admission only. Features include: age, sex, Charlson comorbidity index, admission diagnoses, discharge disposition, lab values at admission and discharge (ejection fraction, creatinine, BNP, hemoglobin), vital signs at discharge, length of stay, count of discharge medications, and medication class presence (beta blockers, ACE inhibitors, diuretics). This set explicitly excludes temporal structure and represents the conventional approach to readmission prediction.

**Experimental (Temporal Sequence):** All baseline features, plus full event history from the preceding 24 months: chronologically ordered admissions with reasons and LOS, complete lab trajectories with timestamps and missingness flags, medication orders with start/stop dates, procedures with timestamps, and temporal statistics (creatinine trend, BNP volatility, admission frequency in the past 6 months). Timestamps are preserved; no post-discharge information is included.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The experimental model is a recurrent neural network (GRU or LSTM) with multi-head attention, designed to ingest irregular time series data (labs, events, medications) aligned by timestamp. The architecture includes embedding layers for categorical events, GRU cells over the time dimension, attention pooling, and two fully connected layers with dropout. Hyperparameters (hidden size, attention heads, dropout) are tuned on validation data to maximize the primary metric.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the static feature set with L2 regularization tuned via grid search on validation data using the same primary metric. This ensures fair comparison: both models receive equivalent tuning effort, the same feature engineering pipeline (excluding only temporal structure), and evaluation under identical conditions. A secondary baseline (gradient boosted trees on the static feature set) is also trained to verify that any improvement is not an artifact of RNN overfitting.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Recall at a fixed precision of 85%. This reflects the operational goal: identify high-risk patients for resource-intensive care management such that the intervention cohort is sufficiently enriched (85% precision ensures most interventions reach true high-risk patients) while capturing as many preventable readmissions as possible (recall). This avoids abstract metrics like AUC and directly mirrors deployment constraints.

**Secondary Metrics:** AUC-ROC (ranking quality), precision-recall curve (threshold trade-off), calibration metrics (expected calibration error, Brier score), and stratified sensitivity/specificity and PPV at the top-k patients the care team can manage.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use the validation set (months 25–30) only. Candidate models are evaluated on validation data using the primary metric (recall at 85% precision). The best model is selected based on validation performance. The test set is held out completely and accessed only once, at the end, to report final metrics with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four major confounds are addressed: (1) **Severity:** The baseline feature set includes Charlson index, BNP, EF, and creatinine—capturing unmeasured severity differences between models. Stratified analysis by severity tertile verifies the RNN's advantage is not driven by capturing sicker patients. (2) **Documentation intensity:** Checked via temporal trend analysis across the three calendar periods; shifts in documentation practice would be reported. (3) **Prior history availability:** The temporal split guarantees equivalent history windows across validation and test patients, eliminating confounding by variable documentation lookback. (4) **Discharge disposition:** Included as a feature and analyzed in stratified comparisons. Additional held-out analysis partitions the test set by prior utilization quartiles to confirm the RNN generalizes across patient subtypes and is not merely overfitting to well-documented high-utilizers.

### What This Design Establishes and Does Not

If the RNN achieves materially higher recall at 85% precision than the baseline and secondary models on the test set, and if stratified analyses show consistent improvement across severity levels and utilization patterns, the experiment establishes that temporal sequence modeling captures predictive signal beyond static features—likely by learning dynamic patterns in disease progression, medication response, or admission-to-admission changes. It does not establish causality or identify which specific temporal patterns drive the improvement; that would require additional interpretability analysis. The results generalize to 30-day readmission prediction in the source hospital system but may not transfer to other systems without validation due to differences in EHR infrastructure and care practices.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranker using query text plus product title, attributes, and click history will outperform a BM25-only baseline for e-commerce product search relevance, measured by nDCG@10 on chronologically held-out traffic.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search for a large catalog retailer with user query logs and product metadata
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ranker vs. BM25 Baseline for E-Commerce Search

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

We would use 24 months of production search session data from the marketplace, spanning months 1–24. Each session records a user's query string, the ranked list of products shown (impressions), click and add-to-cart events with precise timestamps, and product metadata including title, category, brand, price, and structured attributes. We retain only sessions with at least 2 impressions to ensure meaningful relevance judgments. Data quality checks include removing sessions with malformed queries, and filtering out bot traffic using standard heuristics (e.g., >10 queries per second from a single IP). We acknowledge that implicit feedback is noisy and position-biased—clicks reflect both relevance and presentation—so we use a conservative labeling scheme: explicit clicks receive relevance score 1, non-clicks on top-5 impressions receive 0, and impressions below rank 5 are excluded from training pairs to avoid the most egregious position bias.

### 2. Label Construction

Relevance labels are aggregated at the query level over the training window using position-aware weighting. For each unique query, all sessions containing that query contribute click and non-click observations. Recent interactions are weighted more heavily (exponential decay, 7-day half-life) to reflect evolving relevance as catalog and demand shift. This temporal weighting mitigates the impact of stale relevance judgments while preserving signal.

### 3. Train–Validation–Test Split

Data is partitioned chronologically: months 1–12 (training), months 13–15 (validation), months 16–18 (test). Temporal splitting is essential because e-commerce search exhibits non-stationarity: catalog inventory fluctuates, promotions start and end, seasonal demand varies, and product popularity shifts. A random split would allow the model to observe future patterns (e.g., next month's seasonal peak) during training, inflating performance estimates. Chronological ordering ensures the model is evaluated on its ability to generalize forward, matching real deployment where the ranker must handle unseen future sessions. No query-level or user-level stratification is applied; we evaluate on the natural distribution of traffic.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only. For both models:
- Query and product title are tokenized (lowercased, stopwords removed, special characters normalized).
- Vocabulary is built from the training corpus; out-of-vocabulary terms in validation/test are mapped to an OOV token.
- For the transformer ranker, additional features are engineered: product category and brand are mapped to learned embeddings (vocabulary and embedding matrix fit on training product catalog); numerical features (log-scaled historical click frequency, normalized price, days since product added to catalog) are scaled to zero mean and unit variance using training set statistics.
- For BM25, only query and title are used; no additional features are included. This is intentional—BM25 is a feature-light baseline.
- Both models process the same raw query and product text, ensuring fair comparison on the transformation step.

### 5. Model Architecture

The transformer ranker encodes query and product title using a shared transformer encoder (12 layers, 8 attention heads, 768 hidden dimensions). Encoded query and title representations are concatenated with structured attributes (category and brand embeddings; numerical features). This concatenation flows through a 2-layer feed-forward network (512 hidden units, ReLU; 128 hidden units, ReLU; 1 output) to produce a single relevance score per query-item pair. The model is trained using pairwise ranking loss (RankNet or LambdaRank) on session-level pairs: for each session, a clicked item is ranked higher than an unclicked item, and the model learns to maximize nDCG@10. The BM25 baseline scores each query-item pair using the standard probabilistic formula, with hyperparameters k1 and b tuned on the validation set.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

For the transformer: training proceeds on the training set with validation monitoring. Early stopping is applied if validation nDCG@10 does not improve for 5 consecutive epochs. The checkpoint achieving the highest validation nDCG@10 is selected. For BM25: a grid search over k1 ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0} and b ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} is performed, evaluating nDCG@10 on the validation set. The hyperparameter pair maximizing validation nDCG@10 is retained. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated on identical validation data.

### 7. Primary and Secondary Metrics

The primary metric is nDCG@10, computed as the sum of discounted gains at ranks 1–10 divided by the ideal DCG (the DCG of a perfect ranking). nDCG@10 directly aligns with the business goal: products users want should appear in the top 10 search results. The discount (log rank) emphasizes top positions, where most user attention is concentrated. Secondary metrics include:
- nDCG@1 and nDCG@5: measure shallow-ranking quality, ensuring the model places the best result first.
- Recall@10: ensures the ranker does not sacrifice coverage for precision.
- MRR: measures time-to-first-click, relevant if immediate relevance is critical.
- Per-segment nDCG@10: stratified by query frequency (head/torso/tail) and item freshness (new/medium/old) to detect whether gains are driven by semantic understanding or by popularity/freshness exploitation.

### 8. Evaluation on Test Set

After model selection, both the transformer and tuned BM25 are applied to the test set (months 16–18) exactly once. Metrics are computed; no further tuning or re-training occurs. The test set is held completely separate from all hyperparameter optimization to provide an honest generalization estimate.

### 9. Controls and Interpretation Scope

To address the known confound—that gains could reflect exploitation of popularity or freshness rather than semantic query-item understanding—we examine nDCG@10 stratified by query frequency and item age. Balanced improvements across all segments would strengthen the semantic understanding claim. Conversely, concentrated gains on head queries or new items would suggest the transformer is learning shallow signals rather than deeper relevance patterns. We also compute feature importance (gradient-based attribution) to verify that learned query-title semantic alignment meaningfully contributes to predictions. This experiment would establish whether the transformer ranker outperforms BM25 on forward-looking e-commerce search traffic under realistic temporal distribution shift. It would not establish causality of individual mechanisms (e.g., attention weights) or performance on other e-commerce sites; those would require additional focused studies.
sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Ranker vs. BM25 Baseline for E-Commerce Search

### 1. Input Data and Preprocessing

The experiment uses 24 months of production search data (months 1–24), including user queries, impression lists, click events, product metadata, and historical interaction statistics. Sessions are filtered to those with ≥2 impressions to ensure meaningful relevance judgments. Bot traffic is removed using heuristics; malformed queries are excluded. Preprocessing (tokenization, stopwords, normalization) is applied consistently to query and product text. All transformations (vocabulary, scaling, embeddings) are fit on training data only (months 1–12) to prevent leakage.

### 2. Label Strategy

Relevance is defined via conservative, position-aware implicit feedback: clicks = 1, top-5 non-clicks = 0, ranks 6+ excluded. Labels are aggregated at the query level with exponential temporal weighting (7-day half-life) to reflect evolving relevance.

### 3. Split Strategy

Temporal split: months 1–12 (train), 13–15 (validate), 16–18 (test). This prevents leakage in non-stationary settings (seasonality, catalog changes). No stratification is applied to maintain natural data distribution.

### 4. Features

Transformer model: query/product text, structured product attributes (category, brand, price), log-scaled historical click frequency, and freshness (days since catalog addition). BM25 baseline: query/product text only.

### 5. Model Architecture

Transformer encoder (12 layers, 8 heads) processes query/title, then concatenated with structured features. FFN (512→128→1) produces relevance scores. RankNet/LambdaRank optimizes nDCG@10. BM25 uses standard probabilistic formula with parameter tuning.

### 6. Metric Strategy

Primary: nDCG@10. Secondary: nDCG@1,5, Recall@10, MRR, and per-segment nDCG@10 (query frequency, item age). Stratification verifies gains reflect semantic understanding, not popularity/freshness exploitation.

### 7. Evaluation Protocol

Validation set tunes hyperparameters. Test set is evaluated exactly once. No retraining post-validation. Model comparison uses same metric on same data.

### 8. Confound Controls

nDCG@10 is stratified by query frequency and item age. Feature attribution analysis confirms query-title alignment drives predictions.

hypothesis: A transformer-based fraud model that ingests raw transaction sequences and device/session context will detect card-not-present fraud better than a gradient-boosted model trained on hand-engineered aggregate account features, as measured by out-of-time precision-recall performance at a fixed false-positive rate.
domain: Credit card fraud detection for a large consumer bank's card-not-present transaction stream
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Card-Not-Present Fraud Detection

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
The goal is to test whether a transformer-based model ingesting raw transaction sequences and contextual features outperforms a gradient-boosted baseline trained on hand-engineered aggregate features, specifically on precision-recall performance at a fixed operational false-positive rate. Success means the transformer achieves higher fraud recall at the same review budget, enabling the bank to catch more fraud without increasing manual review load.

### 2. Data Source and Label Definition
The experiment uses 24 months of production card-not-present transactions from the bank's core system, spanning ~50M+ transactions. The label is binary fraud derived from chargeback filings: a transaction is fraudulent if a chargeback was filed and sustained within 120 days; otherwise it is labeled legitimate. Transactions within the 120-day chargeback window at study end are excluded to avoid censored labels. This 120-day window aligns with card network chargeback periods and reflects true operational ground truth.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
All data are split chronologically to reflect real deployment: **Train (months 1–12):** ~25M transactions used for model training. **Validation (months 13–16):** ~10M transactions used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection via grid/random search. **Test (months 17–24):** ~15M transactions, held-out and untouched during development, evaluated once at the end. This temporal structure prevents leakage from future fraud patterns and accommodates label delay. No random shuffling is applied; order is preserved to enable sequence-based feature engineering. Fraud rates and feature distributions are verified across all three sets to detect unexpected shifts.

### 4. Feature Engineering
Two distinct feature sets are engineered from the same raw data:

**Set A (Transformer input):** Raw transaction sequences per account (last 10–30 transactions, chronologically ordered, padded uniformly), transaction amounts, merchant categories, timestamps (encoded as time-of-day, day-of-week, elapsed time since prior transaction), hashed device identifiers, IP-inferred geolocation, session duration, and account tenure. All features use only information available at authorization time; no post-transaction signals are included.

**Set B (Baseline input):** Hand-engineered aggregate features computed over rolling windows (7-day, 30-day): transaction counts, total amounts, merchant category diversity, device diversity, transaction frequency, geolocation change flags, account age, and prior fraud rate. These aggregates are derived from the same raw data and time-aligned to the baseline's authorization-time information.

All preprocessing (scaling, categorical encoding, missing value imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets.

### 5. Models and Baseline
**Transformer model:** A sequence-to-classification architecture (e.g., encoder-only transformer or GPT-style) that ingests padded transaction sequences and contextual embeddings, with a classification head outputting fraud probability. Training uses weighted cross-entropy or focal loss to handle class imbalance. Hyperparameters (layers, attention heads, sequence length, dropout, learning rate) are tuned on the validation set via 50-trial grid/random search.

**Baseline (XGBoost or LightGBM):** Gradient-boosted trees trained on hand-engineered aggregate features (set B). Hyperparameters (learning rate, depth, estimators, regularization) are tuned with equal effort (50 trials, same validation set) to ensure a fair comparison. Both models use class weighting to handle imbalance.

### 6. Primary Metric and Evaluation
**Primary metric:** Precision-Recall AUC at a fixed false-positive rate (FPR) of 2–5%. This metric directly answers the business question: "At our operational review budget (flagging 2–5% of transactions), how many frauds does each model catch?" The threshold is operationally defined by manual review and decline capacity. Recall at this fixed FPR is the interpretable success measure: a higher recall indicates better fraud capture without exceeding review load.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall and precision at fixed FPR, ROC-AUC, calibration metrics (Brier score, ECE), stratified performance by transaction type, and out-of-time degradation (comparing months 17–20 vs. 21–24).

### 7. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set (months 13–16) for both models. Early stopping and metric monitoring occur on this set. The test set is never touched during tuning. After training is complete, the final models are evaluated on the test set once, and all reported metrics come from this held-out evaluation.

### 8. Confound Controls
**Feature timing audit:** Both models are restricted to features available at authorization time. A document lists the computation time and source of every feature to prevent post-transaction leakage.

**Baseline fairness:** The baseline receives the same raw data and time window access as the transformer; aggregates are computed fairly without artificial handicapping.

**Feedback loop mitigation:** Transactions declined by pre-existing fraud rules are either excluded or flagged in both datasets to prevent the model from learning spurious rule correlations.

**Temporal stability:** Fraud rate, feature distributions, and performance are computed per time window to detect distribution shifts and validate model assumptions.

### 9. Scope of Conclusions
This design establishes whether the transformer architecture outperforms gradient-boosted aggregates on this specific task and data period. It does not establish whether the gain is due to sequence modeling, architectural choice, or simply more complex feature learning; ablation studies (e.g., gradient boosted trees on raw sequence features, or transformer on aggregates) would be needed to isolate the mechanism. Results generalize to the 24-month period studied; longer-term generalization requires additional out-of-time evaluation on future months.
