hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that uses the full alert chain and incident-response context will rank true high-priority incidents above routine alerts better than a binary classifier trained on the same features when evaluated on held-out future weeks of analyst-reviewed alerts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would frame the experiment around the actual SOC decision point: an alert arrives, the system has access to alert-native fields plus contemporaneous context, and the goal is to order the queue so analysts see the most dangerous work first. Use 18 months of SIEM/EDR alerts linked to case-management tickets, enrichment records, and asset/user metadata. Every example is anchored at alert creation time, and any field first populated after analyst action begins is excluded. That includes containment actions, analyst free text, final ticket severity fields, and late enrichment artifacts that would reveal the investigation outcome.

Construct alert-level examples because the operational queue is alert driven, but derive graded relevance from the eventual incident outcome. Alerts tied to confirmed malicious incidents with final high or critical severity receive the highest gain, escalated but lower-severity cases receive intermediate gain, and routine or benign alerts receive zero. Keep duplicate alerts as separate queue items because they compete for analyst attention in production, and add a secondary incident-deduplicated analysis to ensure results are not just driven by repeated copies of the same event chain.

Split data chronologically by week: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right deployment-faithful split for a SOC setting because both attacker behavior and internal detection content evolve over time, and later weeks contain enrichment patterns and workflow conventions that should not inform model development. Fit all preprocessors on training only. For categorical fields, collapse rare levels based on training frequency; for numeric fields, impute and scale from training statistics only; for any text-derived detector metadata, build the vocabulary on training only.

The treatment model is a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank system trained with groupwise ranking by analyst queue week and optimized on graded relevance. Grouping by week reflects how analysts consume a rolling queue and prevents the model from learning a ranking objective over arbitrary global mixes of alerts that never co-occur operationally. The baseline is deliberately strong: a gradient-boosted binary classifier using the same features, same preprocessing, and a comparable hyperparameter search budget. Its output probabilities are used to produce a ranked list, so the comparison isolates whether direct ranking optimization adds value beyond standard classification on identical information.

Select hyperparameters on the validation block using NDCG@20, since the practical objective is concentrated in the top of the queue and severity is graded, not binary. Report MRR, Recall@20, AUROC, and incident-level top-20 recall as secondary metrics. After model choices are frozen, evaluate once on the held-out final 3 months and report weekly bootstrap confidence intervals.

To control confounds, keep feature availability identical across models, include rule identifiers and asset criticality explicitly, and stratify results by month, rule family, and business unit. This design will establish whether ranking optimization improves priority ordering under realistic future-week deployment conditions; it will not by itself prove robustness to major policy changes or brand-new detection content never observed in the training horizon.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the operational threshold K as the median daily number of alerts investigated to first disposition during the test period. Evaluate both models' precision@K and recall@K at this threshold, where K is rounded to the nearest integer. If the learning-to-rank model's precision@K value is more than 5 percentage points lower than the binary classifier's precision@K value while achieving higher NDCG@20, NDCG@20 alone is insufficient as the primary decision metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The learning-to-rank model's precision@K value at the operational threshold is more than 5 percentage points lower than the binary classifier's value
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The learning-to-rank model's precision@K value at the operational threshold is within 5 percentage points or higher than the binary classifier's value
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The learning-to-rank model's precision@K value at the operational threshold is exactly 5 percentage points lower than the binary classifier's value

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the operational threshold K as the median daily number of alerts investigated to first disposition during the test period. Evaluate both models' precision@K and recall@K at this threshold, where K is rounded to the nearest integer. If the learning-to-rank model's precision@K value is more than 5 percentage points lower than the binary classifier's precision@K value while achieving higher NDCG@20, NDCG@20 alone is insufficient as the primary decision metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The learning-to-rank model's precision@K value at the operational threshold is more than 5 percentage points lower than the binary classifier's value
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The learning-to-rank model's precision@K value at the operational threshold is within 5 percentage points or higher than the binary classifier's value
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The learning-to-rank model's precision@K value at the operational threshold is exactly 5 percentage points lower than the binary classifier's value

sound_design_reference:
You would frame the experiment around the actual SOC decision point: an alert arrives, the system has access to alert-native fields plus contemporaneous context, and the goal is to order the queue so analysts see the most dangerous work first. Use 18 months of SIEM/EDR alerts linked to case-management tickets, enrichment records, and asset/user metadata. Every example is anchored at alert creation time, and any field first populated after analyst action begins is excluded. That includes containment actions, analyst free text, final ticket severity fields, and late enrichment artifacts that would reveal the investigation outcome.

Construct alert-level examples because the operational queue is alert driven, but derive graded relevance from the eventual incident outcome. Alerts tied to confirmed malicious incidents with final high or critical severity receive the highest gain, escalated but lower-severity cases receive intermediate gain, and routine or benign alerts receive zero. Keep duplicate alerts as separate queue items because they compete for analyst attention in production, and add a secondary incident-deduplicated analysis to ensure results are not just driven by repeated copies of the same event chain.

Split data chronologically by week: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right deployment-faithful split for a SOC setting because both attacker behavior and internal detection content evolve over time, and later weeks contain enrichment patterns and workflow conventions that should not inform model development. Fit all preprocessors on training only. For categorical fields, collapse rare levels based on training frequency; for numeric fields, impute and scale from training statistics only; for any text-derived detector metadata, build the vocabulary on training only.

The treatment model is a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank system trained with groupwise ranking by analyst queue week and optimized on graded relevance. Grouping by week reflects how analysts consume a rolling queue and prevents the model from learning a ranking objective over arbitrary global mixes of alerts that never co-occur operationally. The baseline is deliberately strong: a gradient-boosted binary classifier using the same features, same preprocessing, and a comparable hyperparameter search budget. Its output probabilities are used to produce a ranked list, so the comparison isolates whether direct ranking optimization adds value beyond standard classification on identical information.

Select hyperparameters on the validation block using NDCG@20, since the practical objective is concentrated in the top of the queue and severity is graded, not binary. Report MRR, Recall@20, AUROC, and incident-level top-20 recall as secondary metrics. After model choices are frozen, evaluate once on the held-out final 3 months and report weekly bootstrap confidence intervals.

To control confounds, keep feature availability identical across models, include rule identifiers and asset criticality explicitly, and stratify results by month, rule family, and business unit. This design will establish whether ranking optimization improves priority ordering under realistic future-week deployment conditions; it will not by itself prove robustness to major policy changes or brand-new detection content never observed in the training horizon.

hypothesis: For retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring, a graph-based model that uses account-network relationships and transaction-merchant metadata will detect suspicious retail and small-business accounts better than a transaction-only gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by precision at a fixed investigator-review budget.
domain: Retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring for retail and small-business accounts
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective account-month prediction design that mirrors deployment. For each month in a 36-month historical window, create a scoring snapshot for every active retail or small-business account with at least 60 days of prior activity. The label is whether that account reaches a confirmed suspicious AML outcome within the next 90 days, based on analyst-confirmed cases, SAR filings, or AML-related closures. Because labels are delayed, enforce a 90-day maturation buffer so every example has a fully observed outcome window before it can enter train, validation, or test. Exclude accounts with unresolved investigations at censoring.

Split the data strictly by time: 24 months train, 6 months validation, 6 months test. This is the right choice for AML because typologies, investigator behavior, and customer mix drift over time, and any design that mixes future periods into training would overstate real monitoring performance. Leakage control is central: all rolling aggregates are computed from events observed before the monthly scoring cutoff, graph edges are built only from pre-cutoff transactions and relationships, and no case outcomes, SAR text, closure reasons, or post-investigation artifacts are allowed into features.

Train two systems. The baseline is a tuned gradient-boosted tree on transaction and profile features only: transaction velocity, amount bands, cash intensity, merchant-category mix, inbound/outbound asymmetry, counterparty concentration, reversal patterns, account age, geography mismatch, and prior alert counts known at the scoring time. The proposed model is a heterogeneous graph neural network over accounts, merchants, and counterparties, using temporally filtered edges from transfers, card payments, and repeated transactional ties. In addition to the tabular signals, it learns from neighborhood structure, shared-counterparty motifs, merchant-neighborhood risk concentration, and ego-network flow imbalance.

Select hyperparameters on the validation window only, using mean monthly precision at the top-K ranked accounts, where K is the fixed investigator review budget per month. This is the primary metric because the operational decision is not to classify every account at a threshold; it is to fill a limited analyst queue with the most suspicious candidates. A model is useful if it produces more confirmed suspicious accounts within that queue. Report recall at top-K, PR-AUC, and alerts-per-hit as secondary metrics, plus segment-level results for retail and small-business accounts.

To control confounds, keep feature availability matched at the scoring timestamp, and run an ablation that removes merchant metadata from the graph model to isolate the value of network structure itself. Also report performance by month, account tenure, and customer segment to check whether gains are concentrated in one subpopulation or vanish under drift. This experiment will establish whether relational modeling improves investigator-yield under a fixed review budget relative to a strong transaction-only baseline; it will not establish causal deterrence effects or completeness of AML labels.

required_empirical_test.condition: On the validation data, compute precision at both the production alert threshold and the top-K cutoff for the graph model and baseline. If the absolute difference in graph-baseline precision gaps between (a) threshold-based and (b) top-K-based evaluation exceeds 3 percentage points, then precision at top-K alone is insufficient.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If precision comparison at the alert threshold yields a graph-baseline gap that is more than 3 percentage points higher or lower than their precision-at-top-K gap
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the precision-gap difference between threshold-based and top-K-based evaluation is within ±3 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: On the validation data, compute precision at both the production alert threshold and the top-K cutoff for the graph model and baseline. If the absolute difference in graph-baseline precision gaps between (a) threshold-based and (b) top-K-based evaluation exceeds 3 percentage points, then precision at top-K alone is insufficient.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If precision comparison at the alert threshold yields a graph-baseline gap that is more than 3 percentage points higher or lower than their precision-at-top-K gap
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the precision-gap difference between threshold-based and top-K-based evaluation is within ±3 percentage points

hypothesis: For hospital emergency department triage, a gradient-boosted model using temporal event history and time-of-arrival features will outperform a static tabular baseline that only uses the latest triage snapshot in predicting 24-hour admission or ICU transfer, especially for patients presenting during overnight shifts.
domain: Healthcare / emergency medicine using emergency department triage records and revisit outcomes
task_prompt: Use four years of ED encounters from the hospital network, linking triage records to ADT and bed-management outcomes. Define the prediction time as completion of the initial triage assessment... [rest of design_narrative]

required_empirical_test.condition: Compare PPV at the operational decision threshold between temporal-history and static models on an independent, held-out operational simulation set with fixed review thresholds or top-k queue fractions. Compute the absolute difference in PPV at the decision point used operationally.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: PPV deficit exceeds 0.05 absolute at the operational threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: PPV deficit ≤ 0.05 absolute at the operational threshold
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: PPV deficit exactly 0.05 or insufficient overlap in high-risk regions to determine

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compare PPV at the operational decision threshold between temporal-history and static models on an independent, held-out operational simulation set with fixed review thresholds or top-k queue fractions. Compute the absolute difference in PPV at the decision point used operationally.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: PPV deficit exceeds 0.05 absolute at the operational threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: PPV deficit ≤ 0.05 absolute at the operational threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: PPV deficit exactly 0.05 or insufficient overlap in high-risk regions to determine

sound_design_reference: Use four years of ED encounters from the hospital network, linking triage records to ADT and bed-management outcomes...

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on short windows of network flow and endpoint telemetry will detect novel compromise activity earlier and with higher AUROC than a static feature-based gradient-boosted baseline trained on the same events.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of enterprise SIEM/EDR telemetry covering network flows, endpoint process activity, authentication logs, DNS/proxy summaries, and incident-response case outcomes. The prediction unit should be a host-session telemetry window ending before any analyst escalation or containment action. That anchor matters: the main confound in this setting is that post-detection artifacts can make a model look strong while only learning the security team’s response trail. Positive labels come from confirmed compromises in incident closures and post-incident investigations. Negative windows are drawn from assets with no linked incident and no containment activity during a 30-day follow-up period. Weakly adjudicated cases can be used for training under a lower-confidence rule, but they should be excluded from validation and test to keep model comparison tied to cleaner ground truth.

Construct train, validation, and test partitions with a stratified random split at the asset level, preserving prevalence and major asset classes. Grouping by asset prevents the same host from leaking repeated behavioral fingerprints across partitions. In this experiment, the purpose of the split is to compare whether ordered short-horizon behavior carries additional detection signal beyond strong static summaries of the same evidence, so keeping platform mix and class balance aligned across partitions gives the cleanest head-to-head comparison.

For features, derive both models from the same raw telemetry windows. The sequence model receives ordered event tokens and numerical side channels capturing process ancestry, authentication bursts, destination rarity, protocol use, user-host novelty, and rate changes through the window. The static baseline receives aggregated versions of those same signals: counts, histograms, rarity scores, recency summaries, and host/user context features. Any analyst-generated alerts, case references, containment actions, forensic tooling events, or fields populated only after triage are excluded. Preprocessing is fit on training only, including normalization, categorical vocabularies or hashing setup, missingness handling, and sequence length rules.

Train a compact sequence encoder with class-weighted loss and compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline on the static aggregates. The baseline should receive equivalent tuning effort so the comparison isolates temporal ordering rather than optimization budget. Use validation AUROC for model selection and early stopping, then touch the test set once for the final comparison.

The primary metric is AUROC because the SOC consumes scores as a ranked queue under changing staffing and alert-budget conditions; the practical question is whether compromised assets move consistently toward the top of review lists. Secondary metrics should include AUPRC, recall at a fixed daily alert budget, false positives per 1,000 host-sessions, Brier score, and median lead time before first analyst escalation so that any ranking gain is checked against operational burden and earlier detection.

Finally, report subgroup results by business unit, operating system family, and asset criticality, plus a held-out check on newly provisioned assets. This design will establish whether short-window sequence modeling adds real detection value on this enterprise’s telemetry while directly guarding against leakage from analyst response artifacts.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the lag-30-day autocorrelation of the daily positive-label rate and of the daily prevalence of top malicious technique families (mapped from incident cases) over the 18-month corpus. If either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30, rerun the evaluation with a chronological split (first 12 months train, next 3 validation, final 3 test) and measure whether the sequence model’s AUROC advantage over the static baseline decreases by more than 0.03 compared to the stratified split result.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Rerunning with chronological split causes the AUROC advantage of the sequence model over the baseline to decrease by more than 0.03 when either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Either autocorrelation is <=0.30 and no rerun is required, or rerunning with chronological split causes AUROC advantage decrease <=0.03 when autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Either autocorrelation is exactly 0.30 and AUROC advantage change is exactly 0.03, or rerun yields no change in AUROC advantage despite autocorrelation exceeding 0.30

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the lag-30-day autocorrelation of the daily positive-label rate and of the daily prevalence of top malicious technique families (mapped from incident cases) over the 18-month corpus. If either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30, rerun the evaluation with a chronological split (first 12 months train, next 3 validation, final 3 test) and measure whether the sequence model’s AUROC advantage over the static baseline decreases by more than 0.03 compared to the stratified split result.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Rerunning with chronological split causes the AUROC advantage of the sequence model over the baseline to decrease by more than 0.03 when either autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Either autocorrelation is <=0.30 and no rerun is required, or rerunning with chronological split causes AUROC advantage decrease <=0.03 when autocorrelation exceeds 0.30
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Either autocorrelation is exactly 0.30 and AUROC advantage change is exactly 0.03, or rerun yields no change in AUROC advantage despite autocorrelation exceeding 0.30

hypothesis: For enterprise email security, a transformer model fine-tuned on raw message text plus header/URL features will detect phishing and malicious email more accurately than a gradient-boosted model trained only on engineered sender-reputation and metadata features, as measured on a temporally held-out month of inbound mail.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on six consecutive months of inbound mail from the enterprise gateway, using only information available at receipt time. The dataset should include raw subject and body text, MIME structure, headers, authentication results, URLs, attachment metadata, and sender-reputation logs. Labels are built from confirmed phishing and malicious-email outcomes: analyst triage, validated user reports, and incident-response confirmations define positives. Negatives are messages with no malicious disposition after a 21-day observation window and no later incident linkage. Messages with disputed or unresolved outcomes are dropped rather than forced into a noisy class.

Because email threats drift quickly and campaign artifacts recur over short intervals, the split is chronological by receipt month: months 1-4 for training, month 5 for validation, and month 6 for the final held-out test. This preserves realistic future generalization and avoids leakage from campaign timing. Near-identical parser duplicates are deduplicated within each split so the model is not rewarded for memorizing repeated ingestion artifacts, but cross-month recurrence is preserved because that is part of the deployment reality.

The proposed model is a transformer fine-tuned on raw subject and message body text, with structured header, URL, attachment, and reputation features fused through a small classification head. Preprocessing that learns from data—numeric scaling, imputers, frequency encodings, and any learned categorical mappings—is fit on the training period only and then frozen. Rule-based parsing and normalization, such as URL canonicalization and header extraction, are fixed prior to training. Features that can directly encode the label or future actions, such as analyst queue status, quarantine disposition after review, or downstream user-click outcomes, are excluded.

The baseline is a strong gradient-boosted tree model trained on engineered metadata and reputation features only. It receives comparable tuning effort on the validation month so the comparison tests the value of raw-text representation rather than tuning asymmetry. This is the relevant baseline because many operational mail-security stacks rely primarily on sender reputation, authentication, routing, and structural indicators.

Model selection is performed on month 5 using average precision as the primary metric. In this environment, positives are rare and security operations consume a ranked stream of suspicious mail; what matters is how concentrated the true malicious messages are near the top of that ranking so review burden stays low while broad detection remains high. Secondary reporting includes precision at fixed recall, recall at high-precision operating points, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and work saved over sampling.

The test month is touched once after architecture, tuning, and calibration are locked. Results should be reported with both message-level and campaign-cluster bootstrap intervals. To address confounds, include subgroup analyses for novel senders versus previously seen senders, label-source strata, and campaign clusters, plus an ablation that removes raw text from the transformer pathway. This experiment can show whether the multimodal transformer generalizes better on a future month of this organization’s mail stream; it will not by itself prove superiority for every deployment policy or every enterprise environment.

required_empirical_test.condition: On the held-out month-6 test set, measure precision at recall=0.85 for both models. Compute the difference between transformer and gradient-boosted models (transformer_precision - baseline_precision).
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is less than 3 percentage points or negative, indicating the average-precision-optimized model underperforms at the operational recall target.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is ≥3 percentage points, confirming average precision aligns with threshold-specific operational needs.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: On the held-out month-6 test set, measure precision at recall=0.85 for both models. Compute the difference between transformer and gradient-boosted models (transformer_precision - baseline_precision).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is less than 3 percentage points or negative, indicating the average-precision-optimized model underperforms at the operational recall target.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the precision difference (transformer - baseline) is ≥3 percentage points, confirming average precision aligns with threshold-specific operational needs.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a graph neural network built on the entity-relationship graph of users, hosts, IPs, processes, and authentication events will detect malicious activity more accurately than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline trained on the same raw event features, especially when evaluated on future-time windows that contain previously unseen attack campaigns.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
Use 18 months of telemetry from one enterprise environment so the comparison reflects the data generating process the SOC actually faces. Construct host-day examples, because this unit is operationally meaningful for triage and allows both relational and non-relational models to score the same decision target. Labels come from incident-response-confirmed malicious activity: mark a host-day positive when the host falls within seven days of a confirmed malicious interval, using ticket timelines and forensic scoping to align telemetry with the incident. Because security labels are delayed and imperfect, exclude host-days near unresolved investigations and remove analyst-authored fields, ticket identifiers, suppression states, and any post hoc annotations that could leak outcome information.

Split data strictly by time: train on months 1-11, validate on months 12-15, and test on months 16-18. In addition, keep all windows tied to the same incident or campaign in the same split based on first observed malicious activity, so later stages of one intrusion cannot bleed into development while earlier stages appear in evaluation. This is essential here because the hypothesis is explicitly about future-time generalization to unseen campaigns, not retrospective reconstruction of known attacks.

Build two representations from the same underlying event stream. The tabular baseline uses host-day aggregates: authentication anomalies, process rarity, parent-child execution patterns, lateral movement indicators, network connection novelty, destination reputation buckets, privilege changes, and rolling behavioral deltas, joined with asset criticality and identity metadata. The graph model uses those same raw signals as typed node and edge attributes in a heterogeneous graph over users, hosts, IPs, processes, and authentication sessions formed from the prior seven days of telemetry. All preprocessing, vocabularies, normalization statistics, and frequency cutoffs are fit on training only and then frozen.

Train a heterogeneous GNN to classify each host-day, tuning depth, hidden width, neighbor sampling, class weighting, and learning rate on the validation period. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline with matched tuning effort over standard tree hyperparameters and class weighting. This is the appropriate baseline because GBDTs are the strongest conventional choice for mixed enterprise telemetry once relations are flattened; if the GNN wins, the gain is credibly attributable to relational modeling rather than simply using richer metadata.

Use AUPRC as the primary metric. In this setting the SOC reviews a ranked alert queue and daily review capacity changes with staffing, incident load, and business criticality. The experiment asks whether the GNN surfaces true intrusions earlier in the queue across future attack windows, and AUPRC captures concentration of positives over the full ranking under extreme class imbalance better than AUROC.

Also report recall at fixed false-positive rate, precision at top-K hosts per day, AUROC, and Brier score so ranking, low-alert-budget performance, and calibration are all visible. Perform model selection on validation AUPRC with early stopping, freeze all choices, and touch the test set once at the end. Finally, run controls for host class, business unit, and campaign novelty, plus an ablation that removes graph edges while preserving node features to test whether any observed gain comes from structure rather than metadata alone. The experiment establishes whether graph-based relational inductive bias improves forward-looking intrusion detection in this environment; it does not by itself prove transfer to other enterprises with different telemetry coverage or investigation practices.

required_empirical_test.condition: Analyze historical alert volume thresholds across 3 months of production days. For each day, compute the daily alert volume threshold used by analysts to triage hosts. Identify the dominant operational threshold as the median alert volume selected across days where alert volume falls within ±10% of the median. If at least 80% of production days align with this threshold, then: (1) calculate precision, recall, and recall at FPR ≤ 0.001 for both models at this threshold on the test set, (2) determine whether the relative performance difference between GNN and GBM across these metric deltas exceeds 5 percentage points of recall or 10 percentage points of precision compared to their AUPRC-based ordering.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The GNN shows higher AUPRC but has ≥5% lower recall or ≥10% lower precision at the dominant operational threshold compared to GBM
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The GNN maintains ≤5% recall and ≤10% precision differences at the threshold compared to AUPRC-based ranking

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Analyze historical alert volume thresholds across 3 months of production days. For each day, compute the daily alert volume threshold used by analysts to triage hosts. Identify the dominant operational threshold as the median alert volume selected across days where alert volume falls within ±10% of the median. If at least 80% of production days align with this threshold, then: (1) calculate precision, recall, and recall at FPR ≤ 0.001 for both models at this threshold on the test set, (2) determine whether the relative performance difference between GNN and GBM across these metric deltas exceeds 5 percentage points of recall or 10 percentage points of precision compared to their AUPRC-based ordering.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The GNN shows higher AUPRC but has ≥5% lower recall or ≥10% lower precision at the dominant operational threshold compared to GBM
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The GNN maintains ≤5% recall and ≤10% precision differences at the threshold compared to AUPRC-based ranking

sound_design_reference:
Use 18 months of telemetry from one enterprise environment so the comparison reflects the data generating process the SOC actually faces. Construct host-day examples, because this unit is operationally meaningful for triage and allows both relational and non-relational models to score the same decision target. Labels come from incident-response-confirmed malicious activity: mark a host-day positive when the host falls within seven days of a confirmed malicious interval, using ticket timelines and forensic scoping to align telemetry with the incident. Because security labels are delayed and imperfect, exclude host-days near unresolved investigations and remove analyst-authored fields, ticket identifiers, suppression states, and any post hoc annotations that could leak outcome information.

Split data strictly by time: train on months 1-11, validate on months 12-15, and test on months 16-18. In addition, keep all windows tied to the same incident or campaign in the same split based on first observed malicious activity, so later stages of one intrusion cannot bleed into development while earlier stages appear in evaluation. This is essential here because the hypothesis is explicitly about future-time generalization to unseen campaigns, not retrospective reconstruction of known attacks.

Build two representations from the same underlying event stream. The tabular baseline uses host-day aggregates: authentication anomalies, process rarity, parent-child execution patterns, lateral movement indicators, network connection novelty, destination reputation buckets, privilege changes, and rolling behavioral deltas, joined with asset criticality and identity metadata. The graph model uses those same raw signals as typed node and edge attributes in a heterogeneous graph over users, hosts, IPs, processes, and authentication sessions formed from the prior seven days of telemetry. All preprocessing, vocabularies, normalization statistics, and frequency cutoffs are fit on training only and then frozen.

Train a heterogeneous GNN to classify each host-day, tuning depth, hidden width, neighbor sampling, class weighting, and learning rate on the validation period. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline with matched tuning effort over standard tree hyperparameters and class weighting. This is the appropriate baseline because GBDTs are the strongest conventional choice for mixed enterprise telemetry once relations are flattened; if the GNN wins, the gain is credibly attributable to relational modeling rather than simply using richer metadata.

Use AUPRC as the primary metric. In this setting the SOC reviews a ranked alert queue and daily review capacity changes with staffing, incident load, and business criticality. The experiment asks whether the GNN surfaces true intrusions earlier in the queue across future attack windows, and AUPRC captures concentration of positives over the full ranking under extreme class imbalance better than AUROC.

Also report recall at fixed false-positive rate, precision at top-K hosts per day, AUROC, and Brier score so ranking, low-alert-budget performance, and calibration are all visible. Perform model selection on validation AUPRC with early stopping, freeze all choices, and touch the test set once at the end. Finally, run controls for host class, business unit, and campaign novelty, plus an ablation that removes graph edges while preserving node features to test whether any observed gain comes from structure rather than metadata alone. The experiment establishes whether graph-based relational inductive bias improves forward-looking intrusion detection in this environment; it does not by itself prove transfer to other enterprises with different telemetry coverage or investigation practices.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model using sequence features from source code changes, pull-request metadata, and developer collaboration graph signals will predict post-merge production incidents better than a baseline trained only on engineered repository-level metrics for enterprise DevOps teams, as measured by out-of-time AUROC and precision at the top risk decile.
domain: Software engineering / DevOps analytics for enterprise code repositories and pull requests
task_prompt:
Use merged pull requests as the prediction unit and construct a 24-month historical dataset from source-control, code-review, CI, deployment, and incident systems for repositories with reliable merge-to-deploy linkage. Define the label as whether the deployed PR is associated with a production incident or rollback within 7 days of first production deployment. Because attribution is imperfect, keep the labeling rule operationally strict and consistent: only incidents tied through deployment or rollback records are positive. Exclude PRs lacking deployment traceability from supervised training, but report how much traffic this removes to quantify coverage bias.

Respect the production setting with a chronological split by merge date: train on the earliest 70% of the timeline, tune on the next 15%, and reserve the final 15% as a pure out-of-time test. This matches the stated goal of forecasting future risky changes under process drift. Leakage is controlled by computing every feature from information available no later than the PR merge timestamp and by enforcing the 7-day outcome window before assigning labels. Encoders, imputers, and any normalization statistics are fit only on training data.

Engineer three feature families. First, sequence features summarize the dynamics of change creation and review: number of commits, elapsed time between commits, churn trajectories, touched-file entropy, reviewer turnaround, comment-thread depth, CI rerun sequences, and failure-to-pass patterns. Second, PR and repository context features capture static and recent operational state: changed files, added/deleted lines, language mix, service ownership, historical deploy cadence, rolling incident rate, branch age, and CI stability. Third, developer collaboration graph features capture organization structure at merge time: author-reviewer centrality, prior collaboration counts, cross-team review edges, reviewer-set novelty, and bus-factor proxies. Exclude any feature generated after merge or derived from incident systems except lagged historical aggregates.

Train a LightGBM classifier on the full feature set with class weighting and early stopping. Tune depth, leaves, learning rate, minimum leaf size, and subsampling on the validation block. Compare it against a fair baseline of the same model family trained only on repository-level engineered metrics. Using the same learner and tuning budget isolates the value of sequence and collaboration signals rather than conflating feature improvements with a more expressive algorithm.

Use AUROC on the out-of-time test set as the primary metric. The deployment use case is score-based triage: engineering leaders need a stable ranking of merged changes so scarce additional review, canarying, or staged rollout capacity can be directed toward the riskiest items across many teams. Since incident prevalence is low, also report precision and recall at the top risk decile, average precision, and Brier score. Model selection is driven by validation AUROC, with a check that top-decile precision is not materially degraded.

Control for confounds by excluding raw repository and team identifiers, using lagged and smoothed historical-rate features, and reporting subgroup performance by repository criticality, business unit, and incident prevalence. The experiment establishes whether richer code-change and collaboration signals improve incident-risk prediction beyond historical repository context under realistic forward-looking evaluation; it does not establish causal mechanisms for why individual changes fail in production.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the area under the ROC curve (AUROC) and precision at the top 10% of predicted scores for both the proposed model and the baseline on the held-out out-of-time test set. Determine whether the proposed model achieves at least a 0.02 absolute increase in AUROC relative to the baseline while exhibiting either a <0.01 absolute improvement in precision@10% or a decrease in precision@10%.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The proposed model improves AUROC by ≥0.02 but improves precision@10% by <0.01 or reduces it
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The proposed model improves AUROC by ≥0.02 and improves precision@10% by ≥0.01
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The proposed model does not improve AUROC by 0.02 relative to baseline

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the area under the ROC curve (AUROC) and precision at the top 10% of predicted scores for both the proposed model and the baseline on the held-out out-of-time test set. Determine whether the proposed model achieves at least a 0.02 absolute increase in AUROC relative to the baseline while exhibiting either a <0.01 absolute improvement in precision@10% or a decrease in precision@10%.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The proposed model improves AUROC by ≥0.02 but improves precision@10% by <0.01 or reduces it
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The proposed model improves AUROC by ≥0.02 and improves precision@10% by ≥0.01
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The proposed model does not improve AUROC by 0.02 relative to baseline

hypothesis: For a large hospital network, a transformer model that jointly encodes free-text chief complaints, triage notes, and the first 6 hours of vitals/labs will predict 24-hour emergency department admission better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline trained on structured triage variables alone.
domain: Healthcare / emergency department operations using triage records, free-text chief complaints, and early bedside/lab data from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective cohort of ED encounters from all hospitals in the system over four years. Define the prediction time uniformly at 6 hours after ED arrival and include only data that would have been available by that point: demographics, arrival mode, triage acuity, chief complaint, triage note, timestamped vitals, and ordered/resulted labs up to 6 hours. The outcome is inpatient admission within 24 hours of arrival, taken from administrative disposition and admission-order records. Exclude encounters with invalid timestamps, missing encounter linkage, or dispositions that are operationally non-comparable, such as direct transfers already assigned to inpatient care before ED evaluation. Standardize lab units and deduplicate near-simultaneous measurements using pre-specified clinical rules.

Split the data at the encounter level into 70/15/15 train, validation, and test partitions, stratified by hospital and admission status. Assign all encounters from the same patient to a single partition to prevent repeat-patient leakage. This split preserves each site’s case mix and admission prevalence in every partition, which is important because the objective is to compare how much additional early clinical signal the multimodal model extracts, not to measure adaptation to a changing policy regime. Leakage is prevented by censoring every feature strictly at the 6-hour mark and excluding bed requests, consultant disposition recommendations, post-6-hour orders, and any text entered after the prediction time.

Construct two model families. The proposed model is a multimodal transformer that jointly encodes chief complaint text, triage notes, and the ordered sequence of vitals/labs/events from the first 6 hours, fused with static structured fields. Relative-time embeddings capture when measurements occurred, and missingness indicators are retained because test-ordering behavior is itself informative. The main baseline is gradient-boosted trees on structured variables available by 6 hours, including triage data, initial vitals, abnormality flags, and early lab-order indicators, but excluding free text. Tune both models with comparable effort on the validation set.

Use AUROC as the primary comparison metric because the operational need is to sort the active ED census by admission likelihood so bed managers can prioritize attention under shifting capacity constraints; the exact intervention threshold will vary by hospital and by hour. Report AUPRC because prevalence may differ substantially across sites, and report Brier score, calibration slope/intercept, expected calibration error, and threshold-based PPV/recall at pre-specified high-risk cutoffs to characterize whether the ranking remains clinically usable at the top end.

Select hyperparameters and perform early stopping on validation only. After locking the configuration, refit on train plus validation and evaluate once on the held-out test set. Report pooled results and hospital-stratified results, plus subgroup checks by acuity, shift, interpreter use, and note length. This design will establish whether jointly modeling text and early physiological trajectory improves early admission discrimination beyond structured triage variables alone; it will not establish causal benefit in operations without a downstream deployment study.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision@hospital-specific K at the capped top fraction of the ED census reviewed for escalation decisions, comparing the transformer and baseline models on the held-out test set. If precision@K for the transformer is more than 0.03 absolute lower than the baseline at the operational K threshold, AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer's precision@K falls >0.03 absolute below the baseline at the deployed K threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer's precision@K is within 0.03 absolute of or exceeds the baseline at the deployed K threshold

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision@hospital-specific K at the capped top fraction of the ED census reviewed for escalation decisions, comparing the transformer and baseline models on the held-out test set. If precision@K for the transformer is more than 0.03 absolute lower than the baseline at the operational K threshold, AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer's precision@K falls >0.03 absolute below the baseline at the deployed K threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer's precision@K is within 0.03 absolute of or exceeds the baseline at the deployed K threshold

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates incident response ticket history and alert graph connectivity will prioritize true malicious alerts above a flat binary classifier trained on the same raw alert fields, yielding higher top-k precision on a chronological holdout period.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a production-faithful retrospective study built from 18 months of SIEM and EDR alert streams joined to analyst dispositions, incident response tickets, and alert-linkage metadata. The unit of ranking is the alert as seen at triage time. Labels should reflect confirmed maliciousness rather than mere analyst attention, so an alert is positive if it is marked malicious by an analyst or attached to an incident ticket that is ultimately confirmed malicious within 14 days. Alerts explicitly closed benign are negatives. Unresolved and weakly reviewed alerts are excluded from supervised fitting to avoid forcing noisy negatives, but they remain in descriptive analyses so you can quantify coverage and selection bias.

Because SOC data drift with attacker behavior, detection content, and analyst practice, the split should be strictly chronological: months 1-12 for training, 13-15 for validation, and 16-18 for test. Alerts tied to the same incident or case must stay in the same split to prevent leakage through duplicated campaigns. Every transform is fit on the training window only. Feature computation must also respect event time: graph and history features are derived only from alerts and tickets available up to each alert timestamp, never from future case closures or later-linked alerts.

The feature set has three parts. First are raw alert fields available immediately at triage, such as rule name, product, severity, host class, user type, alert volume, and diurnal context. Second are incident-history features summarizing prior security activity for the same host, user, subnet, or rule over trailing windows. Third are graph-connectivity features that capture whether an alert sits inside a dense connected structure of related entities or near previously confirmed malicious activity. Fields that encode analyst workflow after triage, including queue assignment, final closure metadata, or responder identity, are excluded.

For the proposed method, train a LambdaMART ranker with hourly analyst triage windows as query groups so the model learns to order alerts within realistic review batches. Use a gradient-boosted binary classifier on the same raw alert fields as the baseline, with comparable hyperparameter search budget and probability-based sorting. This isolates the hypothesis: whether ranking with historical and graph context outperforms a flat alert-only classifier.

Model selection is done on the validation block using NDCG@20, with early stopping and predeclared ablations for raw-only, raw-plus-history, and full features. The final test block is touched once after all decisions are frozen. Primary reporting uses NDCG@20 because analysts act on the head of the queue, and the experiment asks whether malicious alerts are lifted into that reviewed slice. Secondary metrics include Precision@5, Precision@20, MRR, and AUCPR.

To address confounds, compare performance on first-seen entities, on newly introduced rules, and across business units and detection sources. Also report whether gains remain after controlling for simple recency and alert-volume effects. This experiment establishes whether the added context improves prioritization on future SOC traffic; it does not by itself prove reduced analyst workload or incident containment time without an online study.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the absolute improvement in NDCG@20 and Precision@K (where K equals the SOC's observed median per-shift review depth, e.g., K=10) between the LambdaMART ranker and the baseline classifier on the validation and test periods. The test is resolved if NDCG@20 improvement exceeds 0.03 AND Precision@K improvement is less than 0.01.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: NDCG@20 improves by >0.03 and Precision@K improves by <0.01
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: NDCG@20 improves by ≤0.03 OR Precision@K improves by ≥0.01

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the absolute improvement in NDCG@20 and Precision@K (where K equals the SOC's observed median per-shift review depth, e.g., K=10) between the LambdaMART ranker and the baseline classifier on the validation and test periods. The test is resolved if NDCG@20 improvement exceeds 0.03 AND Precision@K improvement is less than 0.01.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: NDCG@20 improves by >0.03 and Precision@K improves by <0.01
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: NDCG@20 improves by ≤0.03 OR Precision@K improves by ≥0.01

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model fine-tuned on raw email text plus header metadata and sender-reputation features will detect phishing emails better than a baseline gradient-boosted model trained only on engineered URL, domain, and attachment features, as measured by PR-AUC on a future-time holdout set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of inbound mail-gateway data from one enterprise, using only signals available at message receipt time. The dataset includes subject and body text, parsed header fields, embedded URL metadata, attachment metadata, and sender-reputation logs. Because labels in this environment mature slowly, you should include only messages whose case outcome is finalized and impose a 21-day maturation window before extraction so messages later escalated to phishing are not treated as benign. The binary target is phishing versus non-phishing, where phishing includes credential theft, malware delivery, business email compromise, and malicious lures confirmed by analysts.

The split should be strictly chronological: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test by receipt timestamp. In email defense, campaign signatures, sender infrastructure, and lure language drift continuously, so the correct question is whether a model trained on past mail ranks future mail better. To prevent inflated estimates from campaign duplication, remove exact duplicates before splitting and group near-duplicates using normalized URL sets and subject hashes so variant copies of the same campaign do not appear in both training and evaluation windows.

For the proposed model, fine-tune a pretrained transformer on subject plus body text, and concatenate pooled text embeddings with structured header and sender-reputation features before the final classifier. Useful structured inputs include SPF/DKIM/DMARC outcomes, Reply-To mismatch indicators, sending IP geography/ASN, MIME irregularities, historical sender complaint rates, first-seen age, prior malicious verdict rate, and burstiness features computed only from events preceding each message. All tokenizers, imputers, frequency encoders, and scalers are fit on training data only and then frozen.

The baseline should be a strong gradient-boosted tree model over the structured artifact features that production systems commonly rely on: URL lexical characteristics, domain/TLD and reputation features, redirect and obfuscation indicators available at receipt, attachment type and macro/archive signals, plus lightweight header anomaly counts. Give the baseline equivalent tuning effort on the same validation window so the comparison tests the added value of raw-text representation learning rather than differential optimization effort.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric on the final future-time holdout because phishing is rare and the security queue operates on a ranked stream of candidates. Secondary reporting should include precision at 90% recall, recall at a fixed false-positive rate per 10,000 messages, ROC-AUC, and calibration metrics such as Brier score and expected calibration error. Select hyperparameters and early stopping solely on validation PR-AUC, freeze the pipeline, and touch the test set once at the end.

Finally, control for confounds that could mimic semantic improvement. Report a seen-versus-unseen sender-domain breakdown, plus strata for link-only versus attachment-bearing messages and recipient business unit. This experiment establishes whether the transformer stack improves future phishing detection under realistic enterprise conditions; it does not, by itself, prove that the gain comes exclusively from deeper language understanding rather than a combination of text and richer contextual cues.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision at the operational recall level (e.g., 90% recall) and false positives per 10,000 messages on the future-time holdout set, comparing the transformer and gradient-boosted models at a fixed decision threshold derived from production policy. If the threshold is not fixed, validate PR-AUC differences only at the same recall operating point.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer’s precision at the operational recall level is less than 2 percentage points higher than the baseline or results in more than 10 false positives per 10,000 messages
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer’s precision at the operational recall level is ≥2 percentage points higher than the baseline and results in ≤10 false positives per 10,000 messages
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer’s precision is exactly 2 percentage points higher or false positives are exactly 10 per 10,000 messages

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision at the operational recall level (e.g., 90% recall) and false positives per 10,000 messages on the future-time holdout set, comparing the transformer and gradient-boosted models at a fixed decision threshold derived from production policy. If the threshold is not fixed, validate PR-AUC differences only at the same recall operating point.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer’s precision at the operational recall level is less than 2 percentage points higher than the baseline or results in more than 10 false positives per 10,000 messages
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer’s precision at the operational recall level is ≥2 percentage points higher than the baseline and results in ≤10 false positives per 10,000 messages
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Transformer’s precision is exactly 2 percentage points higher or false positives are exactly 10 per 10,000 messages

You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of inbound mail-gateway data from one enterprise, using only signals available at message receipt time. The dataset includes subject and body text, parsed header fields, embedded URL metadata, attachment metadata, and sender-reputation logs. Because labels in this environment mature slowly, you should include only messages whose case outcome is finalized and impose a 21-day maturation window before extraction so messages later escalated to phishing are not treated as benign. The binary target is phishing versus non-phishing, where phishing includes credential theft, malware delivery, business email compromise, and malicious lures confirmed by analysts.

The split should be strictly chronological: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test by receipt timestamp. In email defense, campaign signatures, sender infrastructure, and lure language drift continuously, so the correct question is whether a model trained on past mail ranks future mail better. To prevent inflated estimates from campaign duplication, remove exact duplicates before splitting and group near-duplicates using normalized URL sets and subject hashes so variant copies of the same campaign do not appear in both training and evaluation windows.

For the proposed model, fine-tune a pretrained transformer on subject plus body text, and concatenate pooled text embeddings with structured header and sender-reputation features before the final classifier. Useful structured inputs include SPF/DKIM/DMARC outcomes, Reply-To mismatch indicators, sending IP geography/ASN, MIME irregularities, historical sender complaint rates, first-seen age, prior malicious verdict rate, and burstiness features computed only from events preceding each message. All tokenizers, imputers, frequency encoders, and scalers are fit on training data only and then frozen.

The baseline should be a strong gradient-boosted tree model over the structured artifact features that production systems commonly rely on: URL lexical characteristics, domain/TLD and reputation features, redirect and obfuscation indicators available at receipt, attachment type and macro/archive signals, plus lightweight header anomaly counts. Give the baseline equivalent tuning effort on the same validation window so the comparison tests the added value of raw-text representation learning rather than differential optimization effort.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric on the final future-time holdout because phishing is rare and the security queue operates on a ranked stream of candidates. Secondary reporting should include precision at 90% recall, recall at a fixed false-positive rate per 10,000 messages, ROC-AUC, and calibration metrics such as Brier score and expected calibration error. Select hyperparameters and early stopping solely on validation PR-AUC, freeze the pipeline, and touch the test set once at the end.

Finally, control for confounds that could mimic semantic improvement. Report a seen-versus-unseen sender-domain breakdown, plus strata for link-only versus attachment-bearing messages and recipient business unit. This experiment establishes whether the transformer stack improves future phishing detection under realistic enterprise conditions; it does not, by itself, prove that the gain comes exclusively from deeper language understanding rather than a combination of text and richer contextual cues.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity threat detection, a graph-based model that incorporates user-device-email relationship features will identify account-takeover incidents more accurately than a tabular model trained only on per-event identity provider and device telemetry, as measured on future incidents in a chronologically held-out period.
domain: Cybersecurity threat detection for enterprise account takeover investigation using identity provider logs, device telemetry, and email security events
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis as a future-incident binary classification problem at the user-day level. The data should cover 18 months of routine production telemetry: identity provider authentication and SSO logs, endpoint/device events, email security events, and analyst-confirmed account-takeover outcomes. Because ground truth is delayed, labels should be generated only where case closure maturity is sufficient. A positive label is assigned to user-days within the pre-investigation detection window for a confirmed account takeover, anchored to the earliest verified compromise onset when available and otherwise to a conservative backdated proxy from investigation open time. User-days with no linked takeover investigation in the following 30 days serve as negatives. Periods with unresolved investigations are excluded.

The split should be strictly chronological. Train on months 1-12, validate on months 13-15, and test on months 16-18, with a 30-day maturity buffer at each boundary. All features must be computed using only information available up to the scoring timestamp, which avoids leakage from future campaign artifacts, retrospective analyst knowledge, or post-incident enrichment.

To make the comparison fair, both models should use the same raw event sources and the same 30-day cutoff window. The tabular pipeline will build per-user/day aggregates such as failed login counts, MFA challenge patterns, impossible-travel flags, IP risk summaries, geovelocity, new-device indicators, endpoint alert rates, mailbox rule creation, unusual sender/recipient behavior, and deviations from each user’s historical baseline. The graph pipeline will use those same raw signals to form a heterogeneous graph over users, devices, IPs, mailboxes, and message entities, with recency-weighted edges and node attributes derived from the same aggregates. Analyst workflow fields, ticket metadata, and any post-investigation annotations are excluded from both pipelines.

The proposed model is a heterogeneous graph neural network with two message-passing layers and class-weighted loss. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree trained on the matched tabular feature set. Both receive comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the comparison tests representational advantage rather than optimization budget.

Model selection is done on the validation period using PR-AUC as the primary metric. That metric matches the operational setting because account takeover is rare and the SOC consumes a ranked queue under variable daily capacity; the key question is whether true compromises are concentrated near the top of that queue. Secondary reporting should include precision at the top 0.5% of scored user-days, recall at the historical alert budget, AUROC, and calibration via Brier score. The test set is touched once at the end for final reporting only.

To address confounds, hold raw inputs, label logic, and lookback window constant across models. Report subgroup results by campaign cluster, policy segment, and unseen-device prevalence, and run ablations that remove email-derived features from both systems. This design establishes whether the graph formulation improves forward-looking detection performance under realistic deployment constraints; it does not by itself prove which relational mechanism is responsible for any observed gain.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute both models' recall at the fixed historical investigation budget and precision at the alert threshold corresponding to the top 0.5% of scored user-days on the validation set. If both differences are within ±3 percentage points, PR-AUC alone is sufficient; if either difference exceeds 3 percentage points in the PR-AUC-favored model's disfavor, PR-AUC must be demoted and threshold-specific metrics elevated to co-primary status.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The model favored by PR-AUC has precision at top 0.5% OR recall at the budget 3+ percentage points worse than the alternative metric
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Both precision and recall at threshold are within 3 percentage points between models (including PR-AUC-favored model being better or equal)

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute both models' recall at the fixed historical investigation budget and precision at the alert threshold corresponding to the top 0.5% of scored user-days on the validation set. If both differences are within ±3 percentage points, PR-AUC alone is sufficient; if either difference exceeds 3 percentage points in the PR-AUC-favored model's disfavor, PR-AUC must be demoted and threshold-specific metrics elevated to co-primary status.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The model favored by PR-AUC has precision at top 0.5% OR recall at the budget 3+ percentage points worse than the alternative metric
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Both precision and recall at threshold are within 3 percentage points between models (including PR-AUC-favored model being better or equal)

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model fine-tuned on email body text plus headers and URL features will detect phishing emails with higher AUROC and better precision at a fixed low false-positive rate than a gradient-boosted model trained on handcrafted sender, header, and lexical features.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt: Use 18 months of inbound gateway data from a single enterprise because the deployment question is organization-specific... Control key confounds explicitly... The experiment establishes whether richer text-plus-structure modeling improves phishing ranking and low-FPR detection on future enterprise mail...

required_empirical_test.condition: On the held-out validation set, compute AUROC, precision at FPR <= 0.1%, and TPR at FPR <= 0.1%. If the model with higher AUROC is worse than the alternative by more than 5 percentage points in precision or 3 percentage points in TPR at FPR <= 0.1%, AUROC is insufficient as the primary selection metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer achieves higher AUROC but has precision at 0.1% FPR < gradient-boosted by >5% or TPR by >3%
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer achieves higher AUROC and maintains precision at 0.1% FPR ≥ gradient-boosted by ≤5% AND TPR ≥ baseline by ≤3%

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: On the held-out validation set, compute AUROC, precision at FPR <= 0.1%, and TPR at FPR <= 0.1%. If the model with higher AUROC is worse than the alternative by more than 5 percentage points in precision or 3 percentage points in TPR at FPR <= 0.1%, AUROC is insufficient as the primary selection metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer achieves higher AUROC but has precision at 0.1% FPR < gradient-boosted by >5% or TPR by >3%
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer achieves higher AUROC and maintains precision at 0.1% FPR ≥ gradient-boosted by ≤5% AND TPR ≥ baseline by ≤3%

The experiment establishes whether richer text-plus-structure modeling improves phishing ranking and low-FPR detection on future enterprise mail...

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that combines SIEM alert features with incident response ticket text and historical analyst feedback will surface true high-priority incidents above the fold more effectively than a classifier-based alert scoring baseline trained only on structured alert metadata.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on production SOC alert streams joined to downstream incident response records over an 18-month period. The unit of prediction is the alert at triage time, but training and evaluation are done as grouped ranking problems so the model learns which alerts should rise within the set actually competing for analyst attention. To keep the study aligned with deployment, include only production alert types and queues that feed the analyst workflow you intend to support.

Labels should reflect downstream operational consequence rather than raw analyst queue order. A practical graded relevance target is 2 for alerts tied to confirmed High/Critical incidents, malicious findings, or containment/remediation within 7 days; 1 for confirmed Medium-severity incidents; and 0 for benign, duplicate, or non-actionable alerts. Cases unresolved after 7 days should be dropped from supervised training because they do not provide stable supervision. This gives a ranking target that prioritizes the incidents the SOC most wants surfaced immediately.

Because attacker behavior, rule tuning, and asset inventory shift over time, use a strict chronological split: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. Build ranking groups within analyst queue-day and business unit so each query approximates a real triage slate. All transforms are fit on the training window only. Feature generation must be time-safe: numerical aggregates are computed from history strictly before the alert timestamp, and any text used from tickets or case summaries is limited to text available within two hours of alert creation. Final disposition fields, closure codes, post-incident writeups, and explicit queue-order signals are excluded because they would leak outcomes or simply reproduce prior human decisions.

The proposed model is LambdaMART, using structured SIEM/EDR metadata, short-horizon alert history, entity recurrence statistics, asset/user context, MITRE mappings, and sparse text features from early ticket content. Historical analyst feedback is included only as prior-pattern summaries for rule-asset-user combinations, never as the current alert's own outcome fields. The main baseline is a gradient-boosted classifier trained only on structured metadata and ranked by predicted probability within the same queue groups. This baseline is the correct comparison for the hypothesis because it isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information source from a realistic metadata-only scoring system.

Use NDCG@10 as the primary metric, with MRR, Recall@10, Precision@10, and AUCPR on a binary severe-incident label as secondary metrics. Model selection is performed on the validation window using NDCG@10 with grouped bootstrap intervals over queue-days, and the test window is held untouched until the end. Finally, run ablations by feature family and subgroup checks by business unit, alert family, and unseen rule IDs to determine whether gains are broad or concentrated. The experiment can establish improved historical triage prioritization under drift-aware evaluation; it does not by itself prove causal reduction in dwell time or immunity to policy-driven label bias.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the operational review cutoff K as the median number of alerts reviewed per analyst queue within the first 30 minutes of triage. Compare the candidate and baseline models' NDCG@10 and Precision@K scores on the validation set: (1) Check whether the relative model order flips between metrics (e.g., model A > model B in NDCG@10 but model B > model A in Precision@K), or (2) Check whether absolute Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 while NDCG@10 difference is less than 0.01.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The models’ relative performance differs between NDCG@10 and Precision@K or the Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 when NDCG@10 difference is below 0.01
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The models' orderings are consistent across metrics and the Precision@K gap does not exceed 0.05 when NDCG@10 differs by less than 0.01

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the operational review cutoff K as the median number of alerts reviewed per analyst queue within the first 30 minutes of triage. Compare the candidate and baseline models' NDCG@10 and Precision@K scores on the validation set: (1) Check whether the relative model order flips between metrics (e.g., model A > model B in NDCG@10 but model B > model A in Precision@K), or (2) Check whether absolute Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 while NDCG@10 difference is less than 0.01.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The models’ relative performance differs between NDCG@10 and Precision@K or the Precision@K gap exceeds 0.05 when NDCG@10 difference is below 0.01
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The models' orderings are consistent across metrics and the Precision@K gap does not exceed 0.05 when NDCG@10 differs by less than 0.01
