hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity email security, a transformer model trained on raw email text plus header and URL features will detect phishing and credential-harvesting emails better than a feature-engineered gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by precision-recall AUC on a future-time holdout set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on one year of inbound enterprise email collected at the mail gateway, using only signals available at receipt time: subject, body, MIME/header metadata, extracted URLs, sender/domain reputation logs, attachment indicators, and downstream analyst or incident dispositions. The supervised task is binary phishing/credential-harvesting detection. Positive labels should require confirmed malicious disposition through analyst review, threat-intel enrichment, or incident closure. Negatives should be messages explicitly resolved as benign. Messages that remain merely suspicious or unresolved should be excluded from supervised training so the comparison is not driven by ambiguous labels.

Because this is an adversarial and drifting domain, the split should be chronological: 8 months train, 2 months validation, 2 months test by receipt timestamp. In addition, repeated campaigns are a major leakage risk, so near-duplicate emails must be clustered using normalized subject/body/URL shingles, with each cluster assigned entirely to one split. This prevents a templated phish from appearing in both train and test under minor edits. Any sender reputation or domain-age feature must be snapshotted as of message receipt, never recomputed using future observations.

The proposed model is a multimodal transformer that encodes raw subject and body text and fuses that representation with structured features from headers, authentication outcomes, URL lexical properties, sender/domain reputation buckets, attachment indicators, routing metadata, and basic formatting cues. The baseline should be a strong gradient-boosted tree model using a fair engineered feature set: character and token n-gram summaries, phishing heuristics, URL statistics, SPF/DKIM/DMARC signals, sender mismatch indicators, reputation values, and attachment metadata. Both models should receive comparable tuning effort on the validation set so the comparison is about representation power rather than search asymmetry.

All preprocessing must be fit on the training data only. That includes tokenizer/statistics construction, categorical vocabularies, scaling parameters, and any URL normalization maps. Validation is used for hyperparameter selection and early stopping. The test set remains sealed until all choices are frozen.

The primary metric is precision-recall AUC on the future-time holdout. In this environment the class distribution is highly skewed, and the operational question is whether the model ranks truly malicious emails above benign traffic consistently enough to support stricter analyst queues and user-facing controls. PR-AUC captures that ranking quality in the part of the problem that matters: maintaining useful precision while pushing recall upward. Secondary metrics should include recall at 95% and 99% precision, precision at a fixed analyst-review budget K, ROC-AUC, and calibration measures.

To address confounds, run ablations that remove length and formatting features to verify that gains are not purely template-driven. Report subgroup performance on previously unseen sender domains, unseen URL domains, and reputation strata to test generalization to novel campaigns. This experiment can establish whether the transformer provides better future-time phishing discrimination than a strong engineered baseline under realistic deployment constraints; it does not by itself establish robustness to fully novel attack modalities outside the observed enterprise traffic mix.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision of both models at the operational threshold (e.g., 99% precision requirement) on the future-time test set; also compute recall at this threshold. Compare precision difference (transformer vs. baseline). If transformer's precision is ≤2 percentage points below baseline at this exact threshold and/or recall is comparable, PR-AUC remains valid. If precision deficit exceeds 2%, PR-AUC is insufficient.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer model's precision at the operational threshold is more than 2 percentage points lower than the baseline's precision at the same threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer model's precision deficit at the threshold is ≤2% and/or recall is higher
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer model's precision deficit is exactly 2 percentage points below baseline

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision of both models at the operational threshold (e.g., 99% precision requirement) on the future-time test set; also compute recall at this threshold. Compare precision difference (transformer vs. baseline). If transformer's precision is ≤2 percentage points below baseline at this exact threshold and/or recall is comparable, PR-AUC remains valid. If precision deficit exceeds 2%, PR-AUC is insufficient.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer model's precision at the operational threshold is more than 2 percentage points lower than the baseline's precision at the same threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer model's precision deficit at the threshold is ≤2% and/or recall is higher
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Transformer model's precision deficit is exactly 2 percentage points below baseline

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity incident triage, a learning-to-rank model trained on SIEM alerts plus incident-response ticket history will prioritize true incidents more effectively than a binary alert classifier, yielding higher precision in the top-k alerts reviewed by analysts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a retrospective, production-shaped study built from the organization’s historical SIEM/EDR alert stream joined to incident-response tickets and analyst action logs. Use 18 months of data so the experiment spans multiple detector updates, staffing patterns, and attacker campaigns. Each alert is represented only with information available at alert arrival: native alert fields, detector identity, host/user context, enrichment signals, short-horizon burst statistics, recent entity history, and queue-state variables such as current queue depth. Exclude any analyst-authored or post-disposition fields because they would leak the target.

Construct labels from ticket outcomes. An alert is positive if it is linked within 72 hours to a confirmed incident or a substantive escalation requiring investigation or containment; alerts dispositioned benign are negative. Because the operational task is triage within a live queue rather than isolated scoring, form ranking groups as hourly analyst queues within business unit. This lets the model learn the exact decision problem analysts face: which alerts should be reviewed first among competing contemporaneous candidates.

Use a strict chronological split: train on months 1-12, validate on months 13-15, and test on months 16-18. In this domain, random shuffling would overstate performance because detector tuning, enrichment coverage, and attacker behavior evolve materially over time. Fit all preprocessors only on the training period, then apply them unchanged to later periods. Any rolling-count or prior-history feature must be computed from events strictly preceding the scored alert, and detector metadata introduced later must not be backfilled across split boundaries.

Train a LambdaMART ranker as the proposed model. It is well matched to heterogeneous alert features and the objective of ordering positives ahead of benign items inside each queue. Compare it against a strong binary baseline: a gradient-boosted classifier trained on the same features, with the same train/validation/test periods and comparable hyperparameter effort, then converted to a ranked list by predicted score. Also report the current operational heuristic baseline of vendor severity followed by FIFO ordering, since superiority over today’s process is practically important.

Select models on the validation set using mean NDCG@20 across queues. This metric is appropriate because the SOC’s fixed analyst review budget is concentrated at the top of each queue, and gains in the first handful of positions are far more valuable than improvements deep in the list. Report Precision@5, Precision@20, MRR, recall@20, and AUCPR as secondary diagnostics. Freeze the entire pipeline before touching the final test period and evaluate once, with bootstrap confidence intervals over queues.

To address confounds, report performance by detector family, asset criticality, business unit, and time-of-day. Run feature ablations removing recency and source-product indicators to check whether lift is merely exploiting workflow regularities. Because reviewed alerts are a selected subset, also quantify analyst-review propensity and document where labels are sparse. The experiment will establish whether ranking produces better future triage ordering on historical SOC data, not whether it causally reduces breach impact.

required_empirical_test.condition: Measure the historical analyst review budget per hour as the median number of alerts reviewed per queue before handoff/rollover. On the validation set, compute both NDCG@20 and Precision@K for the measured K value. Compare whether the model selected by NDCG@20 would differ from the model selected by Precision@K at that exact K. A 0.05 absolute gap in Precision@K between the top-ranked models determines the threshold.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the top-ranked model by NDCG@20 has Precision@K at the measured K less than 0.05 higher, or worse, than a model ranked lower by NDCG@20 but better by Precision@K
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the model with highest NDCG@20 also has Precision@K at the measured K that is at least 0.05 higher than any other model

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Measure the historical analyst review budget per hour as the median number of alerts reviewed per queue before handoff/rollover. On the validation set, compute both NDCG@20 and Precision@K for the measured K value. Compare whether the model selected by NDCG@20 would differ from the model selected by Precision@K at that exact K. A 0.05 absolute gap in Precision@K between the top-ranked models determines the threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the top-ranked model by NDCG@20 has Precision@K at the measured K less than 0.05 higher, or worse, than a model ranked lower by NDCG@20 but better by Precision@K
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the model with highest NDCG@20 also has Precision@K at the measured K that is at least 0.05 higher than any other model

sound_design_reference:
You would evaluate the hypothesis with a retrospective, production-shaped study built from the organization’s historical SIEM/EDR alert stream joined to incident-response tickets and analyst action logs. Use 18 months of data so the experiment spans multiple detector updates, staffing patterns, and attacker campaigns. Each alert is represented only with information available at alert arrival: native alert fields, detector identity, host/user context, enrichment signals, short-horizon burst statistics, recent entity history, and queue-state variables such as current queue depth. Exclude any analyst-authored or post-disposition fields because they would leak the target.

Construct labels from ticket outcomes. An alert is positive if it is linked within 72 hours to a confirmed incident or a substantive escalation requiring investigation or containment; alerts dispositioned benign are negative. Because the operational task is triage within a live queue rather than isolated scoring, form ranking groups as hourly analyst queues within business unit. This lets the model learn the exact decision problem analysts face: which alerts should be reviewed first among competing contemporaneous candidates.

Use a strict chronological split: train on months 1-12, validate on months 13-15, and test on months 16-18. In this domain, random shuffling would overstate performance because detector tuning, enrichment coverage, and attacker behavior evolve materially over time. Fit all preprocessors only on the training period, then apply them unchanged to later periods. Any rolling-count or prior-history feature must be computed from events strictly preceding the scored alert, and detector metadata introduced later must not be backfilled across split boundaries.

Train a LambdaMART ranker as the proposed model. It is well matched to heterogeneous alert features and the objective of ordering positives ahead of benign items inside each queue. Compare it against a strong binary baseline: a gradient-boosted classifier trained on the same features, with the same train/validation/test periods and comparable hyperparameter effort, then converted to a ranked list by predicted score. Also report the current operational heuristic baseline of vendor severity followed by FIFO ordering, since superiority over today’s process is practically important.

Select models on the validation set using mean NDCG@20 across queues. This metric is appropriate because the SOC’s fixed analyst review budget is concentrated at the top of each queue, and gains in the first handful of positions are far more valuable than improvements deep in the list. Report Precision@5, Precision@20, MRR, recall@20, and AUCPR as secondary diagnostics. Freeze the entire pipeline before touching the final test period and evaluate once, with bootstrap confidence intervals over queues.

To address confounds, report performance by detector family, asset criticality, business unit, and time-of-day. Run feature ablations removing recency and source-product indicators to check whether lift is merely exploiting workflow regularities. Because reviewed alerts are a selected subset, also quantify analyst-review propensity and document where labels are sparse. The experiment will establish whether ranking produces better future triage ordering on historical SOC data, not whether it causally reduces breach impact.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity phishing detection, a transformer-based model fine-tuned on email body text plus header and URL features will achieve higher event-level F1 than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on engineered sender, recipient, and lexical statistics, particularly on previously unseen phishing campaigns.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
Use a year of inbound enterprise email data that reflects the actual deployment stream: raw subject and body text, SMTP headers, extracted URLs, attachment metadata, and sender reputation logs, linked to analyst review outcomes, sandbox verdicts, user reports, and downstream incident confirmations. Because phishing labels mature slowly and negatives are partly censored, impose a 21-day maturation window before assigning negatives and exclude the most recent unlabeled mail from all splits. Positives are emails confirmed as phishing through adjudication, malicious sandbox outcome, or later incident linkage within 14 days. Remove exact re-ingests and collapse near-duplicate messages into campaign clusters using normalized body, URL, and sender-template similarity so recurring variants can be tracked.

Split by arrival time, not randomly: train on months 1-8, validate on months 9-10, and test on months 11-12. In addition, assign each near-duplicate campaign cluster to the first split in which it appears so later copies do not leak across train, validation, and test. This directly measures the intended deployment scenario: detecting future phishing traffic, especially campaigns not previously seen. All preprocessing statistics for engineered features are fit on training only; validation and test use frozen transforms. Exclude any fields that arise after message arrival, including analyst notes, user actions after delivery, remediation events, and incident workflow metadata.

Train two models. The proposed model is a multimodal classifier that fine-tunes a pretrained transformer on subject plus body text and concatenates the resulting text embedding with structured header, URL, attachment, and sender-reputation features before a small MLP classification head. The baseline is a strong gradient-boosted tree over the same structured features plus engineered lexical statistics from text, such as token counts, urgency phrases, brand impersonation patterns, obfuscation markers, and URL rarity signals. Tune both models with comparable effort on the validation split only.

The primary evaluation metric is event-level F1. This gives a direct message-level comparison between the multimodal transformer and the boosted-tree detector under severe imbalance, and it rewards models that improve the overall precision-recall balance rather than optimizing only one side of the tradeoff. Thresholds are selected on the validation set and then frozen. In addition to F1, report recall at false-positive rates of 0.1% and 0.01%, precision-recall AUC, and thresholded precision/recall, since security teams care about catching more malicious mail without creating review overload.

Model selection uses validation F1, with early stopping for the transformer and standard hyperparameter search for both systems. The test set is touched once at the end. To address confounds, report performance separately for seen versus unseen campaigns, rerun evaluation with URL/domain reputation features removed to check whether any gain is driven mainly by memorized artifacts, and slice results by label source because analyst-confirmed and user-reported positives have different noise profiles. This experiment establishes whether the transformer-based multimodal detector improves held-out message classification and future-campaign generalization under realistic operational data constraints; it does not by itself prove that any gain comes specifically from deeper semantic understanding rather than better use of residual template signals.

required_empirical_test.condition: On the validation set, identify the decision thresholds corresponding to false-positive rates (FPR) of 0.1% and 0.01% for each model. Compare the recall of the transformer-based model and the gradient-boosted baseline at these FPR thresholds against the recall achieved by the model with higher F1 score. If the higher-F1 model's recall is more than 5 percentage points lower at either FPR threshold, the F1 metric is insufficient as the primary endpoint.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The higher-F1 model achieves recall more than 5 percentage points lower than the competing model at either 0.1% or 0.01% FPR thresholds
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The higher-F1 model's recall is no more than 5 percentage points lower than the competing model at both FPR thresholds

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: On the validation set, identify the decision thresholds corresponding to false-positive rates (FPR) of 0.1% and 0.01% for each model. Compare the recall of the transformer-based model and the gradient-boosted baseline at these FPR thresholds against the recall achieved by the model with higher F1 score. If the higher-F1 model's recall is more than 5 percentage points lower at either FPR threshold, the F1 metric is insufficient as the primary endpoint.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The higher-F1 model achieves recall more than 5 percentage points lower than the competing model at either 0.1% or 0.01% FPR thresholds
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The higher-F1 model's recall is no more than 5 percentage points lower than the competing model at both FPR thresholds

hypothesis: For enterprise software supply-chain security, a graph model that combines package dependency edges, maintainer history, and vulnerability scanner signals will detect vulnerable open-source dependencies better than a tabular baseline built only from package metadata and scanner counts, as measured by PR-AUC on held-out future advisories.
domain: Software supply-chain security analytics for enterprise code repositories and dependency management systems
task_prompt:
Build the experiment around dependency snapshots taken weekly over a 24-month period from enterprise repositories. For each snapshot, create an example for every resolved dependency instance appearing in active codebases, joining manifest and lockfile data with repository metadata, package-manager dependency relations, scanner outputs, public CVE records, and internal advisory logs. Define the prediction task as early warning: given information available on the snapshot date, predict whether that dependency will receive a new vulnerability advisory within the next 90 days. Because disclosure lag can make recent negatives unreliable, drop the final 90 days of raw observations before splitting.

Use a strict chronological split: the earliest 70% of weeks for training, the next 15% for validation, and the final 15% for test. This is the correct deployment-faithful design because both package ecosystems and disclosure patterns drift over time, and the model is intended to score future dependencies rather than interpolate among contemporaneous records. To avoid leakage, compute every feature from information available no later than the snapshot date, fit imputers, encoders, scalers, and graph normalization statistics only on training data, and freeze them afterward. Repeated appearances of the same dependency in adjacent snapshots can remain as separate examples because they represent separate prediction opportunities, but duplicates created by redundant ingestion within a week should be collapsed.

Train a heterogeneous graph neural network over package-version, maintainer, and repository nodes, with edges for dependency relations and maintainer-package links. Use node and edge attributes capturing package age, ecosystem, release cadence, scanner findings by severity, transitive depth, historical maintainer advisory rate, repository security posture, update recency, and popularity buckets. Compute graph-derived statistics such as centrality from the training graph only. Exclude any signal that directly references future advisories, future patch commits, or remediation actions. As the baseline, train a gradient-boosted tree on an aligned tabular feature set containing package metadata, repository metadata, and scanner aggregates but no graph neighborhood information. Give both models comparable tuning effort using the validation window.

Evaluate the final comparison on the held-out future test period using PR-AUC as the primary metric. The practical objective is to rank truly risky dependencies near the top so analysts can review the most concerning items first under tight capacity constraints; in a heavily imbalanced security setting, this ranking quality is better reflected by PR-AUC than by metrics dominated by abundant negatives. Report recall at the top 1%, 5%, and 10% of scored dependencies, precision at a top-K corresponding to weekly analyst budget, ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics as secondary views.

Select hyperparameters solely on validation PR-AUC, lock the full pipeline, and touch the test set once. To address confounds, report performance by package popularity, repository size, ecosystem, and whether a dependency is newly introduced or long-lived. Also run ablations removing popularity and maintainer-history features and compare results within popularity-matched strata. This experiment establishes whether relational structure improves future vulnerability ranking beyond strong tabular security signals; it does not by itself prove causal mechanisms behind why a package becomes vulnerable.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the proportion of operational scoring runs using a fixed threshold or weekly review budget within 10% of the mean value in historical triage logs. If at least 80% of scoring runs fall into this category, measure the graph model's precision at that threshold/top-K and compare to the baseline's precision. A 5 percentage point deficit or greater at the operational point indicates PR-AUC is insufficient as the sole primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: At least 80% of operational cases use a fixed threshold or stable review budget AND the graph model's precision at that point is lower than the baseline by ≥5 percentage points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Fewer than 80% of cases use fixed/stable thresholds OR the graph model's precision deficit is ≤5 percentage points at the stable operating point

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the proportion of operational scoring runs using a fixed threshold or weekly review budget within 10% of the mean value in historical triage logs. If at least 80% of scoring runs fall into this category, measure the graph model's precision at that threshold/top-K and compare to the baseline's precision. A 5 percentage point deficit or greater at the operational point indicates PR-AUC is insufficient as the sole primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: At least 80% of operational cases use a fixed threshold or stable review budget AND the graph model's precision at that point is lower than the baseline by ≥5 percentage points
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Fewer than 80% of cases use fixed/stable thresholds OR the graph model's precision deficit is ≤5 percentage points at the stable operating point

hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates alert graph context, asset criticality, and recent incident-response labels will prioritize true incident alerts better than a model that ranks using alert content and vendor severity scores alone, as measured by precision at the top-K alerts per analyst shift.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
Use 18 months of historical SOC data from the enterprise SIEM/EDR environment, joined across alert telemetry, analyst disposition logs, incident-response tickets, asset inventory, and containment records. The target should reflect operationally meaningful relevance rather than raw analyst closure status, so define a positive item as an alert that is escalated to or linked with a confirmed incident within 72 hours of alert creation. This avoids treating workflow-specific benign closures as the truth signal. Because analyst labels can lag alert time, assign labels only from downstream events inside that post-alert window and drop any examples whose labeling window would cross a partition boundary.

Split the data strictly by time: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the correct deployment-faithful choice because the model will rank newly arriving alerts using only information available at fire time, and the alert stream, analyst behavior, and attacker activity all drift over time. Group ranking examples by analyst shift so each query corresponds to the set of alerts competing for attention in an actual review period. Leakage controls are straightforward: all graph features, recency counts, and incident-history aggregates are computed with left-closed windows ending at the alert timestamp; preprocessing statistics are fit on training only and reused unchanged downstream.

Train a LambdaMART ranker. The baseline should not be weak: use the same ranking model class, same query grouping, and the same hyperparameter budget, but restrict inputs to alert content and vendor severity family features. This makes the comparison answer the intended question—whether graph context, asset criticality, and recent IR labels add value beyond the information analysts already get from the alert itself. Include two ablations as controls: one that adds asset criticality without graph context, and one that adds recent label-history features without graph structure. Those isolate whether any gain is coming from obvious operational priors rather than the relational context claim in the hypothesis.

Construct baseline features from rule signature, source product, vendor severity, MITRE tags, host and user metadata, and cyclical time features, plus compact text or categorical representations of alert content. The proposed model adds graph-derived features over a 30-day pre-alert entity graph: node degree, repeated co-occurrence counts, neighborhood incident rates, prior containment on related entities, and asset criticality/ownership tier. Recent incident-response labels are aggregated only from events observed before the current alert.

Evaluate primarily with Precision@K, where K equals the median number of alerts an analyst reviews in the first 30 minutes of a shift. That aligns the metric to the first-screen prioritization problem SOC teams actually face. Report NDCG@K, Recall@K, and MRR as secondary views of ranking quality. Select hyperparameters on the validation block using mean Precision@K across shifts, then lock the design and score the final comparison on the untouched test period with bootstrap confidence intervals over shifts. Also report results stratified by source product and asset tier to verify that improvements are not concentrated in one detector family or one class of critical assets.

required_empirical_test.condition: Train the same proposed ranker with graph/context features computed over 30-day and 90-day lookback windows, keeping all other settings fixed, and compare validation Precision@K averaged across analyst shifts. Compute the difference with bootstrap confidence intervals over shifts to account for variation in daily workloads and analyst attention windows.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by more than 0.02 absolute over the 30-day window on the validation set
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by 0.02 absolute or less over the 30-day window on the validation set
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The point estimate shows improvement but the 90% confidence interval includes the 0.02 threshold

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Train the same proposed ranker with graph/context features computed over 30-day and 90-day lookback windows, keeping all other settings fixed, and compare validation Precision@K averaged across analyst shifts. Compute the difference with bootstrap confidence intervals over shifts to account for variation in daily workloads and analyst attention windows.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by more than 0.02 absolute over the 30-day window on the validation set
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The 90-day window improves Precision@K by 0.02 absolute or less over the 30-day window on the validation set
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The point estimate shows improvement but the 90% confidence interval includes the 0.02 threshold

hypothesis: For enterprise email security, a transformer model fine-tuned on message text plus header and URL features will detect spear-phishing emails better than a gradient-boosted baseline using only engineered metadata, measured by PR-AUC on a temporally held-out test set.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
Use 12 months of inbound mail-gateway data from a single enterprise and join each message to the telemetry available at receipt time: raw subject/body text, routing and authentication headers, sender and recipient metadata, URL and attachment indicators, and point-in-time sender/domain/IP reputation logs. Define positives as emails confirmed malicious or spear-phishing by analysts within 14 days of receipt. Define negatives from messages explicitly resolved as benign or bulk/spam and never later escalated. Exclude unlabeled or weakly adjudicated traffic from supervised training so the comparison is driven by reliable outcomes.

Because attacker tactics and local mail policy drift over time, split strictly by receipt timestamp: train on months 1-8, validate on months 9-10, and reserve months 11-12 as a final future-like test. Before splitting, cluster near-duplicate messages and campaign variants using normalized body similarity, shared URL infrastructure, and subject/header overlap, then assign each cluster wholly to one partition based on its first appearance. This prevents the model from seeing one copy of a phishing wave during training and another in test. All transformations are fit on training only, including tokenization decisions, imputers, and categorical encodings.

Train the proposed model by fine-tuning a pretrained transformer on subject and body text, then concatenate the pooled text representation with structured features from headers, URLs, attachments, sender-recipient history, authentication outcomes, and reputation snapshots. Feed the combined representation through a small classification head. Compare against a strong gradient-boosted tree baseline built on engineered metadata only, excluding free text so the experiment directly tests whether message language plus structured context adds detection value beyond conventional gateway features. Give both models comparable tuning effort on the validation window.

Use PR-AUC as the primary metric on the temporally held-out test set. In this setting, analysts consume a ranked queue of suspicious emails rather than a single fixed decision threshold, and the practical objective is to place truly malicious messages as high in that queue as possible despite extreme class imbalance. PR-AUC therefore captures whether the new model improves triage quality across the range of alert volumes the security team can realistically review. To make the result operationally interpretable, also report recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, precision in the top 500 and top 1000 weekly alerts, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and campaign-level recall after deduplication.

Perform model selection and early stopping only on the validation period, choosing the best checkpoint and hyperparameters by PR-AUC. Touch the test set once at the end. To address confounds, run ablations that remove reputation features and, separately, remove body text so any gain can be attributed more precisely to learned language versus sender cues. Also report slice performance on previously unseen sender domains and previously unseen campaign clusters. This design establishes whether the multimodal transformer gives better future-period phishing ranking than a strong metadata baseline under realistic drift and duplicate-control constraints; it does not by itself prove robustness across organizations with different mail mixes or analyst labeling practices.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the percentage of analyst actions observed in the top 0.5% of alerts from validation scores. Compare transformer and baseline model precision at this fixed budget threshold on the test set: if >80% of analyst actions fall within this budget and the transformer's precision is 2+ percentage points lower than the baseline's, then PR-AUC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Validation data shows >80% of analyst actions occur within the top 0.5% of alerts AND the transformer's precision at this threshold is 2+ percentage points below the baseline
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Validation data shows ≤80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% of alerts OR the transformer's precision at this threshold is within 2 percentage points of the baseline
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Validation data shows exactly 80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% budget AND the transformer's precision difference is exactly 2 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the percentage of analyst actions observed in the top 0.5% of alerts from validation scores. Compare transformer and baseline model precision at this fixed budget threshold on the test set: if >80% of analyst actions fall within this budget and the transformer's precision is 2+ percentage points lower than the baseline's, then PR-AUC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Validation data shows >80% of analyst actions occur within the top 0.5% of alerts AND the transformer's precision at this threshold is 2+ percentage points below the baseline
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Validation data shows ≤80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% of alerts OR the transformer's precision at this threshold is within 2 percentage points of the baseline
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Validation data shows exactly 80% of analyst actions within the top 0.5% budget AND the transformer's precision difference is exactly 2 percentage points

Give both models comparable tuning effort on the validation window.

In this setting, analysts consume a ranked queue of suspicious emails rather than a single fixed decision threshold, and the practical objective is to place truly malicious messages as high in that queue as possible despite extreme class imbalance. PR-AUC therefore captures whether the new model improves triage quality across the range of alert volumes the security team can realistically review. To make the result operationally interpretable, also report recall at fixed alert-volume budgets, precision in the top 500 and top 1000 weekly alerts, AUROC, calibration diagnostics, and campaign-level recall after deduplication.

Perform model selection and early stopping only on the validation period, choosing the best checkpoint and hyperparameters by PR-AUC. Touch the test set once at the end. To address confounds, run ablations that remove reputation features and, separately, remove body text so any gain can be attributed more precisely to learned language versus sender cues. Also report slice performance on previously unseen sender domains and previously unseen campaign clusters. This design establishes whether the multimodal transformer gives better future-period phishing ranking than a strong metadata baseline under realistic drift and duplicate-control constraints; it does not by itself prove robustness across organizations with different mail mixes or analyst labeling practices.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a ranking model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner history will surface the vulnerabilities most likely to be remediated or exploited within 30 days better than a baseline ranking model using scanner severity scores alone.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
You would frame the experiment around newly detected asset-vulnerability findings and evaluate whether richer enterprise context improves prioritization over scanner severity alone. Build the dataset from 18 months of scanner findings joined to CMDB asset metadata, network exposure/topology summaries, patch state, and remediation tickets. The unit of analysis is the first detection of a given asset-vulnerability pair. Repeated scanner observations after first detection are collapsed so the model cannot benefit from later scanner persistence updates. The label is positive if, within 30 days of first detection, the finding is patched, formally suppressed/accepted, or tied to a confirmed exploitation signal; otherwise it is negative. Restrict the cohort to managed endpoints and servers that match the intended deployment scope.

Use a chronological split by first-detection month: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right split for vulnerability management because the scoring problem is explicitly forward-looking: analysts rank fresh findings now and outcomes happen later. Every feature must be timestamp-safe. Compute historical remediation rates, team patch latency, prior CVE-family prevalence, and topology summaries using only data available up to the detection timestamp. Fit imputers, encoders, and any scaling on the training window only, then freeze them for validation and test.

Engineer four feature groups. First, scanner-derived features: severity/CVSS, exploitability flags, finding age, recurrence history, and prior reopen patterns. Second, asset context: asset class, OS family, business criticality, ownership, internet-facing status, and baseline patch compliance. Third, topology/exposure: adjacency counts, segment privilege, reachable vulnerable neighbors, and enclave membership. Fourth, historical remediation behavior available before detection: owner-team median patch latency and asset-level compliance history. Exclude post-detection ticket status, patch timestamps, and any scanner refresh information that occurs after the prediction point.

Train a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank model with pairwise ranking loss on weekly batches of newly detected findings. Compare it against two fair baselines: raw scanner severity ordering and a severity-only regularized logistic model using scanner-native signals. Tune both model families on the same validation window with the same degree of care.

Use mean NDCG@100 across weekly batches as the primary selection and reporting metric. In this workflow, analysts operate from a short weekly queue rather than an exhaustive threshold rule, so the experiment should reward getting the first page of the queue right and penalize burying actionable findings below less urgent ones. Report Precision@25, Precision@100, MAP, and AUROC as secondary diagnostics, with calibration reported if probability outputs are exposed.

Model selection is done strictly on validation. The test window is touched once after architecture, features, and hyperparameters are fixed. To address confounding, report slices by business unit, asset class, and severity band, and compare against baselines that already capture scanner severity and basic criticality. This design can establish whether enterprise context improves prioritization of actionable vulnerabilities in the organization’s actual workflow; it does not, by itself, prove that the model isolates causal exploit risk from organizational remediation policy.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the historical median weekly queue size of remediated/exploited findings across the test period using organizational ticketing or remediation data. If the 95% confidence interval of weekly remediation count falls within ±5% of a stable value (e.g., 24–26 items per week), then evaluate Precision@25 on the test set. Calculate the Precision@25 difference between the candidate model and the best severity-only baseline model.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The historical median weekly queue size is consistent (±5%) AND the candidate model's Precision@25 is lower than the severity-only baseline by >0.03 absolute points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The historical median weekly queue size is inconsistent (no ±5% stability) OR the candidate model's Precision@25 gap is ≤0.03 absolute points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the historical median weekly queue size of remediated/exploited findings across the test period using organizational ticketing or remediation data. If the 95% confidence interval of weekly remediation count falls within ±5% of a stable value (e.g., 24–26 items per week), then evaluate Precision@25 on the test set. Calculate the Precision@25 difference between the candidate model and the best severity-only baseline model.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The historical median weekly queue size is consistent (±5%) AND the candidate model's Precision@25 is lower than the severity-only baseline by >0.03 absolute points
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The historical median weekly queue size is inconsistent (no ±5% stability) OR the candidate model's Precision@25 gap is ≤0.03 absolute points

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity SOC alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that uses incident-response ticket text plus SIEM/EDR alert metadata and historical case outcomes will prioritize true high-severity incidents more effectively than a binary classifier that predicts alert maliciousness and then sorts by predicted probability, as measured by precision@k and mean time-to-triage on held-out future alerts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM/EDR alert streams and incident response tickets
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on 18 months of historical SOC data joined across SIEM/EDR alerts, enrichment records, and incident-response tickets. The unit of prediction is an individual alert at creation time. Labels come from the final linked case outcome, mapped to a three-level relevance target: high-severity confirmed incident, lower-severity confirmed security incident, or non-incident. Because SOC datasets contain repeated alert copies and multi-alert incidents, exact duplicate alerts within a short window should be collapsed and all alerts tied to the same incident kept in the same split.

The split should be strictly chronological: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. This is the right evaluation frame for SOC operations because attacker behavior, detections, and analyst playbooks drift over time; the model should be judged on future queues, not random re-samplings of the past. Leakage is controlled by grouping on incident ID across splits and by constructing historical aggregates only from events preceding each alert timestamp.

Feature construction should combine the information analysts actually have when triaging. From text, use alert titles, rule descriptions, and analyst notes entered before final disposition, represented with TF-IDF unigrams and bigrams. From structured telemetry, include detector family, product source, asset criticality, host role, user risk markers, process ancestry, network destination reputation, MITRE tags, and basic timing fields. Add causal history features such as prior alerts on the same asset or identity, prior incident counts, and recent detector escalation rates. Exclude closure notes, containment actions, and any fields populated after investigation because they encode the outcome.

The proposed model is LambdaMART trained in a pairwise ranking setup, with analyst work queues defined by shift-hour buckets so each training query corresponds to alerts competing for analyst attention at the same time. Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation window. The baseline is a gradient-boosted binary classifier trained on the identical features to predict maliciousness, then sorted by predicted probability. Matching features and tuning budget ensures the comparison tests ranking formulation rather than input richness.

The primary metric is Precision@20 averaged across queues. In this SOC setting, success depends on whether the first page of alerts contains more truly urgent incidents, because analyst attention is scarce and queue head quality matters more than global probability calibration. Report NDCG@20, MRR, Precision@50, and an offline replay estimate of mean time-to-triage as secondary metrics. Model selection is done on validation Precision@20 with NDCG@20 as a tie-breaker, and the test window is touched once after all choices are frozen.

To address confounds, report subgroup performance by detector family, asset criticality, and rule age, and include a no-text ablation. This design will establish whether a ranking objective improves future alert prioritization on observed SOC outcomes under realistic drift. It will not establish a fully unbiased estimate of latent attack risk, since analyst investigation depth influences which cases receive definitive severity labels.

required_empirical_test.condition: Audit 30 days of production triage logs to measure the distribution of alerts-per-queue processed before analyst refresh. If at least 80% of analyst queues fall within 15–25 alerts worked per cycle, Precision@20 is an adequate primary metric. Otherwise, compare the ranking model's precision at the observed operational alert count and score threshold against the baseline under the same threshold.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Fewer than 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, and the ranking model's precision at the observed operational cutoff trails the baseline by >3 percentage points.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: At least 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, or the ranking model's precision at the observed cutoff remains within 3 percentage points of the baseline despite differing queue structures.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Audit 30 days of production triage logs to measure the distribution of alerts-per-queue processed before analyst refresh. If at least 80% of analyst queues fall within 15–25 alerts worked per cycle, Precision@20 is an adequate primary metric. Otherwise, compare the ranking model's precision at the observed operational alert count and score threshold against the baseline under the same threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Fewer than 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, and the ranking model's precision at the observed operational cutoff trails the baseline by >3 percentage points.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: At least 80% of queues fall within 15–25 alerts, or the ranking model's precision at the observed cutoff remains within 3 percentage points of the baseline despite differing queue structures.

hypothesis: For vulnerability management in enterprise endpoint/server fleets, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, scanner findings, and exploitability signals will surface truly urgent remediation items more effectively than ranking by raw CVSS score alone, as measured by prioritized discovery of vulnerabilities that are actually exploited or addressed within the next remediation cycle.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
The experiment should be framed around the actual remediation decision unit: a weekly queue of open vulnerability findings competing for limited analyst attention. Build a 12-month panel by joining scanner outputs, CMDB records, network exposure/topology metadata, exploit intelligence, and remediation tickets at the vulnerability-instance level. A record is created for each asset-CVE finding that is open at the start of a weekly remediation cycle. Labels are assigned from events occurring strictly after that cutoff: a finding is positive if it is confirmed exploited or remediated within the next 30 days, and negative otherwise. This definition reflects practical urgency while keeping feature construction temporally clean.

Use a chronological split by week, with the earliest 70% of cycles for training, the next 15% for validation, and the final 15% for test. This is necessary because scanner observations, exploit feeds, and remediation behavior evolve over time. Keep all findings from the same asset-week in the same split, and derive every feature from information available at cycle start only. Fit imputers, encoders, and normalizers on training data only, then freeze them for validation and test.

Feature engineering should combine four signal families. First, vulnerability-level signals: CVSS components, scanner severity, finding age, persistence across prior scans, software family, and whether the CVE is present in exploit intelligence. Second, asset context: business criticality, owner team, server versus endpoint role, OS generation, patch cadence, and prior remediation responsiveness. Third, exposure/topology: internet exposure, subnet sensitivity, reachability to crown-jewel segments, local degree/centrality summaries, and counts of neighboring exposed assets. Fourth, queue context: number of open findings on the asset, concentration of similar CVEs, and backlog burden for the owning team. Exclude closure fields, future persistence indicators, and any post-cutoff ticket updates.

Train a LambdaMART ranker with query groups defined by remediation cycle and business unit so the model learns the ordering problem security teams actually face. Compare it against two baselines: raw CVSS score ranking, which is the incumbent policy being challenged, and a tuned gradient-boosted pointwise classifier over the same features, ranked by predicted probability. Give the classifier comparable tuning effort so any advantage is attributable to ranking formulation rather than optimization asymmetry.

Use NDCG@100 as the primary metric because operations teams act on a bounded top segment of the queue, and the metric rewards placing the most consequential items early while allowing higher gain for exploit-confirmed positives than for routine remediations. Track Recall@100 for exploit-confirmed findings, MAP@100, MRR, PR-AUC, and top-decile lift as secondary diagnostics. Select models on validation NDCG@100 with early stopping, and touch the test set once at the end.

To address confounds, run ablations that remove CVSS-derived fields, recency features, and asset criticality/exposure variables, then check whether the learned ranker still outperforms CVSS alone. Also report performance by asset class, internet exposure, and business unit to verify that gains are not concentrated only in obviously high-priority segments. The study establishes whether integrated contextual ranking improves prioritization under realistic queue constraints; it does not by itself prove causal reduction in incidents after deployment.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the historical remediation cycle throughput (K) from the training data, then calculate Precision@K and Recall@K for exploit-confirmed findings on the validation set using the proposed model and the CVSS baseline. Also compute NDCG@100 for both models. If the proposed model's NDCG@100 improvement over the baseline exceeds 0.03 while its Precision@K improvement is ≤ 0.01 or negative, NDCG@100 is insufficient as the sole primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: NDCG@100 gain over baseline > 0.03 AND Precision@K gain ≤ 0.01 OR negative
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: NDCG@100 gain over baseline > 0.03 AND Precision@K gain > 0.01

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the historical remediation cycle throughput (K) from the training data, then calculate Precision@K and Recall@K for exploit-confirmed findings on the validation set using the proposed model and the CVSS baseline. Also compute NDCG@100 for both models. If the proposed model's NDCG@100 improvement over the baseline exceeds 0.03 while its Precision@K improvement is ≤ 0.01 or negative, NDCG@100 is insufficient as the sole primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: NDCG@100 gain over baseline > 0.03 AND Precision@K gain ≤ 0.01 OR negative
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: NDCG@100 gain over baseline > 0.03 AND Precision@K gain > 0.01

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner severity signals will prioritize truly exploitable hosts more effectively than ranking by CVSS score alone, yielding higher recall@k for confirmed remediation actions on held-out weeks.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
Build the experiment around weekly ranking episodes, because that is how vulnerability management is actually run. For each week in an 18-month history, create the candidate set of open asset-vulnerability findings visible to the security team at that time. Join scanner outputs, CMDB attributes, network topology summaries, and remediation system events using only information timestamped on or before the scoring week. Define relevance from downstream operational evidence: a finding is relevant if it is confirmed remediated within 21 days of entering the queue or if it is escalated through incident-response validation in that window. This is not a perfect exploitability label, but it is the best decision-proximal outcome available and is aligned with what the team ultimately acts on.

Use a strict chronological split: train on the earliest 12 months, tune on the next 3 months, and reserve the final 3 months for test. This avoids leakage from future patching behavior, future asset classifications, and evolving network state. Any preprocessing that learns from data distribution, including imputation, scaling, or frequency encoding, is fit on the training window only. Graph features such as exposure counts, vulnerable-neighbor counts, and centrality summaries are recomputed per week from the network data available at that point in time.

Train a gradient-boosted learning-to-rank model with weekly query groups so the model directly learns within-week prioritization. The main comparison should be against the operational baseline of sorting by CVSS score, with deterministic vendor-severity tie-breaks. Also include a stronger non-ranking baseline: logistic regression on the same tabular non-graph signals with comparable hyperparameter tuning effort. That establishes whether any gain comes from the richer ranking formulation and nonlinear interactions rather than from simply adding obvious metadata.

Evaluate primarily with NDCG@100. In this setting, the team reviews only a bounded top slice of the weekly queue, and the practical objective is to concentrate the findings most likely to warrant action near the very top, not merely to improve average ordering over the full tail. NDCG@100 captures both truncation and position sensitivity, which is appropriate for analyst workflows with finite triage bandwidth. Report Recall@100, Recall@50, and MAP as secondary metrics so the result can also be interpreted in terms of coverage of acted-on findings.

Select hyperparameters using mean validation NDCG@100 across weeks, then retrain on train plus validation with the chosen settings and score the untouched test weeks once. To address confounding, run ablations for severity-only, asset-only, and asset-plus-exposure inputs, and report stratified results for internet-exposed versus internal assets and for high- versus lower-criticality systems. This experiment will establish whether combining inventory, topology, and scanner signals improves operational prioritization over CVSS alone on future weeks. It will not establish causal exploitability in the absence of action-label bias, so conclusions should remain about prioritization effectiveness under real enterprise workflow constraints.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the coefficient of variation (CV) of historical weekly review depths (K). If ≥80% of weekly remediation actions fall within a fixed K with CV(K) ≤ 0.2, measure the absolute difference between the model's Recall@K and NDCG@100 rankings on validation data. If this difference exceeds 5 percentage points, NDCG@100 alone is an insufficient primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Fixed K stability (CV(K) ≤ 0.2 with ≥80% coverage) and absolute ranking discrepancy >5 percentage points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Either (a) fixed K stability not met or (b) ranking discrepancy ≤5 percentage points under stable K
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Historical K coverage between 75–85% with CV between 0.15–0.25 and ranking discrepancy between 4–6 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the coefficient of variation (CV) of historical weekly review depths (K). If ≥80% of weekly remediation actions fall within a fixed K with CV(K) ≤ 0.2, measure the absolute difference between the model's Recall@K and NDCG@100 rankings on validation data. If this difference exceeds 5 percentage points, NDCG@100 alone is an insufficient primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Fixed K stability (CV(K) ≤ 0.2 with ≥80% coverage) and absolute ranking discrepancy >5 percentage points
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Either (a) fixed K stability not met or (b) ranking discrepancy ≤5 percentage points under stable K
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Historical K coverage between 75–85% with CV between 0.15–0.25 and ranking discrepancy between 4–6 percentage points

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model that combines inbound message text, header features, URL features, and sender-reputation signals will detect phishing emails more accurately than a feature-limited baseline using only metadata and handcrafted rules, especially on novel campaign variants seen in a later time period.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise email security using inbound message text, headers, URLs, and sender reputation logs
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on a 12-month stream of real inbound email telemetry captured at delivery time. The training corpus should include subject and body text, parsed headers, authentication results, URL and attachment indicators, and sender/domain reputation signals that were actually available when the message arrived. Labels should come from confirmed phishing verdicts established through analyst review, incident response, or validated user reports. Because some cases are resolved late, use a 30-day label maturation window and drop unresolved messages rather than forcing noisy negatives. This keeps supervision cleaner while still reflecting realistic class imbalance.

The split should be strictly chronological: months 1-8 for training, 9-10 for validation, and 11-12 for test. That is the right design for this domain because phishing tactics, domains, and message templates drift over time, and a later-period test is the only credible way to assess detection of novel campaigns. To further limit contamination, remove exact duplicates and near-duplicate campaign variants that would otherwise appear across windows through replayed templates or shared URL kits.

Feature construction should mirror what the gateway can know before delivery. For text, encode subject plus normalized body content. For structured inputs, include sender/domain age, authentication pass/fail, header inconsistencies, display-name deception indicators, URL lexical and hosting features, attachment type features, and historical sender aggregates computed only from events prior to the message timestamp. Fit all preprocessing objects—tokenizer, vocabularies, imputers, scalers, and any frequency encoders—on training data only, then freeze them for validation and test. Explicitly exclude analyst verdict fields, case metadata, quarantine actions, and any post-delivery user interaction signals because they can encode the label.

Train a multimodal transformer that produces a text representation from subject/body and fuses it with structured features through a classification head. The fair baseline is not a weak rules engine alone; it should be a competitive metadata-only system, such as gradient-boosted trees over headers, authentication, URL, attachment, and handcrafted rule outputs, tuned with the same validation window and similar search effort. That comparison isolates whether adding content and multimodal modeling improves detection beyond mature metadata-driven defenses.

Use partial AUROC in the region up to 0.5% false-positive rate as the primary metric. In this environment, analysts and gateway policies act on the highest-risk slice of mail, so model quality is determined by how well it orders malicious messages above legitimate mail where only a very small false-positive budget is tolerable. Report recall at 0.1% and 0.5% FPR, PR-AUC, full ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics as secondary views.

Select hyperparameters and early stopping based on the validation window only; touch the test set once at the end. In addition to overall performance, report results on held-out campaign clusters, business units, and language strata. This experiment would establish whether multimodal transformers improve phishing detection under realistic temporal drift and low-FPR operating conditions. It would not by itself establish robustness to future adversarial adaptation beyond the observed test period or guarantee transportability to organizations with materially different mail distributions.

required_empirical_test.condition: Measure the precision of both the transformer and baseline models at a single operational threshold (determined by the enterprise's deployed false-positive rate budget on the validation set) on the held-out test set. Compute the precision difference (transformer precision minus baseline precision) at this threshold.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer precision at the operational threshold is ≤ baseline precision (precision_diff ≤ 0) despite higher partial AUROC, or precision_diff ≤ 5 percentage points while operational success depends on threshold-specific blocking
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer precision is ≥ baseline precision + 5 percentage points at the operational threshold on the test set, validating the partial AUROC-driven prioritization
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer precision is > baseline but ≤ +5 percentage points, indicating marginal gain insufficient to conclusively resolve the dispute

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Measure the precision of both the transformer and baseline models at a single operational threshold (determined by the enterprise's deployed false-positive rate budget on the validation set) on the held-out test set. Compute the precision difference (transformer precision minus baseline precision) at this threshold.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Transformer precision at the operational threshold is ≤ baseline precision (precision_diff ≤ 0) despite higher partial AUROC, or precision_diff ≤ 5 percentage points while operational success depends on threshold-specific blocking
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Transformer precision is ≥ baseline precision + 5 percentage points at the operational threshold on the test set, validating the partial AUROC-driven prioritization
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Transformer precision is > baseline but ≤ +5 percentage points, indicating marginal gain insufficient to conclusively resolve the dispute

sound_design_reference:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on a 12-month stream of real inbound email telemetry captured at delivery time. The training corpus should include subject and body text, parsed headers, authentication results, URL and attachment indicators, and sender/domain reputation signals that were actually available when the message arrived. Labels should come from confirmed phishing verdicts established through analyst review, incident response, or validated user reports. Because some cases are resolved late, use a 30-day label maturation window and drop unresolved messages rather than forcing noisy negatives. This keeps supervision cleaner while still reflecting realistic class imbalance.

The split should be strictly chronological: months 1-8 for training, 9-10 for validation, and 11-12 for test. That is the right design for this domain because phishing tactics, domains, and message templates drift over time, and a later-period test is the only credible way to assess detection of novel campaigns. To further limit contamination, remove exact duplicates and near-duplicate campaign variants that would otherwise appear across windows through replayed templates or shared URL kits.

Feature construction should mirror what the gateway can know before delivery. For text, encode subject plus normalized body content. For structured inputs, include sender/domain age, authentication pass/fail, header inconsistencies, display-name deception indicators, URL lexical and hosting features, attachment type features, and historical sender aggregates computed only from events prior to the message timestamp. Fit all preprocessing objects—tokenizer, vocabularies, imputers, scalers, and any frequency encoders—on training data only, then freeze them for validation and test. Explicitly exclude analyst verdict fields, case metadata, quarantine actions, and any post-delivery user interaction signals because they can encode the label.

Train a multimodal transformer that produces a text representation from subject/body and fuses it with structured features through a classification head. The fair baseline is not a weak rules engine alone; it should be a competitive metadata-only system, such as gradient-boosted trees over headers, authentication, URL, attachment, and handcrafted rule outputs, tuned with the same validation window and similar search effort. That comparison isolates whether adding content and multimodal modeling improves detection beyond mature metadata-driven defenses.

Use partial AUROC in the region up to 0.5% false-positive rate as the primary metric. In this environment, analysts and gateway policies act on the highest-risk slice of mail, so model quality is determined by how well it orders malicious messages above legitimate mail where only a very small false-positive budget is tolerable. Report recall at 0.1% and 0.5% FPR, PR-AUC, full ROC-AUC, and calibration diagnostics as secondary views.

Select hyperparameters and early stopping based on the validation window only; touch the test set once at the end. In addition to overall performance, report results on held-out campaign clusters, business units, and language strata. This experiment would establish whether multimodal transformers improve phishing detection under realistic temporal drift and low-FPR operating conditions. It would not by itself establish robustness to future adversarial adaptation beyond the observed test period or guarantee transportability to organizations with materially different mail distributions.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a ranking model that combines SIEM/EDR alert features with incident response ticket text and historical analyst dispositions will surface true incidents higher in the queue than a feature-only gradient-boosted classifier, improving top-k precision and reducing mean time to investigation.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM alerts, endpoint telemetry, and incident response tickets from a mid-to-large enterprise
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on two years of enterprise SOC data integrating SIEM alerts, EDR telemetry, and case-management records. The prediction unit is the individual alert, but all alerts linked to the same investigation case are treated as a dependency group throughout the pipeline. Ground-truth relevance is defined by downstream incident confirmation: alerts tied to cases that end in a confirmed security incident, or that are escalated into a confirmed incident workflow within seven days, are relevant. Alerts with unresolved final status are excluded from supervised training so the target reflects operationally meaningful incidents rather than analyst uncertainty.

Use a chronological split by alert creation time, with case-level grouping to prevent leakage across partitions. Train on months 1-16, validate on months 17-20, and test on months 21-24. This mirrors the real deployment problem: the model ranks incoming alerts using only information available before those alerts arrive. It also avoids contamination from repeated cases, reopened investigations, or ticket-thread artifacts that could otherwise let the model memorize outcomes across splits.

Feature construction should combine three sources of signal. First, build structured alert features from SIEM/EDR data: rule name, product, severity, tactic/technique tags, host criticality, user risk tier, entity counts, and rolling counts of related alerts in trailing windows. Second, encode linked ticket text that is available at ranking time using TF-IDF n-grams plus a compact semantic embedding, capturing analyst-described context that does not exist in raw telemetry alone. Third, add historical analyst disposition statistics, such as prior escalation rates for the same rule, host class, or user segment, computed strictly from events preceding the alert timestamp. Exclude closure codes, final disposition fields, post-triage comments, and any timestamps created after the initial queueing event.

Train a LambdaMART ranking model over daily operational queue groups, using the full structured-plus-text feature set. Compare it against a feature-only gradient-boosted classifier trained on the same alert and telemetry features but without ticket text or historical disposition context. Give both approaches comparable tuning effort on the validation period so the experiment tests whether richer context improves prioritization, not whether one model received more optimization.

Use NDCG@20 as the primary metric because analysts act on a ranked queue and most operational value is concentrated in the first screenful of alerts; the metric rewards getting true incidents near the top while preserving sensitivity to ordering within those high-value positions. Also report Precision@10, Precision@20, MRR, and a test-period replay estimate of mean time to first investigation. Hyperparameters are selected on the validation window only, with grouped cross-validation inside the training period for coarse search. The test set is touched once at the end, and uncertainty is estimated by bootstrapping case-grouped ranking units.

To address confounds, include alert age, native severity, and source system in all models; report sliced performance by source and severity; and run an ablation removing ticket metadata fields that could simply encode analyst workflow shortcuts. This design establishes whether richer contextual ranking improves operational prioritization under realistic SOC conditions, but it does not by itself prove causal reduction in incident risk outside the measured queueing workflow.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compare the candidate model and baseline model's Precision@10 and NDCG@20 on the held-out test set. For alerts grouped by operational queue day, compute: (1) NDCG@20, (2) Precision@10 where K=10 reflects analysts' historical first-screen review depth. Annotate which alerts analysts reviewed and flagged during deployment if real-world feedback is available.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is ≥2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher (ΔNDCG>0.03 MRR-equivalent improvement).
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is <2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher, or Precision@10 improves.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compare the candidate model and baseline model's Precision@10 and NDCG@20 on the held-out test set. For alerts grouped by operational queue day, compute: (1) NDCG@20, (2) Precision@10 where K=10 reflects analysts' historical first-screen review depth. Annotate which alerts analysts reviewed and flagged during deployment if real-world feedback is available.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is ≥2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher (ΔNDCG>0.03 MRR-equivalent improvement).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Candidate model's Precision@10 is <2 percentage points lower than baseline while NDCG@20 is higher, or Precision@10 improves.
