hypothesis: In enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a ranking model that incorporates asset graph features and recent exploit intelligence will prioritize truly high-risk endpoints more accurately than a model using scanner severity scores alone, improving the top-k capture of critical vulnerabilities within a 7-day remediation window.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on a longitudinal weekly panel of asset-vulnerability observations built from scanner output, inventory, patching records, network topology, and exploit-intelligence feeds over an 18-month period. Each record corresponds to an open vulnerability on an asset at a given scan week. Repeated detections within the same week are deduplicated, and open findings persist until remediation is observed. To avoid training on poorly observed systems, include only endpoints and servers with at least 60 days of prior inventory visibility.

The target should reflect what the remediation team actually cares about: exposures that become urgent within the next 7 days. Label an asset-vulnerability pair positive if it is tied to a confirmed incident on that asset during the window or if it remains unremediated past the organization’s critical-vulnerability SLA while active exploit intelligence is present. This preserves operational relevance while avoiding a label defined purely by patching convenience.

Use a strict chronological split: 12 months train, 3 months validation, 3 months test. In this setting, exploit campaigns, patch behavior, and asset criticality drift materially over time, so the evaluation needs to mirror prospective prioritization. All transformations are fit on training data only. Graph features are recomputed week by week from the network snapshot available at that time, and no post-ranking remediation or incident annotations are allowed into features.

Engineer features from four sources: scanner-native severity and finding history, asset business context, recent exploit intelligence, and graph structure. Useful graph features include degree, approximate betweenness, reachability to internet-exposed nodes, proximity to crown-jewel assets, and local concentration of similar findings. Also include controls for scan cadence and historical remediation latency so the model is not rewarded simply for learning which systems are looked at often.

Train a LambdaMART ranker on weekly candidate sets. Compare it against two baselines: the organization’s current heuristic ranking based on scanner severity, exploit presence, and finding age, and a linear learning-to-rank model trained on the same non-graph engineered features with a comparable tuning budget. This gives both an operational baseline and a fair learned baseline.

Select models using weekly mean NDCG@500 on the validation period. NDCG@500 is the primary metric because remediation teams work through a bounded queue each week, and the main question is whether the most dangerous exposures are moved upward within that actionable slice. Report Recall@100, Recall@500, Precision@100, MRR, and AUCPR as secondary diagnostics. Once hyperparameters and feature sets are fixed, retrain on train plus validation and evaluate once on the held-out test period.

To address confounds, run subgroup checks by asset type, business unit, and internet exposure, and verify that improvements persist after controlling for recency, scan frequency, and team-level patching effects. The result would establish whether graph and exploit-intelligence features improve prioritization quality for this enterprise remediation workflow; it would not by itself prove causal exploit prediction outside this environment.

required_empirical_test.condition: Measure the organization's actual weekly vulnerability remediation capacity over the 12 weeks preceding model deployment to determine the median K findings reviewed per week. Recompute model validation rankings by truncating at K and compare Precision@K and Recall@K against the existing NDCG@500 metric. A winning model under NDCG@500 must show that its Precision@K does not trail the top-performing model by more than 5 percentage points at the cutoff.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The current winning model under NDCG@500 scores 5 percentage points lower in Precision@K than the top-performing model at the determined cutoff K.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The current winning model under NDCG@500 scores within 5 percentage points or higher in Precision@K compared to all other evaluated models at the determined cutoff K.
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: If the Precision@K difference between the NDCG@500-selected model and the top-performing model at cutoff K is exactly ±5 percentage points.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Measure the organization's actual weekly vulnerability remediation capacity over the 12 weeks preceding model deployment to determine the median K findings reviewed per week. Recompute model validation rankings by truncating at K and compare Precision@K and Recall@K against the existing NDCG@500 metric. A winning model under NDCG@500 must show that its Precision@K does not trail the top-performing model by more than 5 percentage points at the cutoff.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The current winning model under NDCG@500 scores 5 percentage points lower in Precision@K than the top-performing model at the determined cutoff K.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The current winning model under NDCG@500 scores within 5 percentage points or higher in Precision@K compared to all other evaluated models at the determined cutoff K.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: If the Precision@K difference between the NDCG@500-selected model and the top-performing model at cutoff K is exactly ±5 percentage points.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data will surface the vulnerabilities most likely to be exploited within 30 days better than a severity-only ranking based on CVSS scores.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
To test the hypothesis, build a retrospective ranking dataset from 12 months of vulnerability management operations data. Each example is an asset-vulnerability finding at the time it first appears in a scanner snapshot. Join scanner output to asset inventory, CMDB criticality, exposure metadata, and snapshot-consistent network topology summaries. Define the outcome on a 30-day forward window: a finding is positive if the same vulnerability on the same asset is later confirmed exploited or linked to an incident within 30 days through EDR, SIEM, threat hunting, or incident-response records. Restrict the study to assets with stable telemetry and scanner coverage so negatives are not dominated by simple observability gaps.

Use a chronological split by observation week: 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test. This is the correct deployment-faithful split because ranking decisions are made on newly observed findings, and any random interleaving of weeks would let the model absorb future remediation behavior, incident investigation intensity, or changing scanner policy. All preprocessing is fit on training only. Imputation, categorical encoding, and any scaling are learned on the training period and then applied unchanged to validation and test.

Engineer features from four sources: vulnerability attributes, asset context, finding state, and network topology. Include CVSS components, scanner severity, vulnerability age, exploit-publication flags if available, asset OS and role, business criticality, external exposure, prior patch latency by asset class, local vulnerability density, and graph summaries such as connectivity, segment exposure, and neighborhood risk concentration. Exclude any feature created after ranking time, especially remediation ticket status, analyst annotations, and incident workflow fields.

Train a LambdaMART learning-to-rank model with observation week as the ranking group. This matches the operational problem: the security team reviews a bounded weekly queue and needs the highest-risk findings surfaced first. Compare against a severity-only CVSS ranking, which is the practical incumbent, and against a logistic regression baseline limited to CVSS-derived vulnerability fields so the comparison is not merely “model versus heuristic” but “enterprise context versus severity-only information.” Give both learned models equivalent validation-based tuning effort.

Select hyperparameters on the validation period using mean NDCG@100 across weeks, with early stopping and a modest search over tree depth, learning rate, and leaf size. NDCG@100 is the primary metric because remediation capacity is finite and the operational question is whether the top review set contains the findings most likely to be exploited soon. Also report Precision@25, Recall@100, MAP, and AUCPR for supplementary visibility into extreme-top performance and class imbalance.

Hold the test set untouched until the end, then report final performance with bootstrap confidence intervals over weeks. To address confounds, repeat evaluation within internet-exposed vs internal assets, high- vs lower-criticality assets, and servers vs endpoints, and run feature-family ablations for recency, exposure, and criticality. This experiment will show whether adding enterprise context improves prioritization beyond CVSS-centric ranking under realistic operational constraints.

required_empirical_test.condition: Analyze historical remediation logs to identify the consistent weekly action cutoff K (80th percentile of weekly remediation counts). If 20 ≤ K ≤ 30, evaluate both models on Precision@K on the final 15% test set. If the Precision@K difference between the top two models exceeds 0.05 absolute points, NDCG@100 is insufficient.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: When K ∈ [20,30], the model with higher NDCG@100 has Precision@K ≤ 0.05 below the alternative
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: When K ∈ [20,30], the model with higher NDCG@100 has Precision@K ≥ 0.05 above the alternative

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Analyze historical remediation logs to identify the consistent weekly action cutoff K (80th percentile of weekly remediation counts). If 20 ≤ K ≤ 30, evaluate both models on Precision@K on the final 15% test set. If the Precision@K difference between the top two models exceeds 0.05 absolute points, NDCG@100 is insufficient.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: When K ∈ [20,30], the model with higher NDCG@100 has Precision@K ≤ 0.05 below the alternative
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: When K ∈ [20,30], the model with higher NDCG@100 has Precision@K ≥ 0.05 above the alternative

This matches the operational problem: the security team reviews a bounded weekly queue and needs the highest-risk findings surfaced first. Compare against a severity-only CVSS ranking, which is the practical incumbent, and against a logistic regression baseline limited to CVSS-derived vulnerability fields so the comparison is not merely “model versus heuristic” but “enterprise context versus severity-only information.” Give both learned models equivalent validation-based tuning effort.

Select hyperparameters on the validation period using mean NDCG@100 across weeks, with early stopping and a modest search over tree depth, learning rate, and leaf size. NDCG@100 is the primary metric because remediation capacity is finite and the operational question is whether the top review set contains the findings most likely to be exploited soon. hypothesis: A graph neural network trained on account-network transaction relationships and merchant/entity links will detect retail banking anti-money-laundering cases with higher precision at the same recall than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline using only per-account transaction aggregates.
domain: Retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring using transaction streams, merchant metadata, and account-network relationships
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis at the account-month level, which matches how an AML monitoring system is typically refreshed and queued for investigators. Build examples from 36 months of historical data drawn from the transaction ledger, customer and account master data, merchant metadata, relationship tables, and case-management outcomes. To prevent label leakage from delayed investigations, define positives only when an account is linked to a SAR or confirmed AML case within 90 days after the prediction timestamp, and exclude examples whose outcomes are not yet fully matured.

Because both transactions and investigations are time-dependent, split the data chronologically: 24 months for training, 6 months for validation, and 6 months for test, with a 90-day maturation buffer before labels are frozen. Every feature must be computed from information available strictly up to the scoring date. Preprocessing statistics, imputers, encoders, and any normalization parameters are fit on the training period only and then applied unchanged to validation and test.

For the tabular baseline, construct a strong feature set from rolling windows over prior activity: transaction count and value, velocity changes, inbound/outbound asymmetry, cash usage, cross-border share, merchant-category composition, counterparty concentration, unusual timing, prior alert history, and customer/profile risk indicators. For the graph model, use the same node features but place them in a heterogeneous graph containing account, merchant, customer/entity, and beneficiary/counterparty nodes. Add edges for transactions and known relationship links, with edge attributes summarizing recency, frequency, and amount over the same historical windows. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of relational message passing rather than differences in raw information.

Train a heterogeneous GNN with two message-passing layers, residual connections, and class-weighted loss to handle the extreme imbalance. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline tuned with equivalent care on the same validation block. The baseline is intentionally strong because a weaker per-account model would not test whether the gain truly comes from network structure.

Use area under the precision-recall curve as the primary metric. In this setting the bank reviews a ranked queue of accounts, and the practical objective is to push more true suspicious cases toward the top of that queue without inflating the long tail of false alerts. AUPRC captures that ranking quality under severe imbalance better than accuracy or AUROC. Also report precision at several recall levels, recall at investigator-capacity cutoffs, and calibration metrics to make deployment implications visible.

Select hyperparameters and stop training using validation AUPRC only, then run the locked comparison once on the untouched test period. To address confounds, include ablations that remove relationship edges or collapse graph information into simple summary counts, and report subgroup performance across products, geographies, and account tenure. This design establishes whether explicit relational modeling adds incremental detection value over a feature-matched tabular system under realistic temporal evaluation. It does not establish causal drivers of suspicious behavior, and it remains limited by imperfect labels and unlabeled suspicious accounts that were never investigated.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute absolute precision difference between the AUPRC-optimized GNN and the best-performing model at two operational thresholds on the test set: (a) investigator capacity cutoff (top K accounts per day, K=average daily reviewed accounts in prior 12 months) and (b) regulatory recall target (Precision@Recall=0.7). The test compares these precision values for both thresholds and checks if either difference exceeds -0.03 (i.e., the AUPRC model's precision is more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at each threshold).
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is worse by more than 3 percentage points at either operational threshold compared to the best-performing model for that threshold
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is no more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at both thresholds

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute absolute precision difference between the AUPRC-optimized GNN and the best-performing model at two operational thresholds on the test set: (a) investigator capacity cutoff (top K accounts per day, K=average daily reviewed accounts in prior 12 months) and (b) regulatory recall target (Precision@Recall=0.7). The test compares these precision values for both thresholds and checks if either difference exceeds -0.03 (i.e., the AUPRC model's precision is more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at each threshold).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is worse by more than 3 percentage points at either operational threshold compared to the best-performing model for that threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The AUPRC-optimized model's precision is no more than 3 percentage points worse than the operationally optimal model at both thresholds

sound_design_reference:
You would evaluate the hypothesis at the account-month level, which matches how an AML monitoring system is typically refreshed and queued for investigators. Build examples from 36 months of historical data drawn from the transaction ledger, customer and account master data, merchant metadata, relationship tables, and case-management outcomes. To prevent label leakage from delayed investigations, define positives only when an account is linked to a SAR or confirmed AML case within 90 days after the prediction timestamp, and exclude examples whose outcomes are not yet fully matured.

Because both transactions and investigations are time-dependent, split the data chronologically: 24 months for training, 6 months for validation, and 6 months for test, with a 90-day maturation buffer before labels are frozen. Every feature must be computed from information available strictly up to the scoring date. Preprocessing statistics, imputers, encoders, and any normalization parameters are fit on the training period only and then applied unchanged to validation and test.

For the tabular baseline, construct a strong feature set from rolling windows over prior activity: transaction count and value, velocity changes, inbound/outbound asymmetry, cash usage, cross-border share, merchant-category composition, counterparty concentration, unusual timing, prior alert history, and customer/profile risk indicators. For the graph model, use the same node features but place them in a heterogeneous graph containing account, merchant, customer/entity, and beneficiary/counterparty nodes. Add edges for transactions and known relationship links, with edge attributes summarizing recency, frequency, and amount over the same historical windows. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of relational message passing rather than differences in raw information.

Train a heterogeneous GNN with two message-passing layers, residual connections, and class-weighted loss to handle the extreme imbalance. Compare it against a gradient-boosted tree baseline tuned with equivalent care on the same validation block. The baseline is intentionally strong because a weaker per-account model would not test whether the gain truly comes from network structure.

Use area under the precision-recall curve as the primary metric. In this setting the bank reviews a ranked queue of accounts, and the practical objective is to push more true suspicious cases toward the top of that queue without inflating the long tail of false alerts. AUPRC captures that ranking quality under severe imbalance better than accuracy or AUROC. Also report precision at several recall levels, recall at investigator-capacity cutoffs, and calibration metrics to make deployment implications visible.

Select hyperparameters and stop training using validation AUPRC only, then run the locked comparison once on the untouched test period. To address confounds, include ablations that remove relationship edges or collapse graph information into simple summary counts, and report subgroup performance across products, geographies, and account tenure. This design establishes whether explicit relational modeling adds incremental detection value over a feature-matched tabular system under realistic temporal evaluation. It does not establish causal drivers of suspicious behavior, and it remains limited by imperfect labels and unlabeled suspicious accounts that were never investigated.

hypothesis: For a large hospital network’s emergency department triage workflow, a model that combines structured EHR variables with chief complaint text embeddings will predict 72-hour ED revisit risk more accurately than a structured-data-only baseline, yielding a higher AUROC and better calibration on a temporally held-out future month.
domain: Healthcare / emergency department operations using triage records, free-text chief complaints, and prior encounter history from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
Use a retrospective cohort of discharged ED visits from the health system’s last 36 months of data. Restrict the cohort to encounters where the model could realistically be used in triage-to-discharge workflow: visits with a completed triage record, a valid chief complaint field, and a final disposition of discharge. Define the label as a return ED visit anywhere in the same network within 72 hours of discharge from the index encounter. Exclude admissions, transfers, deaths, and visits leaving before evaluation because the intervention target is discharged patients.

Respect the stated deployment setting with a strict chronological split: 28 months train, 4 months validation, 4 months test. This directly addresses temporal drift in revisit behavior, documentation practice, and operational policy. All preprocessing is fit on training data only. Missing structured values are imputed within the pipeline; continuous variables are scaled only where needed; categorical encodings and any text normalization rules are frozen after training. Patient history features are computed using data available strictly before the index visit, and no post-discharge orders, diagnosis finalization fields, or utilization events occurring after the prediction timestamp are included.

Construct the structured feature set from variables available by the end of triage or early visit flow: demographics, arrival mode, triage vitals, triage acuity, insurance, site, visit time context, prior ED and inpatient utilization counts, prior diagnosis group indicators already on record, and medication burden. For text, generate fixed dense embeddings from the raw chief complaint string after lowercasing, punctuation cleanup, and removal of obvious templated boilerplate. Include complaint length as a separate covariate to control for verbosity effects.

Train a gradient-boosted tree model on the combined structured and text features. Use the same model family for the baseline, differing only by removal of text embeddings. That keeps the comparison focused on whether chief complaint text contributes incremental predictive information beyond structured triage and utilization history rather than conflating feature modality with model class. Run matched hyperparameter searches for both models on the validation set. After selecting the best configuration, fit isotonic calibration on validation predictions for each model before final test evaluation.

Use AUROC as the primary metric on the temporally held-out final 4 months. In this workflow, the score is used to rank discharged patients for limited follow-up resources whose daily cutoff moves with staffing and census, so global ordering of risk is the core requirement. Also report AUPRC because revisits are uncommon, along with Brier score, calibration slope/intercept, expected calibration error, and sensitivity/PPV in the top risk decile to show whether improved ranking is accompanied by usable probability estimates.

Control for known confounds by including site indicators, reporting performance by hospital and by month, and checking whether any text-related gain remains within site rather than being driven by coding conventions. The experiment establishes whether chief complaint embeddings add predictive value in this health system under future-month deployment conditions; it does not, by itself, establish causal effects on revisit reduction or generalization to out-of-network returns.

required_empirical_test.condition: Analyze operational logs from the pilot implementation phase to determine if at least 80% of intervention days use a stable review capacity within +/-2 percentage points of a single cutoff. If this operational stability threshold is met, compute PPV at this fixed threshold (e.g., top 10% decile) on the test set and verify whether the structured-plus-text model improves PPV by at least 0.02 absolute compared to the baseline.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The pilot logs show a stable operating cutoff meeting the 80% threshold AND the PPV improvement is less than 0.02
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Either (a) the pilot logs show inconsistent operating thresholds (less than 80% stable), OR (b) PPV improves by ≥0.02 at the stable threshold
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: The pilot logs show exactly 80% stability with PPV improvement of exactly 0.02

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Analyze operational logs from the pilot implementation phase to determine if at least 80% of intervention days use a stable review capacity within +/-2 percentage points of a single cutoff. If this operational stability threshold is met, compute PPV at this fixed threshold (e.g., top 10% decile) on the test set and verify whether the structured-plus-text model improves PPV by at least 0.02 absolute compared to the baseline.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The pilot logs show a stable operating cutoff meeting the 80% threshold AND the PPV improvement is less than 0.02
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Either (a) the pilot logs show inconsistent operating thresholds (less than 80% stable), OR (b) PPV improves by ≥0.02 at the stable threshold
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The pilot logs show exactly 80% stability with PPV improvement of exactly 0.02

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a learning-to-rank model trained on SIEM/EDR alerts plus incident-response feedback will prioritize true incident-causing alerts more effectively than a binary alert classifier with a fixed threshold, improving top-k precision and analyst time-to-resolution.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM/EDR alert streams and incident response tickets
task_prompt: Use a retrospective point-in-time study over 18 months of enterprise SOC alert data joined from SIEM, EDR, case-management, and incident-response systems. Build the supervised set at the alert level, but preserve the operational structure of analyst review queues. Labels come from downstream investigation outcomes: alerts linked to confirmed incidents are treated as highest relevance, alerts investigated as suspicious but not escalated receive intermediate relevance, and benign/false-positive closures receive zero relevance. Because many alerts are never reviewed, restrict primary training and evaluation to alerts with explicit analyst or incident outcomes; keep unreviewed alerts out of the supervised benchmark to avoid silently treating unknowns as negatives. Split the data chronologically to respect real drift in attacker behavior, infrastructure, and detection content. Use months 1–12 for training, 13–15 for validation, and 16–18 for test. Keep all alerts tied to the same case or incident in one partition, and compute every aggregate feature in a strict point-in-time fashion so no future analyst action or future incident linkage can leak backward. Engineer features from information available at alert creation: detector metadata, vendor severity, source product, MITRE tags, host and user context, asset criticality, recent alert burst statistics by entity and rule, prior incident involvement up to that timestamp, and textual representations of alert title/description. Exclude any post-triage annotations, ticket routing states, and fields only populated after investigation begins. Train a LambdaMART learning-to-rank model, grouping examples by analyst queue-day so the learner optimizes ordering within realistic daily review batches. This directly matches the operating problem: analysts consume a ranked queue, not isolated probability estimates. For a fair test of the hypothesis, compare it against a gradient-boosted binary classifier trained on the identical feature set, with the same temporal partitions and equivalent tuning effort. The classifier’s scores are converted into a ranking at evaluation time, making the comparison about objective formulation rather than model family or feature advantage. Use NDCG@20 as the primary metric, averaged across queue-days. In this setting the SOC does not need a single hard cutoff as much as it needs the most consequential alerts concentrated near the top of a limited review list, and the graded label structure reflects that a confirmed incident is more valuable than a merely suspicious alert. Report Precision@5, Precision@20, MAP, and AUCPR as secondary views, and estimate operational impact with a replay analysis that measures median time from alert generation to first confirmed incident handling under a fixed analyst review budget. Perform model selection on the validation period only, using NDCG@20. Use grouped, chronology-preserving cross-validation inside the training window to narrow hyperparameters, then retrain on train plus validation only after the configuration is fixed. Touch the test set once at the end and report bootstrap confidence intervals over queue-days. Also provide stratified results by detector family, alert source, and asset criticality, plus a held-out slice for rules first introduced in the test period to check that any ranking gain survives realistic drift.

required_empirical_test.condition: Identify the operational alert-action cutoff in the SOC workflow on the held-out test set by analyzing replayed analyst review logs: determine whether analysts prioritize the top-K alerts per queue-day (median K ≤ 20) or escalate alerts exceeding a fixed score threshold. For the identified cutoff (K or threshold), compute precision of confirmed incident alerts at that operational point for both models (ranking vs. classifier).
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the absolute precision gap between models at the cutoff exceeds 0.05 (e.g., classifier >0.05 higher precision while NDCG@20 remains comparable), NDCG@20 alone fails to capture critical operational differences
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the absolute precision gap at the cutoff is ≤ 0.05, NDCG@20 sufficiently captures prioritization effectiveness regardless of operational workflow
required_empirical_test.ambiguous_if: If the median queue-day alert volume is below 10 or multiple distinct cutoff regimes exist (e.g., both top-K and score-based thresholds apply), making strict precision evaluation non-definitive

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Identify the operational alert-action cutoff in the SOC workflow on the held-out test set by analyzing replayed analyst review logs: determine whether analysts prioritize the top-K alerts per queue-day (median K ≤ 20) or escalate alerts exceeding a fixed score threshold. For the identified cutoff (K or threshold), compute precision of confirmed incident alerts at that operational point for both models (ranking vs. classifier).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the absolute precision gap between models at the cutoff exceeds 0.05 (e.g., classifier >0.05 higher precision while NDCG@20 remains comparable), NDCG@20 alone fails to capture critical operational differences
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the absolute precision gap at the cutoff is ≤ 0.05, NDCG@20 sufficiently captures prioritization effectiveness regardless of operational workflow
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: If the median queue-day alert volume is below 10 or multiple distinct cutoff regimes exist (e.g., both top-K and score-based thresholds apply), making strict precision evaluation non-definitive

hypothesis: For enterprise software repositories, a model that combines commit diffs, code-review comments, and dependency-change graphs will detect supply-chain security vulnerabilities better than a baseline trained only on static repository metadata, as measured by AUROC on future vulnerable commits from later time periods.
domain: Software supply-chain security analytics for enterprise code repositories and dependency management systems
task_prompt:
You would frame the experiment around the actual decision point: scoring a commit or pull request before release using only information available at that time. Assemble four years of timestamped repository history, PR and review logs, dependency manifests and lockfiles, dependency-resolution graphs reconstructed per change, CI scan outputs, and confirmed security findings. The unit of prediction should be the commit or PR that introduced the change. A positive label is assigned when that change is later confirmed as the introduction point for a supply-chain security issue. Because security labels arrive late, impose a 90-day maturation window and drop changes too recent to have had a realistic chance of being discovered; this avoids contaminating negatives with unresolved incidents.

Use a strict chronological split: the earliest 70% of matured examples for training, the next 15% for validation, and the final 15% for test. Keep all commits belonging to the same PR together. This split is necessary because the deployment question is explicitly about future vulnerable changes, and both labels and ecosystem state evolve over time. Any preprocessing artifact that learns from data distribution—text vocabularies, imputers, scaling constants, category maps, feature selection thresholds—must be fit on training only and then frozen.

Construct two model conditions on the identical example set. The baseline uses only static repository metadata and operational context: repository age, language mix, author/reviewer counts, churn, paths touched, historical build status, dependency counts, package ecosystem, and prior alert history. The proposed model adds three signal families: commit diff features, code-review comment features, and dependency-change graph features. For commit diffs and review comments, derive compact text embeddings plus interpretable statistics such as token risk lexicons, edit size, and discussion intensity. For dependency graphs, compute features tied to supply-chain exposure: new package introductions, transitive dependency reachability, centrality shifts, maintainer concentration proxies, version-jump magnitude, and known-risk indicators available at decision time. Feed all modalities into a late-fusion gradient-boosted tree classifier so interactions among metadata, text, and graph signals can be learned without giving the richer model an unfair tuning advantage.

Tune both baseline and full model with the same validation-set search budget and class-weighting strategy. Select the best configuration by validation AUROC, then evaluate once on the untouched future test period. AUROC is the primary metric because the operational workflow is score-based triage: different business units review different fractions of incoming changes, so the model must preserve a useful risk ordering across a range of queue capacities rather than optimize only a single cutoff. Because the data are imbalanced, also report AUPRC, recall at top-k review budgets, precision at the current team capacity threshold, and calibration via Brier score.

To address confounds, include repository identity and age signals in both models, report per-repository and per-ecosystem breakdowns, and run an additional repository-held-out analysis. The experiment establishes whether multi-modal change information improves future vulnerability ranking beyond metadata alone; it does not by itself prove causal mechanisms or guarantee transfer to organizations with substantially different review practices or package ecosystems.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute precision and recall at the historical median review queue budget (e.g., top 5% of daily scored changes) for both models on the test set. Compare the full model's precision and recall at this threshold against the baseline. If the full model's precision at the threshold is less than 2 percentage points higher than the baseline and its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points, then AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is not at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline AND its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline OR its recall gain is at least 5 percentage points

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute precision and recall at the historical median review queue budget (e.g., top 5% of daily scored changes) for both models on the test set. Compare the full model's precision and recall at this threshold against the baseline. If the full model's precision at the threshold is less than 2 percentage points higher than the baseline and its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points, then AUROC alone is insufficient as the primary metric.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is not at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline AND its recall gain is less than 5 percentage points
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The full model's precision at the operational threshold is at least 2 percentage points higher than the baseline OR its recall gain is at least 5 percentage points

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity vulnerability management, a learning-to-rank model that combines asset inventory, network topology, and scanner findings will identify the subset of vulnerabilities most likely to be remediated within 14 days better than a baseline ranked only by severity score (e.g., CVSS), as measured by top-k precision on historical remediation actions.
domain: Cybersecurity vulnerability management for enterprise endpoint and server fleets using asset inventory, network topology, and vulnerability scanner data
task_prompt:
You would evaluate the hypothesis on an 18-month extract from the enterprise vulnerability scanner, CMDB, network exposure data, and remediation ticketing/patch records. The prediction target is defined at the moment a vulnerability finding first appears on an asset after being absent on the prior clean scan. Each example is labeled positive when the finding is remediated within 14 days, verified through ticket closure plus either scanner disappearance or a patch event. Instances lacking a full 14-day observation window are dropped so the label is well defined.

The experiment uses a single decision-time snapshot per finding instance rather than every repeated scan row. This keeps the unit of prediction aligned with how analysts actually prioritize work and avoids inflating sample size with near-duplicate observations. Train, validation, and test sets are created with a stratified random split over finding instances, preserving the overall 14-day remediation rate and maintaining similar mixes of business unit and asset class across splits. Leakage is controlled by excluding any feature updated after ranking time, including later ticket states, subsequent scan results, and post hoc patch metadata.

Feature construction combines the operational signals security teams already use with structural context they usually do not. Vulnerability-level features include CVSS components, exploitability flags, vulnerability age, recurrence on the same asset, and patch availability. Asset-level features include criticality, platform, role, maintenance window, ownership, and backlog size. Topology features summarize network exposure and reachability, such as internet exposure, count of externally reachable paths, subnet centrality, and lateral-movement adjacency counts. Historical team throughput features are computed only from data available prior to the ranking date. All preprocessing, including imputation and encoding, is fit on training data only, then frozen for validation and test.

The proposed model is LambdaMART, trained as a learning-to-rank system over weekly remediation queues. That is the right model class for the hypothesis because the question is not whether an individual finding will eventually be fixed, but whether the model can order findings so the likely-to-be-remediated items rise to the top of each team’s actionable queue. The primary baseline is the current operational heuristic: rank by CVSS severity, breaking ties with exploitability and asset criticality. A stronger baseline is a tuned logistic-regression model using the same standard tabular signals but excluding topology-derived features; this tests whether the ranking lift comes specifically from richer context rather than simply moving beyond CVSS.

Model selection is performed only on the validation set using mean Precision@K, where K matches each team’s typical weekly remediation capacity and results are macro-averaged across queues. Secondary reporting includes NDCG@K, MAP@K, and AUCPR. The test set is used once at the end, with clustered bootstrap intervals by asset and queue to account for repeated structure. To address the main confound, you would run sensitivity analyses by owner team and business unit, plus a leave-one-team-out check and an ablation removing ownership features, so the study can distinguish genuine prioritization signal from simply learning which teams patch fastest.

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the autocorrelation of the daily 14-day remediation rate time series at a 14-day lag on the pre-split dataset. If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient exceeds 0.30, chronological splitting is required; if 0.30 or below, stratified random splitting is acceptable.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is greater than 0.30, indicating substantial temporal dependence that would allow leakage of operational patterns from the future into training data.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is 0.30 or below, suggesting the remediation patterns are sufficiently temporally decorrelated to justify random splitting.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the autocorrelation of the daily 14-day remediation rate time series at a 14-day lag on the pre-split dataset. If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient exceeds 0.30, chronological splitting is required; if 0.30 or below, stratified random splitting is acceptable.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is greater than 0.30, indicating substantial temporal dependence that would allow leakage of operational patterns from the future into training data.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: If the lag-14 autocorrelation coefficient is 0.30 or below, suggesting the remediation patterns are sufficiently temporally decorrelated to justify random splitting.

Historical team throughput features are computed only from data available prior to the ranking date. All preprocessing, including imputation and encoding, is fit on training data only, then frozen for validation and test.

The proposed model is LambdaMART, trained as a learning-to-rank system over weekly remediation queues. That is the right model class for the hypothesis because the question is not whether an individual finding will eventually be fixed, but whether the model can order findings so the likely-to-be-remediated items rise to the top of each team’s actionable queue. The primary baseline is the current operational heuristic: rank by CVSS severity, breaking ties with exploitability and asset criticality. A stronger baseline is a tuned logistic-regression model using the same standard tabular signals but excluding topology-derived features; this tests whether the ranking lift comes specifically from richer context rather than simply moving beyond CVSS.

Model selection is performed only on the validation set using mean Precision@K, where K matches each team’s typical weekly remediation capacity and results are macro-averaged across queues. Secondary reporting includes NDCG@K, MAP@K, and AUCPR. The test set is used once at the end, with clustered bootstrap intervals by asset and queue to account for repeated structure. To address the main confound, you would run sensitivity analyses by owner team and business unit, plus a leave-one-team-out check and an ablation removing ownership features, so the study can distinguish genuine prioritization signal from simply learning which teams patch fastest.

hypothesis: Re] Explaining Groups of Points in Low-Dimensional Representations
task_prompt:
The paper proposes Transitive Global Translations (TGT) as a method for explaining differences between clusters in low-dimensional or latent representations of high-dimensional data. Starting from a learned representation space that contains groups of points, the method seeks an explanation in the original feature space that characterizes how one cluster differs from another. The explanation is expressed as a sparse transformation, with particular emphasis on translations affecting only a small number of input features, so that the resulting description remains interpretable.

To generate an explanation, the approach identifies pairs of clusters in the latent space and searches for a transformation in input space such that, when applied to data from one cluster, the transformed examples move toward the other cluster under the representation function. The paper positions TGT against a simpler baseline, Difference Between the Means (DBM), and argues that TGT better captures cluster differences because it uses the representation model and transitivity constraints to produce globally consistent explanations rather than relying only on average feature differences.

The methodology is evaluated on datasets used to form low-dimensional representations, including settings where ground-truth structure is known and settings with real data patterns. Explanations are assessed in terms of how well they recover known differences between groups, how sparse they are, and whether the important features selected remain consistent across different sparsity levels. The paper further claims that TGT can match meaningful patterns present in the data while outperforming DBM, especially when explanations are restricted to few features.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The main method and results were largely reproduced, but the report raises a contestable methodological issue around unspecified cluster selection in latent space that affects evaluation.

hypothesis: Re] Replication study of "Privacy-preserving Collaborative Learning
task_prompt:
The paper studies privacy preservation in federated learning against gradient-based reconstruction attacks. Its proposed approach is to transform each user's local training samples before gradient computation so that shared gradients reveal less recoverable visual detail while still supporting effective model training. Rather than selecting transformations manually, the paper claims to use an automatic search procedure to discover effective transformation policies from a predefined transformation space.

The methodology centers on evaluating candidate transformation policies with two proposed metrics: a privacy-oriented score and an accuracy-oriented score. These metrics are used to guide the search for policies that both hinder image reconstruction attacks and preserve downstream learning performance. The paper claims that the resulting policies should generalize across multiple variants of reconstruction attacks, and that policies found on one dataset may transfer to other datasets.

Experiments are described as comparing reconstruction quality and training behavior with and without the searched policies in collaborative or federated learning settings. The claimed evaluation examines whether reconstruction attacks become ineffective after applying the selected transformations, whether training efficiency and model accuracy remain largely unchanged, and what kinds of transformations are typically favored by the search. The paper further characterizes good policies as those that remove fine-grained details while preserving semantic content relevant for learning.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The main privacy and utility claims were largely reproduced, but the reported relationship between the paper's privacy score and PSNR was not reproduced and outcome variability across images makes some broader conclusions contestable.

hypothesis: Re] On the Reproducibility of \"FairCal: Fairness Calibration for Face Verification\
task_prompt:
The original paper proposes FairCal, a post-hoc calibration method for face verification systems designed to improve both probability calibration and fairness across sensitive groups. The setting is pairwise face verification: a pretrained face recognition model produces similarity scores or embeddings for image pairs, and the goal is to transform these scores into calibrated match probabilities. The method specifically considers ethnicity as a sensitive attribute and evaluates whether calibration can reduce disparities such as unequal false positive rates across groups while maintaining strong overall verification performance.

FairCal operates by partitioning the embedding or score space using an unsupervised clustering procedure, described as K-means clustering, and then learning calibration functions within these discovered regions rather than using a single global calibrator. The intent is to capture local structure in the verification space without explicitly calibrating separately for protected groups. The paper compares FairCal against existing calibration approaches and presents it as a fairness-aware calibration strategy that can produce well-calibrated probabilities while preserving or improving face verification accuracy.

Experiments are conducted on the Balanced Faces in the Wild (BFW) and Racial Faces in the Wild (RFW) datasets using embeddings from pretrained face recognition models. The evaluation focuses on three claimed outcomes: strong global accuracy, improved calibration quality of predicted probabilities, and greater predictive equality measured through false positive rate parity across sensitive attributes. The paper reports state-of-the-art or competitive results on these criteria and argues that unsupervised partition-based calibration is an effective approach for fair face verification.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report indicates the core methodology was largely reproducible in trend, but some claimed experimental details were insufficiently specified and exact results could not be matched due to undocumented preprocessing and missing intermediate artifacts.

hypothesis: Re] A Cluster-based Approach for Improving Isotropy in Contextual Embedding Space
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a local, cluster-based post-processing method for improving the isotropy of contextual word representations produced by pretrained language models such as BERT, RoBERTa, and GPT-2. The motivation is that contextual embeddings occupy an anisotropic, cone-shaped space in which even unrelated tokens can have highly positive correlations, and that these representations exhibit clustered local structure. Instead of applying a single global correction to all embeddings, the method first identifies local clusters in the embedding space and then performs isotropy enhancement within those clusters.

Methodologically, the approach uses contextual embeddings extracted from several transformer models on multiple text datasets. The embeddings are partitioned using clustering, and principal-component-based post-processing is then applied locally to reduce dominant directions within each cluster. The paper compares this cluster-based method against prior global isotropy enhancement approaches, especially the global PCA-based method of Mu and Viswanath, to test whether exploiting local structure yields greater isotropy gains than a single global transformation.

The claimed evaluation includes both intrinsic and downstream analyses. Intrinsically, the paper measures isotropy and correlation structure of the processed contextual embeddings to show that the cluster-based method produces a more uniform embedding space than global baselines. It also includes analyses of structural or syntactic information in the representations, with the claim that local post-processing removes some syntactic information embedded in contextual vectors.

Finally, the paper evaluates whether these representation changes improve semantic NLP performance. Using contextual embeddings from BERT, RoBERTa, and GPT-2, the authors assess semantic tasks after post-processing and argue that the cluster-based method yields better semantic behavior than global isotropy-enhancement methods by both improving isotropy and suppressing non-semantic local structure.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report supports the paper's isotropy-improvement claim but contests the consistency of the claimed semantic-task gains and some structural-analysis conclusions.

hypothesis: Re] Reproducibility report of \"Interpretable Complex-Valued Neural Networks for Privacy Protection\
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a privacy-preserving deep learning pipeline in which a client device computes an intermediate representation of an input locally, encrypts that representation, and sends it to a cloud-side model for the remaining computation. The key methodological idea is to use complex-valued neural networks so that intermediate features can be transformed in a way that preserves utility for classification while making inversion or private-property inference more difficult for an adversary. The approach extends standard DNNs with complex-valued operations and modified rotation-invariant modules such as activation and pooling layers.

In the claimed setup, the local model acts as an encoder that produces intermediate features from the original input. These features are then encrypted before being processed by a complex-valued network in the cloud. The paper evaluates whether this design improves privacy relative to baseline methods by testing adversarial reconstruction or inference attacks on the transmitted intermediate features. Privacy protection is assessed by the increased difficulty of recovering the original inputs or sensitive properties from those representations.

At the same time, the method is intended to maintain predictive performance. The paper therefore compares the privacy-protecting complex-valued DNN against baselines on downstream classification accuracy, using standard architectures such as LeNet- and ResNet-based components and openly available datasets. The central claims are that the complex-valued encrypted-feature pipeline makes adversarial input inference harder than baseline approaches while largely preserving task accuracy.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report disputes the privacy-improvement claim while indicating that the accuracy-preservation claim is at least partially supported.

hypothesis: Re] Badder Seeds: Reproducing the Evaluation of Lexical Methods for Bias Measurement
task_prompt:
The original paper studies how lexical seed sets used for NLP bias measurement are constructed and whether their properties affect the behavior of common bias metrics. It first gathers many seed lexicons from prior literature, treating these lexicons as the core objects of analysis. The paper then examines these seed sets both qualitatively, by considering how they were selected and sourced, and quantitatively, by testing how they behave when used in standard bias-measurement procedures.

To run the quantitative analyses, the paper trains word embeddings on multiple corpora and applies established bias measurement methods based on seed words, including approaches associated with WEAT- and PCA-style bias subspaces. Using these embeddings and seed sets, it evaluates whether the induced bias subspaces capture the intended semantic contrast represented by the paired seed terms. It also compares outcomes across alternative seed sets and measurement setups.

A central part of the methodology is to probe the stability of bias measurements under changes to seed-set design. The paper varies factors such as the ordering and pairing of seed terms, similarities among the selected words, and their frequencies in the training data, and then observes how these choices alter the resulting bias subspaces and bias scores. The overall goal is to assess whether commonly reused seed lexicons provide a robust foundation for measuring bias across corpora.

Based on these analyses, the paper argues for more careful validation and justification of seed lexicons before they are reused in bias studies. Its methodology is therefore framed as an evaluation of lexical bias-measurement pipelines, with emphasis on how seed selection decisions influence quantitative conclusions.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report indicates the paper's main conclusions were largely reproduced, but some methodological details were underspecified and required clarification, making parts of the design contestable from a reproducibility standpoint.

hypothesis: Re] Neural Networks Fail to Learn Periodic Functions and How to Fix It
task_prompt:
The paper studies whether standard neural networks can extrapolate periodic structure beyond the region covered by training data, and proposes a new activation function, Snake, to address this limitation. The claimed methodology combines theoretical motivation with empirical evaluation focused on extrapolation behavior. The activation is defined as snake_a(x) = x + (1/a) sin^2(ax), with experiments considering both fixed and, in some cases, learnable values of the parameter a.

Empirically, the paper evaluates neural networks using conventional activations such as ReLU, tanh, sigmoid, and related variants on simple periodic-function learning tasks. These experiments are designed to compare how different activation functions shape the learned function outside the bounded training region, with the claim that activation properties carry over to network-level extrapolation behavior. In contrast, networks using Snake are evaluated on the same periodic learning settings to test whether they can better recover periodic behavior beyond the observed interval.

Beyond toy extrapolation examples, the paper also claims to test Snake on real-world tasks to assess whether it preserves favorable optimization properties while improving periodic-function modeling. The reported evaluation therefore includes both controlled function-learning experiments and broader application settings, with comparisons against standard activation functions and other contemporary nonlinearities. Overall, the paper's methodology is to benchmark standard and proposed activations across these tasks and judge whether Snake uniquely combines trainability with the ability to learn periodic functions.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The central experimental claims were reportedly replicated, but the paper's methodology was incompletely specified in several implementation details.

hypothesis: Re] On end-to-end 6{DoF} object pose estimation and robustness to object scale
task_prompt:
The paper studies two claimed methodological contributions relevant to keypoint-based pose estimation. First, it describes an end-to-end 6DoF object pose estimation pipeline in which a neural network predicts 2D object keypoints and a differentiable Perspective-n-Point module is inserted into the computation graph. This backpropagatable PnP layer is derived through implicit differentiation so that gradients from pose-related objectives can flow through the geometric solver back to the keypoint prediction network. The claimed benefit is that training can directly optimize the final pose estimation task rather than only the surrogate keypoint localization task, for example by combining reprojection-based losses within a unified end-to-end objective.

Second, the paper describes the HigherHRNet approach for improving keypoint localization under object scale variation, especially for small objects. The method uses high-resolution feature pyramids to maintain detailed spatial representations across scales, applies multi-scale supervision during training, and aggregates heatmaps across resolutions at inference time. The claimed goal is to learn scale-aware representations that improve localization accuracy when the target occupies a small image region.

In the reproduced benchmark setting, these methodologies are applied to drone/object pose estimation using a keypoint-based pipeline. The experimental design therefore consists of evaluating whether integrating a differentiable geometric optimization module improves final pose estimation metrics, and whether adopting the higher-resolution multi-scale heatmap aggregation approach improves keypoint localization performance for small-scale targets.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report confirms the main claims overall, but documents that the BPnP loss-weight settings from the original methodology did not transfer and required retuning for the new dataset/task.

hypothesis: Re] Replicating and Improving GAN2Shape Through Novel Shape Priors and Training Steps
task_prompt:
The original GAN2Shape paper presents an unsupervised framework for recovering 3D information from images by leveraging a pre-trained StyleGAN2 generator. The central idea is that a GAN trained on a category-specific image collection implicitly stores information about object geometry, viewpoint, and illumination in its weights and latent space. Given an input image from the same category as the pre-trained GAN, the method first projects or inverts the image into the StyleGAN2 latent space to obtain a latent code whose generated image matches the input.

From this latent representation, the framework extracts 3D shape by coupling the GAN image prior with feature-based reconstruction. The method uses pre-trained feature extraction networks and optimization-based inference to estimate scene properties such as depth or shape, albedo/texture, lighting, and viewpoint for the dominant object in the image. The recovered 3D representation is intended to arise without requiring annotations, keypoints, multi-view supervision, or explicit 3D ground-truth labels, and is described as applicable across several object categories including faces, cats, cars, and buildings.

Once the 3D shape and appearance factors are inferred, the reconstructed object can be rendered under altered conditions. In particular, the claimed output supports geometric manipulation such as changing viewpoint and photometric manipulation such as re-lighting, allowing the original 2D image to be edited using the recovered 3D scene structure. The method is framed as exploiting intrinsic knowledge already present in a standard 2D GAN, avoiding changes to GAN training and avoiding supervised 3D training pipelines.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report supports that the method can work in some settings, but documents major methodological underspecification and overbroad claims about precision and general applicability.

hypothesis: Re] Exacerbating Algorithmic Bias through Fairness Attacks
task_prompt:
The paper studies fairness-targeted data poisoning attacks in a binary classification setting with two demographic groups, an advantaged group and a disadvantaged group. Starting from a clean training set, the adversary constructs a poisoned set and appends it to the original training data so that a model trained on the combined dataset becomes less fair. The evaluation is framed around common fairness metrics for two-group settings, and the attacks are compared against prior poisoning approaches from the literature.

The first proposed method, the influence attack on fairness, generates adversarial training points by optimizing an adversarial objective that increases when the learned model makes more unfair decisions. The attack uses gradient-based optimization to search for poison points that maximize this fairness-related loss, and includes a regularization parameter to control the trade-off between degrading fairness and degrading predictive performance.

The second proposed method, the anchoring attack, creates poison points near selected target examples from the two demographic groups. Specifically, it uses one target point from the advantaged group and one from the disadvantaged group, and places nearby poisoned samples with opposite class labels while preserving the demographic attribute of the corresponding target. The paper evaluates whether these attacks can worsen fairness, whether the anchoring attack can do so while leaving accuracy largely unchanged, and whether the influence-based attack provides a controllable fairness-performance trade-off.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The main experimental claims were largely reproduced in trend, but the methodology was insufficiently specified in several implementation-critical areas.

hypothesis: Re] G-Mixup: Graph Data Augmentation for Graph Classification
task_prompt:
The original G-Mixup paper proposes a graph data augmentation method for graph classification based on graphons. The method first groups training graphs by class and estimates a class-conditional graphon for each label from the graphs in that class. These graphons are treated as latent structural summaries of graph distributions. To generate augmented training data, the method interpolates between graphons from different classes using a mixup coefficient, producing mixed graphons and corresponding soft labels. New synthetic graphs are then sampled from these mixed graphons and added to the training set.

The paper evaluates this augmentation strategy in supervised graph classification with graph neural networks. The claimed experimental design compares training with G-Mixup augmentation against training without augmentation and against other graph augmentation baselines. Performance is assessed on standard graph classification benchmarks, and the study also includes analyses of graphon estimation quality and of how interpolated graphons relate to mixtures of graph classes. The paper presents G-Mixup as a simple, model-agnostic augmentation procedure intended to improve graph classification accuracy and robustness across datasets.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report presents the overall method as largely reproducible, but it documents contestable baseline and evaluation choices that affect the strength of some comparative claims.

hypothesis: Re] Learning Memory Guided Normality for Anomaly Detection
task_prompt:
The paper proposes an unsupervised video anomaly detection method called Memory-guided Normality for Anomaly Detection (MNAD). The core idea is to train convolutional encoder-decoder models only on normal video data, using either a reconstruction task (reconstructing the current frame) or a prediction task (predicting a future frame). To improve discrimination between normal and anomalous events, the method introduces a memory module intended to store prototypical patterns of normality. During inference, encoded features are matched against this memory, and the retrieved memory items are used to constrain the model representation so that normal patterns are well reconstructed or predicted while anomalous patterns are handled poorly.

The methodology includes two auxiliary objectives for shaping the memory contents and feature representations. A compactness loss encourages deeply learned features from normal inputs to stay close to their associated memory items, while a separateness loss encourages different memory items to remain distinct from one another so that the memory bank captures diverse normal prototypes. The paper argues that these losses increase the discriminative power of both the learned memory items and the latent features.

For anomaly scoring, the paper uses a hybrid abnormality measure rather than relying only on reconstruction quality. The score combines a frame-level reconstruction or prediction quality term based on PSNR with an additional L2 distance term between query features and their corresponding memory items. The method also includes test-time memory updating, where the memory bank is updated during inference to adapt to normal scene patterns observed at test time. The paper evaluates memory and non-memory variants of both reconstruction and prediction models, along with ablations on the proposed losses and memory updating.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The report broadly supports the method's reproducibility but documents contestable claims about which components actually drive the reported gains and whether the memory module consistently delivers the claimed benefit.

hypothesis: Re] Graph Edit Networks
task_prompt:
The paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a graph neural network architecture designed to predict edits that transform an input graph into a target graph. Rather than directly outputting a full graph, the model predicts local edit operations: node insertions, node deletions, and edge insertions or deletions. The architecture computes node representations with message passing over the input graph and then applies an output layer that assigns edit scores to nodes and node pairs. These scores are interpreted as edit actions, enabling the network to model graph-to-graph transformations in a structured and sparse way.

The methodology formulates graph editing as a supervised learning problem. Training examples consist of input graphs together with desired graph transformations, represented through edit scripts or edit indicators derived from source and target graphs. The model is trained to predict which nodes should be deleted, where new nodes should be inserted, and which edges should be added or removed. The paper evaluates the approach on synthetic graph transformation tasks constructed to reflect rule-based edits, and it compares GENs against alternative neural approaches for graph prediction. Reported experiments examine whether the architecture can learn these transformation rules from examples and generalize to unseen graphs.

The paper also studies a variant with restricted edge prediction to improve efficiency on larger graphs. In this formulation, candidate edge edits are limited using node-level signals so that the model does not need to score all possible node pairs. Experiments are presented to assess both predictive performance on the synthetic tasks and the scalability of the method as graph size increases.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The reproduction supports the general implementation and core methodology but documents contestable claims and methodological issues in dataset generation, evaluation, and scalability evidence.

hypothesis: Re] Learning to count everything
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a few-shot object counting framework called FamNet that treats counting as a regression problem conditioned on a small number of exemplars from the target category. Given an input image and a few example bounding boxes of the object of interest taken from the same image, the model learns to estimate a density map whose integral gives the final count. This formulation is intended to generalize to novel object categories without category-specific retraining, enabling counting across many visual domains from only a few exemplars at test time.

FamNet is presented together with a test-time adaptation strategy for novel categories. The method uses exemplar patches to condition the counting model and refines prediction for the current category using limited supervision available at inference time. The paper evaluates the effect of the number of exemplars and claims that increasing exemplar count improves accuracy. The approach is compared against prior few-shot counting methods as well as detector-based baselines to show improved counting performance.

To support broad-category evaluation, the paper introduces the FSC-147 dataset, containing more than 6000 images spanning 147 visual categories with annotations suitable for few-shot counting. Experiments are conducted on FSC-147 and also on CARPK, with additional analysis intended to show that the approach remains effective not only on the newly introduced multi-category dataset but also on category-specific counting benchmarks. The main claimed findings are that FamNet outperforms competing few-shot approaches, performs well on category-specific data, and benefits from more exemplars.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The main empirical claims were largely supported, but the report explicitly contests the paper's description of ground-truth density-map generation and notes missing implementation details for parts of the methodology.

hypothesis: Re] Weakly-Supervised Semantic Segmentation via Transformer Explainability
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a weakly supervised semantic segmentation pipeline built on transformer explainability. It applies Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) together with gradient backpropagation to Vision Transformer-based image classifiers in order to generate class-specific visual explanations that highlight the image regions most responsible for a classification decision. The method is evaluated on image classification and semantic segmentation settings, using ImageNet for classification-related experiments and ImageNet-Segmentation for segmentation-related analysis.

For semantic segmentation, the approach treats transformer explanation maps as visual seeds for foreground object regions. These explanation-derived seeds are then used within a pixel-affinity learning framework inspired by AffinityNet, where a separate network learns semantic relationships between neighboring pixels and propagates the initial seeds into denser pseudo masks. The overall goal is to convert sparse transformer relevance maps into higher-quality segmentation supervision without requiring ground-truth segmentation masks during training.

The claimed methodology also includes experiments with a Hybrid ViT architecture, where image patches are obtained from a CNN feature map before transformer processing. In this setting, transformer-based explainability is used to initialize pseudo masks, and an affinity model refines them through pixel affinity estimation. The paper’s central claim is that transformer relevance propagation provides sufficiently informative localization cues to support both faithful visual explanation and weakly supervised semantic segmentation.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: The core vision experiments were reproduced successfully, but the report explicitly notes missing methodological detail for one evaluation metric computation.

hypothesis: Re] Warm-Starting Neural Network Training
task_prompt:
The paper studies warm-starting for neural network training in settings where training data arrives over time. Warm-starting is defined as first training a model on an initial subset of the data and then continuing training from those learned weights after more data becomes available, instead of restarting from a random initialization. The main question is whether warm-starting changes the final generalization performance relative to training the same model from scratch on the full dataset.

To evaluate this, the methodology compares warm-started training runs against randomly initialized runs across both offline and online data accumulation scenarios. In the offline setup, a model is first trained on half of the training set and then further trained on the full dataset; its test performance is compared with a model trained from random initialization on the full dataset. The experiments are repeated across multiple standard datasets including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN, with several model classes such as ResNet-18, a three-layer perceptron, and logistic regression, and with different optimizers including SGD and Adam.

The paper reports a warm-starting gap, meaning a difference in final test accuracy between warm-started and randomly initialized models despite using the same full training data at the end. To mitigate this effect, it proposes a Shrink-and-Perturb procedure applied to the pretrained weights before continuing training: the existing weights are shrunk and then a random perturbation is added, after which training proceeds on the expanded dataset. The methodology assesses whether this intervention reduces or removes the warm-starting gap under the same experimental settings.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Most reproduced results matched the paper, but some experimental details and settings were altered for practicality and some mismatches remained unexplained or only partially resolved.

hypothesis: Re] Parameterized Explainer for Graph Neural Network
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a parameterized explanation method for graph neural networks that builds on GNNExplainer. Instead of optimizing a separate explanation mask for each individual prediction at test time, the method trains a neural explainer to directly generate explanations from graph representations. The explainer is designed to output masks over graph structure and/or node features that identify the subgraph components most responsible for a model's prediction, while preserving fidelity to the original GNN output.

Methodologically, the approach assumes a pretrained target GNN for node or graph classification and then learns an auxiliary explainer network against that fixed predictor. The explainer is trained to produce explanation masks that maximize agreement with the target model's predictions under masked inputs, following the same general information-preserving motivation as prior explanation methods while amortizing the explanation process across instances. This parameterization is claimed to make explanation generation more efficient and more suitable for inductive use than per-instance optimization.
